Our experiments show that the use of byte-based serialization with FP16 reduces network latency by up to 85 % and improves the end-to-end frame rate by over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times$</tex>, achieving up to 9 FPS. In contrast, traditional TCP-based backbone feature maps tensor transmission yields less than 1 FPS, making it infeasible for real-time applications. These results demonstrate that efficient tensor serialization and gRPC-based communication are key enablers for scalable, low-latency, distributed inference in edge AI systems.

title: Readiness Dimensions for Digital Twin Adoption and Process Optimization in Sri Lanka's Large-Scale Apparel Sector
authors: Dulanga Thennakoon, Chalani Oruthotaarachchi, Amila Withanaarachchi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Industry 4.0 positions Digital Twin (DT) technology as critical for manufacturing efficiency through real-time virtual replicas, enabling process optimization. This study examines how organizational readiness dimensions for Digital Twin adoption predict process optimization outcomes in Sri Lanka's large-scale apparel manufacturing sector, which faces substantial barriers to digital transformation despite contributing significantly to the national GDP. Using data from 179 industry professionals analyzed through PLS-SEM, the research model examined four readiness dimensions: Technological Infrastructure, Organizational Capability, Workforce Competency, and Financial Resources, alongside environmental moderators including Market and Competitive Pressure and Government Support. Results reveal that Financial Resources and Technological Infrastructure significantly predict process optimization, while Market Pressure critically moderates the Organizational Capability relationship, demonstrating that competitive intensity activates organizational capabilities for performance gains. Government support shows limited influence, revealing institutional gaps. The model explains substantial variance in process optimization outcomes. Findings emphasize that financial capacity and technological infrastructure drive DT adoption with competition-dependent organizational effects. Recommendations include prioritizing strategic technology investments, strengthening adaptive organizational structures, and implementing targeted policy interventions to address sector-specific barriers to digital transformation in emerging economies.

title: Bridging Local and Contextual Features with IChOA-CNN-LSTM for Robust Textual Emotion Recognition
authors: Asif Ahammad Miazee, Ashraful Goni
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The explosive growth of digital communication has transformed social media into a vast repository of human emotion, yet the noisy, informal nature of online language continues to challenge existing natural language processing (NLP) systems. This paper introduces IChOA-CNN-LSTM, a optimised hybrid deep learning architecture designed to bridge the persistent gap between local feature extraction and longrange contextual understanding in textual emotion recognition. The framework integrates convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for capturing fine-grained n-gram patterns, bidirectional long short-term memory networks (BiLSTM) for modeling sequential dependencies, and a multi-head attention mechanism optimized through the Improved Chimp Optimization Algorithm (IChOA) for feature selection. The model achieves an accuracy of 78.19%, outperforming conventional CNN, LSTM, and state-of-the-art hybrid baselines by up to 18%. This research lays a scalable foundation for future multimodal and context-aware emotion analysis.

title: Enhanced Near-Field Power Beam Scanning in E-Plane and H-Plane Using mmWave Leaky-Wave Antenna
authors: Shan Han, Yuan Ding, George Goussetis, Symon K. Podilchak, Lei Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Antenna Technology (iWAT)
abstract: This paper presents a two-dimensional (2-D) near-field-focusing (NFF) leaky-wave antenna (LWA) array formed by stacking 16 one-dimensional (1-D) NFF LWAs with 5.45 mm spacing and element-dependent feed phases. The derived aperture phase distribution is obtained from the finite-distance pathlength condition, and applied as element-dependent feed phases. The proposed excitation synthesizes a converging aperture field that produces a compact focal spot with a 6 dB power-density enhancement compared with a conventional far-field focusing (FFF) configuration at the same distance. While the NFF design exhibits a lower far-field gain (18.4 dBi) than the FFF case (23.9 dBi), it still maintains strong directional radiation and stable far-field characteristics. This demonstrates that NFF and highquality far-field performance can be simultaneously achieved. The resulting dual-function aperture is well suited for wireless power transfer and communication applications, offering a compact and efficient solution for simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) systems.

title: Temporal Personal Fashion AI System with Multi-Component Integration
authors: Raveesha Harshani, Navodya Manamendra, Pabasara Rajapaksha, Shyamantha Godaarawa, Buddika Harshanath
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: The rapid growth of e-commerce has reshaped fashion retail but left gaps in personalized sizing, styling, and grooming solutions. This study presents an integrated, AIpowered ecosystem that addresses these challenges through four interoperable, cloud-based modules. First, wardrobe intelligence uses LSTM networks with multi-head attention to track personal style trends over time and recommends sustainable, wardrobe-centric choices, outperforming standard prediction methods. Second, accurate anthropometric measurements are extracted from dual-view images using EfficientNet-B3, enabling privacy-focused and precise body sizing across brands. Third, occasion-based accessory recommendations are generated by combining ResNet-50 for clothing attribute extraction, Transformer-based cultural modeling, and reinforcement learning to adapt to user feedback. Fourth, specialized CNNs offer comprehensive grooming advice—analyzing facial shape, skin, dental, hair, and nail health—validated by expert professionals. The ecosystem leverages large, diverse datasets and federated learning to ensure scalability and user privacy, resulting in notable reductions in return rates, measurable user savings, and high satisfaction across large deployments.

title: An Intelligent Machine and Deep Learning Framework for Fake News Detection
authors: Amit Sheikh, Soyad Adnan Hossain Pranto, Souhardyo Bhattacharjee, Anamika Rani Nath, S M Samim
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of fake news in the digital era has become a critical challenge, undermining public trust and spreading misinformation across online platforms. Accurate and automated detection of fake news has become essential to ensure the reliability of digital information. In this study, we have performed a comparative evaluation of classical machine learning models and a deep learning-based approach for fake news detection. Logistic Regression and Naïve Bayes classifiers have been implemented, achieving accuracies of 98% and 93%, respectively, which has demonstrated the effectiveness of traditional methods in text classification tasks. To better capture semantic relationships and sequential dependencies in textual data, a Long Short-Term Memory network has been developed. The LSTM model has achieved superior performance with 99% accuracy, which has highlighted the advantages of deep learning in modeling complex linguistic patterns. The dataset has consisted of verified fake and real news articles, which have been preprocessed using tokenization, normalization, sequence padding, and feature extraction techniques such as TF-IDF to optimize classification performance. Experimental results have indicated that LSTMbased approaches have not only improved classification accuracy but also offered robustness against noisy and unstructured text, providing a scalable and reliable framework for automated online news verification. This work has contributed to the growing body of research on fake news detection and has served as a benchmark for future studies integrating deep learning and natural language processing techniques.

title: Design and Analysis of a Symmetric Thirteen-Level Inverter for THD Minimization with Reduced Component Count
authors: Bivas Sarkar, Md. Mohi Uddin Mohin, Mohammad Ibrahim, Shahariar Ahmed Himo, Md. Sabbir Alam, Monjila Afrin Dorothi, Ahmed Al Mansur
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Multilevel inverters (MLIs) have emerged as a pivotal technology for high-efficiency power conversion in renewable energy systems, owing to their superior output waveform quality and reduced dv/dt. However, conventional thirteen-level topologies often incur high component counts and control complexity, limiting large-scale integration. This research proposes a novel symmetric thirteen-level inverter that employs only nine power switches and uses the Optimum Nearest Level Control (ONLC) to minimize the total harmonic distortion (THD). Comprehensive MATLAB/Simulink simulations demonstrate a peak output of 600 V and a THD of 6.24% using a resistive load. The inverter's streamlined architecture reduces cost, simplifies control, and enhances reliability. Key advantages include lower conduction and switching losses, simplified gatedriver requirements, and superior harmonic performance. Comparative analysis confirms the proposed topology's economic feasibility and technical superiority over state-of-theart thirteen-level inverters.

title: Performance Degradation of Monocrystalline PV Modules Under Progressive Uniform Shading Conditions: An Experimental Study
authors: Md. Sabbir Alam, Shahariar Ahmed Himo, Md. Ashraful Islam, Md. Mohi Uddin Mohin, Mst. Umme Habiba, ASM Shihavuddin, Ahmed Al Mansur
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Photovoltaic (PV) modules frequently experience partial shadowing in real-world installations, which significantly reduces energy production. The effect of uniform partial shadow, which covers 0%, 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%, 60%, 70%, 80%, and 90% of the module surface area, on the performance of 20W monocrystalline solar modules in outdoor conditions is investigated experimentally in this work. The panels generate 7.82©9.40 W at 10% shading but drop to 3.215.08 W at 90% shading, indicating that higher shading levels disproportionately reduce available power. The results show a significant fall in maximum power with increased shading. From 58.29% at 10% shading to almost 80% at 90%, power loss increases dramatically, with peak sensitivity between 10% and 50% shading. According to this study's results, the shadow has a greater impact on current, reducing it by almost 46% (from 0.369 A to 0.201 A). While the voltage drops by just 11% (from 23.08 V to 20.46 V), this indicates that current loss is the leading cause of the power decline. In addition to providing crucial information for solar system design, performance forecasts, and shade mitigation strategies in both urban and rural environments, this work offers quantitative insights into the consequences of uniform shadowing.

title: LLM is Not All You Need: A Systematic Evaluation of ML vs. Foundation Models for text and image based Medical Classification
authors: Meet Raval, Tejul Pandit, Dhvani Upadhyay
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: The combination of multimodal Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and Large Language Models (LLMs) opens up new possibilities for medical classification. This work offers a rigorous, unified benchmark by using four publicly available datasets covering text and image modalities (binary and multiclass complexity) that contrasts traditional Machine Learning (ML) with contemporary transformer-based techniques. We evaluated three model classes for each task: Classical ML (LR, LightGBM, ResNet-50), Prompt-Based LLMs/VLMs (Gemini 2.5), and Fine-Tuned PEFT Models (LoRA-adapted Gemma3 variants). All experiments used consistent data splits and aligned metrics. According to our results, traditional machine learning (ML) models set a high standard by consistently achieving the best overall performance across most medical categorization tasks. This was especially true for structured text-based datasets, where the classical models performed exceptionally well. In stark contrast, the LoRA-tuned Gemma variants consistently showed the worst performance across all text and image experiments, failing to generalize from the minimal fine-tuning provided. However, the zero-shot LLM/VLM pipelines (Gemini 2.5) had mixed results; they performed poorly on text-based tasks, but demonstrated competitive performance on the multiclass image task, matching the classical ResNet-50 baseline. These results demonstrate that in many medical categorization scenarios, established machine learning models continue to be the most reliable option. The experiment suggests that foundation models are not universally superior and that the effectiveness of Parameter-Efficient FineTuning (PEFT) is highly dependent on the adaptation strategy, as minimal fine-tuning proved detrimental in this study.

title: Khelte Khelte Shikhi: A Proposed HCI Framework for Gamified Interactive Learning with Minecraft in Bangladeshi Education Systems
authors: Mohd Ruhul Ameen, Akif Islam, Momen Khandokar Ope
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Game-based learning shows real promise for engaging students in well-resourced classrooms, but what about the millions who study in schools with far fewer opportunities? We propose a practical framework for bringing Minecraft Education Edition into Bangladesh's 130,000 schools, where 55% lack reliable internet, rural areas receive only 12-16 hours of electricity per day, computer access in rural schools is just 8%, and student-teacher ratios can reach 52:1. Our approach addresses these constraints directly through three deployment tiers: cloudbased multiplayer for urban schools with stable infrastructure (15%), local-area network (LAN) solutions with optional solar power for semi-urban schools <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(30 \%)$</tex>, and fully offline, turnbased modes using refurbished hardware for rural contexts (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 5 \%} \boldsymbol{)}$</tex>. We provide eight curriculum-aligned Minecraft worlds with complete Bangla localization, covering topics from Lalbagh Fort reconstruction to monsoon flood simulation. The interface supports first-time users through progressive complexity, culturally familiar metaphors rooted in local farming and architecture, and accessibility features such as keyboard-only controls and 200% text scaling. We outline evaluation benchmarks including <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5 \%}$</tex> learning gains, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0 \%}$</tex> transfer-task mastery, System Usability Scale scores above 70, and a per-student cost below two dollars per hour. Although not yet empirically validated, this work synthesizes game-based learning theory, HCI principles, and contextual analysis to offer implementable specifications for pilot testing in resource-constrained settings. It is presented as a design-oriented conceptual framework rather than a field deployment, providing an implementation-ready blueprint for future empirical validation.

title: AI-Based Forecasting Model for Sustainable Development in Arab and GCC Countries
authors: Nabil El Kadhi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This paper predicts the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) Index of Arab and GCC nations based on high-quality AI-processed time-series forecasts to predict its value in 2027 using SDG data (2000 -2022) of the SDG Transformation Center. Findings indicate that GCC countries have better performance when compared to non-GCC countries because of better institutional capacities, governance and investments on sustainability. The United Arab Emirates is expected to be top of the list with a score of above 75 in 2026 , and this is due to its efforts on diversification, renewable energy, and digital governance systems. The SDG Index of Saudi Arabia is expected to increase to 72.5 in 2026 with a steady rise in 2018 (63.7) in relation to Vision 2030 and moderately in Oman and Qatar, focusing on green employment and inclusive society. On the other hand, Kuwait and Bahrain are disadvantaged by their delayed reform and by its dependence on oil. Comparative experiments show that cross-validation has a considerable increase in model performance relative to the 80/20 split, with a 35-60 per cent reduction in MAE and RMSE and an average of 0.9 in R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> in a stable economy. As an example, the R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of Saudi Arabia increased by 0.72 to 0.96, and RMSE decreased by 3.34 to 1.13 in Oman. These findings demonstrate the strength of cross-validation in SDG forecasting and the urgency of the implementation of transformational and innovation-led policies to maintain long-term developments towards the 2030 Agenda in the Arab and GCC worlds.

title: CV-QKD Security Improvement Through SNMP-Based Quantum-Network Management
authors: Alberto Sebastia'n-Lombraña, Laura Ortiz, Sebastian Etcheverry, Arturo Villegas, Josefina Cresta, Marco Cofano, Vicente Mart¹n
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum key distribution (QKD) offers information-theoretic security based on quantum mechanics, but practical implementations must address real-world threats to achieve certification. Standards such as ETSI GS QKD 016 define security objectives and requirements to mitigate risks including physical tampering, side-channel exploitation, and protocol-level attacks. This work explores how SNMP-based quantum-network management, following FCAPS principles, can enhance the practical security of continuous-variable QKD (CV-QKD) systems integrated into classical networks.

title: 1 Transistor-Dynamic Random Access Memory as Reservoir for Temporal Signal Processing
authors: MD Yasir Bashir, C Lalith Adithya Sai, Shubham Sahay
publish: IEEE Journal on Exploratory Solid-State Computational Devices and Circuits
abstract: Reservoir computing (RC), a computational paradigm inspired by the recurrent neural networks, offers a promising framework for efficient temporal processing with minimal training overhead. Hardware implementation of RC primitive requires devices that exhibit short-term memory, nonlinearity, and energy-efficient state-switching dynamics. While emerging non-volatile memory (eNVM) technologies have been explored as physical reservoirs, their limited endurance and wafer scale fabrication challenges hinder widespread application for real-time edge-compatible RC systems. In contrast, 1-transistor dynamic random-access memories (1T-DRAMs) have recently gained significant attention owing to their high scalability, low footprint, and high endurance. In this work, we propose the use of 1T-DRAMs as physical reservoirs for computing and temporal signal processing. We show that the inherent charge storage and its temporal decay in the body of 1T-DRAMs owing to the dynamic recombination efficiently emulates the fading (short-term) memory behavior, making it well-suited for reservoir computing. Furthermore, networks of the 1T-DRAM physical reservoirs achieve classification accuracy of 82.46% and 85.02% on reduced MNIST and Weizmann human action dataset, respectively.

title: Generation of brain PET synaptic density imaging from MRI and FDG-PET using a 3D Multi-stage Residual U-Net
authors: Xinyuan Zheng, Patrick Worhunsky, Takuya Toyonaga, Qiong Liu, Xueqi Guo, Yinchi Zhou, Xiongchao Chen, Bo Zhou, Adam P. Mecca, Ming-Kai Chen, Ryan S. O’Dell, Christopher H. Van Dyck, Richard E. Carson, Rajiv Radhakrishnan, Chi Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: Synaptic vesicle glycoprotein 2A (SV2A) in human brains is an important biomarker of synaptic loss associated with several disorders, including Alzheimer's disease (AD). However, SV2A tracers, such as [<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>C]UCB-J, are less available in clinical practice due to constraints that include cost and the need for an onsite cyclotron. As a result, there is interest in generating synthetic SV2A PET images using a more commonly used tracer, or another widely available image modality. We therefore investigated the feasibility of using a deep learning approach to generate synthetic [<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>C]UCB-J SV2A PET images from T1-weighted (T1w) Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and/or [<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F]FDG PET imaging. In our study, we used paired data from 54 participants for training and evaluation, including 22 healthy controls (HC) and 32 AD patients. We implemented two architectures, 3D UNet and 3D Multi-stage ResUNet (MS ResUNet) for translating either MRI or [<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F]FDG PET or both into [<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>C]UCB-J PET images. Our experiments show that, through progressive learning 3D MS ResUNet achieved less bias compared to a single-stage design. Adopting residual connection, cross-stage feature fusion and supervised attention strategies to our model contributes to better gradient flow, mitigates information loss and enables selective focus, leading to better generation results. Additionally, combining MRI T1w and [<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F]FDG PET images provides the best prediction performance, whereas having [<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F]FDG PET alone produces better synthetic images than using MRI alone. We also evaluated our model’s performance for 14 typical brain regions. The MS ResUNet achieved less than 5% bias in all regions. Furthermore, the differences between AD and HC in certain regions (e.g., hippocampus and entorhinal) are still significant in generated images (p=0.000). In conclusion, the 3D MS ResUNet was able to generate desirable SV2A PET images that are visually consistent with true images in terms of contrast and tracer distribution, quantitatively with low bias (-0.26% ± 5.56%) and high similarity (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>=0.975), for both AD and HC. Compared with the 3D vanilla UNet (FDG: −2.39% ± 5.55%, MRI: −5.03% ± 13.24%, or both: −1.89% ± 6.04%), the modified design produced more robust SV2A images. The synthetic images can potentially provide additional pathological insights into AD and other neurological disorders without increasing radiation exposure and cost.

title: Joint Source and Sensor Localization by Hybrid TDOA–AOA: Analysis and Solution
authors: Yongde Ye, Gang Wang, K. C. Ho
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: This paper investigates the joint source and sensor localization (JSSL) problem using hybrid time-difference-of-arrival (TDOA) and angle-of-arrival (AOA) measurements in a multi-source-multi-sensor scenario. Without the knowledge of sensor positions, this problem jointly estimates the relative positions of the sources and sensors together. We first conduct the localizability analysis by examining the rank of the Jacobian matrix, from which we obtain the minimum number of sources and sensors required for TDOA-AOA JSSL and the special configurations where the JSSL problem is not solvable. Next, we construct a novel parametric model by integrating the TDOA and AOA measurements, and use it to formulate a non-convex constrained weighted least squares (CWLS) problem. JSSL is then accomplished by solving the CWLS problem through semidefinite relaxation and semidefinite programming. Finally, theoretical analyses are conducted. It shows that the proposed CWLS solution is able to achieve the Cramér-Rao lower bound performance under small Gaussian noise. Moreover, it reveals that increasing the number of sources or sensors can improve the overall JSSL performance. Simulation results validate the theory and also demonstrate the capability of the proposed method for TDOA-AOA JSSL.

title: Three-Point Calibration Method for Conductive Liquid Characterization Using High-Dynamic-Range Microwave Sensor
authors: Mehri Ziaee Bideskan, Nima Karbaschi, Zahra Abbasi
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Accurately quantifying ionic compound levels in aqueous media remains challenging due to the strong dielectric losses introduced by dissolved ions. This work presents a resonator-based microwave sensing system designed for non-invasive characterization of such lossy media, demonstrated using ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) as a representative analyte. The system employs an electric-LC resonator tag coupled to two different reader configurations, a gap-coupled transmission line (GCTL) and a standard transmission line (TL). Changes in ionic concentration modify the effective permittivity of the surrounding medium, which in turn perturbs the resonator’s electromagnetic loading. These perturbations manifest as measurable variations in the resonant response, allowing the reader to sense the medium entirely through wireless coupling without requiring physical contact. Based on wideband dielectric assessments of the material samples, 1000 MHz was selected as the optimal operating frequency balancing sensitivity and signal attenuation. Experimental and simulation studies show that the resonance amplitude varies monotonically with ionic content, exhibiting exponential behavior for the GCTL and a logarithmic dependence for the TL reader with R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.99. Leveraging these predictable behaviors, we introduce a three-point calibration methodology that parameterizes the full response curve from only three measurements. This approach drastically reduces computational overhead while maintaining high accuracy (error <2.5%) across the whole concentration range from 0 to 6000 ppm. The primary contribution of this work is the generalizable three-point calibration strategy that drastically reduces the number of required measurements while remaining valid for a wide range of ionic solutions, enabling practical deployment of resonator-based sensing in precision agriculture and environmental monitoring.

title: A modular geospatial data quality framework: FAIRness, comparability, and reusability
authors: B. Riedler, E.S. Klaußner, S. Lang, K. Zakir
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The richness of global, geospatial data offers unprecedented opportunities. At the same time, the sheer volume and diversity of data in combination with a variety of data quality (DQ) assessment strategies poses challenges for data integration and cross-disciplinary utilization. We introduce a structured (modular), harmonized (comparable) and transferable (reusable) framework for evaluating geospatial data. Common DQ dimensions are integrated with new aspects of upstream validity (Metadata, Input data, Reproducibility), recognizing the importance of data provenance. Together with aspects of downstream usability (Applicability, Data constraints) this supports the integration into operational workflows. The spatial variability of dimensions is considered as key aspect influencing the utilization across space (Transferability). The flexibility of the protocol integrates the two interlinked concepts of general DQ and data adequacy and allows the assessment of the degree of FAIRness through the harmonization with FAIR metrics. Machine-evaluable measures are balanced with expert judgement, essential for contextual insights and critical reflection. Reliability-focused and suitability-oriented assessment needs are reflected in a modular, hierarchical organisation of DQ dimensions in the four domains: Descriptives, Design, Conformance and Context. This enables time-efficient assessments through (1) comparability across data of different processing levels, origin, type and context and (2) reusability of reliability-related results.

title: Acoustic Sensing and GAN-Based Augmentation for Non-Autonomous Driving Environment Recognition Towards Visually Impaired Inclusion
authors: Cihun-Siyong Gong, Yu-Hua Chen
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This study proposes an acoustic sensing–based framework for driving environment recognition in electric vehicles (EVs), designed for intelligent transportation systems (ITS) in non-autonomous driving scenarios and edge-enabled Internet of Things (IoT) environments. The proposed approach analyzes short-duration acoustic signals and employs multiple feature representations, including MFCC, LFCC, and wavelet-based features. To address data scarcity and class imbalance, a feature-level generative adversarial network (GAN) is introduced for data augmentation. Experimental results demonstrate that MFCC-based features consistently achieve stable and superior recognition performance across different configurations. Moreover, GAN-based augmentation significantly improves recognition stability and accuracy for minority classes, such as tunnel and rainy conditions. In addition, under a unified experimental framework, this study systematically investigates the interactions among acoustic features, data augmentation strategies, and model architectures. The findings indicate that passive acoustic sensing offers advantages in terms of low cost and real-time computational capability, making it well suited for deployment in edge IoT systems. The proposed approach is applicable to intelligent transportation scenarios, including driver assistance and environmental perception for visually impaired passengers. Overall, the proposed approach offers advantages in terms of low cost, minimal privacy concerns, and real-time computational capability. It is well suited for deployment in edge computing environments and ITS applications, providing a practical solution for acoustic-based environmental perception in electric vehicles.

title: Medical Aerosol Flow Rate Estimation by Vibrational Assessment for Smart Inhaler Development
authors: Ivan Mysovskikh, Mathew Legg, Serge Demidenko, Moi Tin Chew
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The study focuses on flow-rate detection and evaluation using acoustic vibration assessment for the development of a smart inhaler, aiming to effectively manage respiratory diseases. It highlights the importance of adhering to proper inhalation techniques and maintaining consistency in managing respiratory conditions. Methods and relevant commercial and prototype research-type devices for sensing and measuring inhalation flow rate in smart inhalers are evaluated and compared. The findings show that utilising acoustic analysis is a promising approach for detecting and assessing inhalation, ultimately improving treatment outcomes and quality of life for patients with respiratory diseases.

title: Quantum Technology Readiness Adoption Model: A TOE-Aligned Framework for Addressing Quantum Technology Risk Exposure
authors: Shadya Maldonado Rosado, Trevor Bowman, Andrew Smallwood, Anna Shebanow
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum computing, quantum communication and networking, quantum sensing, and post-quantum cryptography (PQC) each require distinct organizational readiness assessment. Existing maturity models address these domains in isolation, conflate PQC with quantum technologies, omit quantum networking, exhibit regional standards bias, or mandate specific vendor relationships. The Quantum Technology Readiness Adoption Model (QTRAM), grounded in the Technology-Organization-Environment (TOE) framework, structures assessment through five independent Technology sub-dimensions within shared Organization and Environment contexts, using six maturity levels (05) aligned with international standards from IEEE, NIST, ETSI, ITU, TNO, and regional bodies. QTRAM remains theoretical and requires empirical validation.

title: Power Cycling Lifetime Modeling and Validation for Flip-Chip PQFN GaN Devices
authors: Siddhesh Gajare, Duanhui Li, Angel Espinoza, Ricardo Garcia, Peter Tieu, Shengke Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: The power cycling reliability of flip-chip power quad flat no leads GaN transistors was investigated over a wide range of power-on durations. A lifetime model was developed to characterize the Weibull power cycling lifetimes as a function of power-on durations, by incorporating the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t_{0 n}$</tex> term into a modified Norris-Landzberg equation, yielding a frequency exponent <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\gamma)$</tex> of −2.09. Failure analysis revealed that gate solder joint cracking was the dominant wear-out failure mechanism. Finite element analysis simulations using COMSOL validated the failure analysis results by predicting the shortest lifetime at the same gate bump location. After implementing the Anand viscoplasticity and Darveaux fatigue models, further simulations showed good agreement with experimental results, validating the applicability of the developed power cycling lifetime model.

title: A Machine Learning–Driven Framework for Enhancing Cognitive Function Using tDCS and Brain Gym Interventions
authors: Wasee Ahsan, Md Labib, Fahima Haque, Md Minhajul Islam, Md Iqbal Hossain, Mohammad Sakib Mahmood, Rifatuzzaman Khan Afridi, Md Tareq Hasan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Structured cognitive training produced the strongest effects within the short-term, doing better than the other two interventions, Brain Gym + tDCS and Brain Gym-only methods. Cognitive training intervention participants exhibited significant within-group improvement between pre and post testing in all of the targeted cognitive domains (global cognition, processing speed, and executive function), with results large enough to be considered practically significant (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p<.05$</tex>) and the group differences were large enough to be considered statistically significant (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex> <. 001) ANOVA. The main strengths of this research are the integration of an interpretable machine learning pipeline along with a non-pharmacological cognitive enhancement protocol that used a multimodal approach. To synthesise change scores across the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA), Trail Making Test A (TMT-A), and Trail Making Test B (TMT-B), a new composite scale was created the Cognitive Improvement Index. Thirty patients were assigned randomly to three time pre-intervention) arms: structured cognitive training, Brain Gym + tDCS and Brain Gym only. To overcome the burden of a small sample size, oversampling was done on the training data to make the development of the model more robust. Gradient Boosting with 5fold cross-validation achieved the lowest prediction error (RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.5542$</tex>). These results indicate that the derived features and the composite outcome metric capture a meaningful shift in cognitive performance. These results highlight the potential of cognitive training as a core component of transient boosts of cognitive performance and confirm the value of transparent, machine learning-supported assessment procedures in complex neurocognitive interventions. However, sample size and synthetic augmentation issues point to the necessity of future research conducted with larger randomised groups and in modified approaches in response to the intervention.

title: Study on Fault Location Method of Power Cable Using Wavelet Transform Modulus Maxima
authors: Xingchao Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: In the process of locating a power cable fault, finding the singular point of fault traveling wave is a challenge and the uncertainty of traveling wave velocity affects the accuracy of fault location. In this paper, a cable fault location algorithm to remove the impact of wave velocity was derived based on the wavelet transform modulus maximum theory and the reflection law of cable fault traveling wave. The simulation model of the cable fault location system was built by Matlab/Simulink software, the db5 wavelet 3-layer decomposition of fault waveform was realized, and the modulus maxima sequence of high-frequency coefficients was extracted for analysis and calculation. The simulation ranging results indicate that the relative errors of different fault distances under different fault types are less than 1.83 %, which indicates that the power cable fault location approach based on wavelet transform modulus maxima theory can easily detect the singular points of fault signals, remove the interference caused by traveling wave velocity effectively, and improve the accuracy of cable fault location.

title: Applying Mission Engineering to Counter-Unmanned Aircraft Systems Missions
authors: Shannon Dubicki, Risa Gorospe
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) are changing modern warfare due to their rapidly evolving tactics. Small UAS are inexpensive and increasingly capable, which when paired with Counter-UAS (C-UAS) statutory authorities, create a complex mission space in United States homeland defense and theater defense. Both technical and non-technical solutions are required to defend against UAS. Due to the complexity of the C-UAS mission space, mission engineering is needed to adequately explore the problem space and to recommend solution sets to stakeholders that consider their individual constraints and needs. This paper captures observations from applying mission engineering to $\mathbf{C}$ UAS missions. The engineers followed the United States Department of War’s Mission Engineering Guide (MEG) Version 2.0 and noted any deviations. The deviations span various aspects of mission engineering such as mission stakeholder adjudication, characterization of the adversary, and modularity of the solution architectures. From this C-UAS mission engineering effort and the noted deviations from the MEG, seven recommendations to update the MEG are provided.

title: National Quantum Strategies: a Data-Driven Approach to Understanding the Quantum Ecosystem
authors: Simon Richard Goorney, Emre Aslan, Aleksandrs Baskakovs, Borja Munoz, Jacob Sherson
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: As quantum technologies (QT) move from foundational research toward industrial and societal deployment, national strategies have become critical instruments for shaping the future of this emerging field. In this study, we conduct the first large-scale, data-driven analysis of 62 national quantum strategic documents (QSDs) from 20 countries. Using AI-based natural language processing (topic modeling), we extract 12,786 paragraphs and identify 12 topics present in the text, ranging from technical development areas to transversal aspects such as workforce development and governance. Temporal analysis reveals a distinct shift in policy discourse toward applications of QT and commercialisation, and relatively away from basic science. Our findings highlight the increasing diversification of the QT field, and contribute to the growing area of quantum policy studies. We advocate for more AI and data-driven analyses of the quantum ecosystem, to work toward a scalable framework for understanding the technological and societal challenges of the second quantum revolution.

title: File-Size-Aware Heterogeneous Coded TeraSort
authors: Haoyun Deng, Yong Deng
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: This paper considers the design of coded Terasort with heterogeneities in both input data popularities and sizes, which are commonly seen in practice. While no existing work has studied coded TeraSort with heterogeneous file sizes, we propose a file-size-aware low-complexity key-value (KV) pair placement strategy to maximize the efficiency of coded multicasting. We develop a heterogeneous coded TeraSort (HCT) algorithm that efficiently handles files with heterogeneous sizes, while considering the KVs to be sorted are of heterogeneous popularity. Experimental results show that the proposed HCT outperforms existing uncoded and coded TeraSort algorithms.

title: High Granularity, Moving Target Address Space Layout Randomization Through Dynamic Fragmentation
authors: Patrick W. Hong
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Code-reuse attacks are a common cyberattack technique that reuses existing code segments in the code base. Attackers create functional “gadgets” out of the existing code base and execute chains of gadgets to achieve their malicious goals.This paper identifies a system and method for address space layout randomization (ASLR) called Dynamic Fragment ASLR (DFASLR), US Patent 11,809,871. DFASLR separates position independent instructions from the rest of the code base and subdivides the relocatable instructions into groupings called fragments. Software hardening is applied to the fragments which are then randomly placed and reconnected at load time. During runtime, continuous re-randomization of fragment location occurs. DFASLR offers randomization with configurable granularity up to disjointing and randomizing individual instruction locations. DFASLR also allows for true runtime ASLR. To date, no practical and generally applicable runtime ASLR has been demonstrated. Along with load and runtime ASLR, DFASLR offers side-channel blinding and is resistant to the state of the art (SoTA) ASLR attacks such as side-channel analysis and memory disclosure attacks. The DFASLR process also provides covert technique insertion, gadget smashing capabilities, Return-Oriented Programming (ROP)/JumpOriented Programming (JOP) traps, and other benefits due to the nature of the technique.

title: Cross Talk in Multiplexed Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution
authors: Olena Kovalenko, Vladyslav Usenko
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We address the role of linear inter-mode cross talk in continuous-variable quantum key distribution in the most typical prepare-and-measure scenario. Considering coherent and well as squeezed signal states and either homodyne or heterodyne detection in the receiver station, we show how the cross talk deteriorates the secret key rate and robustness of the protocols to the channel noise. We show how optimization of the signal modulation can improve the performance of the protocols in the presence of cross talk and point out possibilities for further cross talk compensation.

title: Modeling and Simulation Based Development of a Teleoperated Slit Lamp
authors: Sasisekhar Govind, Gabriel Wainer, Lindsey Marvel, Kasturirangan Iyengar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: A significant proportion of blindness and visual impairment is preventable through early diagnosis, yet access to critical diagnostic tools remain limited in under served areas. Traditional medical equipment, which is often expensive and requires operation by trained ophthalmologists, poses a barrier to effective care in remote areas. To help serve these regions better, several not-for-profit organizations typically establish eye clinics, but the need for medical professionals to visit these areas for diagnoses beyond preliminary assessments remains a significant challenge. The widespread adoption of the internet offers an opportunity to bridge this gap through telemedical devices (internet connected medical equipment) that can be operated remotely by doctors, enabling them to serve multiple, distant areas and marginalized communities. However the hurdles of development of telemedical devices still persist. Hence, this work proposes the development of a teleoperated medical device (specifically a device called the Slit Lamp), harnessing modeling and simulation based engineering to aid with the development of a robust telemedical device. We explore the use of the Discrete Event Systems Specification (DEVS), to create a discrete event model of the entire telemedical system (predominantly the slit lamp). DEVS Simulation tools, such as Cadmium, allow users to test and refine the model before implementation, ensuring the design meets performance standards. Once the system is simulated and optimized, it can be implemented as-is in the real world (which is an advantage of using DEVS), significantly reducing development time and improving reliability. In the future, this also opens the door to the implementation of a digital twin of the slit lamp, which would provide real-time feedback to medical practitioners by reflecting their inputs as movements in the system. More importantly, digital twins would allow for running simulations in-situ, helping prevent potential accidents.

title: A Multi-Star Astronomical Positioning Method Based on BFGS with Backtracking Line Search
authors: Yulin Wu, Tao He, Xianyi Li, Chao Zhuo, Lei Ren, Xiaoxuan Lin
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Astronomical positioning is a critical technology for autonomous navigation. To address the inherent limitations of geometric ambiguity and initialization sensitivity in traditional multi-star positioning, we propose a robust multi-star positioning framework based on a star vector model. We formulate the positioning problem as a constrained least-squares optimization problem within the Cartesian coordinate system and investigate two numerical solving methods, specifically Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) Quasi-Newton method equipped with backtracking line search. While PSO offers global search capabilities without the need for initial position information, the proposed BFGS algorithm effectively solves the non-linear multi-star equations by approximating the inverse Hessian matrix to utilize second-order curvature information and incorporating backtracking line search to ensure global convergence. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared to the PSO method, the BFGS approach achieves significantly higher positioning accuracy and computational efficiency, providing a robust solution for high-precision autonomous astronomical navigation.

title: Integrated Design and Experimental Study of GIS Current Sensing Based on Fiber-Optic Integrated Basin Type Insulator
authors: Kun Zhang, Shuai Yang, Zhongyu Wang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To meet the integration requirements of primary and secondary systems in smart grids, a novel integrated solution of fiber optic current transformer (FOCT) and gas-insulated switchgear (GIS) based on the basin type insulator is proposed. By integrating the optical sensing unit inside the GIS basin type insulator, high-precision current signal acquisition and coordinated monitoring of equipment status are achieved. The structural design of the integrated basin type insulator is completed, and the distribution of electric and magnetic fields is simulated and verified using finite element analysis software. The simulation results show that the electric field distortion rate of the basin type insulator under the integrated solution is small, and the magnetic field uniformity meets the sensing requirements. This paper further discusses the long-term reliability of the fiber-resin bonding structure under complex working conditions, the impact of casting process defects on insulation performance, and the costbenefit and manufacturability of the proposed scheme compared with traditional solutions, which further improves the theoretical and engineering application value of the scheme. This research provides a feasible technical path for the primary and secondary integration upgrade of GIS equipment and has significant engineering application value.

title: PFANet: A Progressive Feature Alignment Network with Local-Global Attention for Joint Classification of Hyperspectral and LiDAR Data
authors: Man Li, Yiyang Tan, Kai Xu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral image (HSI) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data contain complementary information for land cover classification. However, the substantial differences in imaging mechanisms often cause cross-modal feature discrepancies, leading to feature misalignment that hinders the formation of consistent joint representations and ultimately weakens fusion performance. Moreover, insufficient consideration of multiscale features results in incomplete integration, preventing the complementary information of the two modalities from being fully exploited. In addition, the severe class imbalance commonly observed in HSI-LiDAR datasets impairs the recognition of minority categories, further reducing overall classification performance. To address these issues, we propose a progressive feature alignment network (PFANet). First, a progressive alignment module is developed to mitigate feature misalignment through semantic distribution calibration and spatial fine registration. Then, a window interval cross-attention module integrates local window attention and interval-based sparse global attention. It jointly captures multiscale spectral-spatial details and elevation information, producing more discriminative fusion features. Finally, a dynamic class-balanced loss is introduced to adaptively enhance minority-class learning and improve robustness under class-imbalanced conditions. Experimental results on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that PFANet outperforms other advanced methods. It achieves overall accuracies of 92.84% on Houston2013, 99.58% on Trento, and 97.50% on Augsburg, and exhibits clear advantages in minority-class performance, thereby confirming its stable performance on three HSI-LiDAR benchmarks and stronger performance on few-shot classes.

title: Learning High-Order Relationships with Hypergraph Attention-based Spatio-Temporal Aggregation for Brain Disease Analysis_supp1-3690795.pdf
authors: Aijing Lin
publish: N/A
abstract: Functional connectivity derived from functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) primarily captures pairwise interactions between brain regions, which limits its ability to characterize complex high-order relationships. Hypergraph-based methods provide a natural way to model such interactions, yet most existing approaches rely on predefined hypergraph structures and neglect temporal dynamics, resulting in limited expressiveness and interpretability. To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework that jointly learns informative and sparse high-order brain structures along with their temporal dynamics. Inspired by the information bottleneck principle, we introduce an objective that maximizes information and minimizes redundancy, aiming to retain disease-relevant high-order features while suppressing irrelevant information. The proposed framework consists of three key components: (1) a multi-hyperedge binary mask module for hypergraph structure learning, (2) a hypergraph self-attention aggregation module that captures spatial features through adaptive attention across nodes and hyperedges, and (3) a spatio-temporal low-dimensional network for extracting discriminative spatio-temporal representations for disease classification. Experiments on benchmark fMRI datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches and effectively captures meaningful high-order brain interactions. These findings provide new insights into brain network modeling, showing potential for analyzing neuropsychiatric disorders.

authors: David G. Michelson
publish: IEEE Intelligent Transportation Systems Magazine
abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: Extending Repeater Spacing in All-Photonic Quantum Repeaters
authors: Ryosuke Shiina, Kenneth Goodenough, Filip Rozpędek
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum communication between two distant parties will serve as a cornerstone of the future quantum internet. However, many existing quantum communication schemes face a critical bottleneck: the need to generate a sufficient number of entangled Bell pairs over long distances. Although photons are ideal carriers of quantum information, overcoming photon loss and the exponential attenuation of signals remains a major challenge. We propose an all-photonic quantum repeater architecture that enables quantum communication over a distance of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1,000 \text{km}$</tex> with equidistant repeater spacing extended to 9 km -approximately 2 to 6 times longer than in previously proposed all-photonic schemes. This extended repeater spacing arises from the combined use of elementary entangled Bell pairs protected by both continuous-variable and discrete-variable quantum error correction codes-namely, the bosonic Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) code and the [[7], [1], [3]] Steane code-whose combination yields a synergistic effect that surpasses a simple additive improvement, enhancing robustness against photon loss. This architecture incorporates a new ranking criterion and a multi-reflection mirror-based optical cavity as a free-space photonic memory module, which we model in terms of its length and mirror-reflection efficiency. To more accurately capture realistic physical conditions during photonic resource preparation, we model the presence of switching-induced imperfections by introducing an optical switch into the simulation, in addition to commonly considered imperfections such as finite GKP squeezing, finite detection efficiency, and finite fiber coupling efficiency. In the presence of these imperfections, our realization requires fewer than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3, 8 0 0}$</tex> GKP qubits per repeater station per protocol run-a value significantly smaller than in prior all-photonic proposals.

title: Resolving Bottlenecks in Quantum Machine Learning: Encoding Theory and Data Augmentation
authors: Mariia Baidachna, Isabel Pedraza, Sergei Gleyzer
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum machine learning (QML) models operating on classical data require an encoding step to map information into the Hilbert space. However, this imposes a computational bottleneck absent in classical frameworks, necessitating rigorous theoretical complexity analysis and algorithmic mitigations. We provide theoretical foundations of different encoding methods that prepare quantum states for both simulators and hardware, analyzing normal and sparse high energy physics data. In addition, we propose a quantum data augmentation method based on randomly sampled circuits to improve sample efficiency and mitigate the data scarcity bottleneck. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this method on a generative quantum diffusionlike model to produce synthetic samples of classical images. Our results demonstrate that choosing domain optimal encoding and applying the novel quantum data augmentation leads to faster convergence and improved model performance, offering a highly sample-efficient and scalable approach to encoding and augmenting sparse classical data for QML.

title: Reliability and Anti-Interference Analysis of Emergency Diesel Generator Sets for Geological Drilling Rigs
authors: Chao Li, Tong Lu, Wenwu Liu, Kun Guo, Yuwen Huang, Qing Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: The continuous and stable operation of geological drilling rigs is crucial for the safety and efficiency of geological drilling projects, necessitating an emergency power supply to ensure stable operation and maintain power quality. As the primary emergency power source, the operational reliability and stability of emergency diesel generator sets are self-evident for geological drilling rigs. This paper analyzes the abnormal operation of the emergency diesel generator set and its control system for electric drilling rigs in the Huainan Coal Mine Flood Control Project. Through a detailed study of the harmonic distortion propagation mechanism, it elucidates the mutual influence between the nonlinear load in the electric drilling rig system and the emergency diesel generator set. Based on these findings, we propose systematic improvement measures for the configuration of emergency diesel generator sets for electric drilling rigs. These optimization strategies aim to enhance the operational stability of the emergency power supply, thereby ensuring continuous and safe construction of geological drilling projects.

title: Machine Learning-Based Typhoid Prediction Using Clinical Data
authors: Amit Kumar Ghosh, Shahriar Marjan, Rejowan Arifin Nayeem, S.M. Abdullah Al Muhib, Md. Hasan Imam Bijoy, M. Humayet Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: The paper investigates the issue of early diagnosis of typhoid fever with the help of a machine learning procedure based on the clinical and demographic data. Such preprocessing of the data was based on which a number of models were trained and assessed. Gradient Boosting turned out to be the most efficient model due to the given 100 per cent accuracy and the totally accurate multiple classes. The results indicate that machine learning and, in particular, the ensemble methods could act as an effective and efficient diagnostic instrument in a low-resource environment. Such method can be an alternative and cheaper to use as the traditional diagnostics. They still need to be externally validated using other datasets, though, to be sure of generalizability.

title: PhoenixNet: A Deep Learning-Based Low-Light Image Enhancement Method for Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Afsana Nadia, Md Nuruzzaman, Khan Md Shibli Nomani, Md Masum Billal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: In low-light environments, autonomous vehicles face difficulty to detect the object. In order to overcome these shortcomings, PhoenixNet, a deep learning architecture, has been proposed for enhancing low-light images in autonomous driving scenarios. The objective of this paper is to enhance low-light images in a way that maintains both structural details and perceptual quality and also improves object detection performance with higher confidence scores. We evaluate PhoenixNet against four state-of-the-art methods-Zero-DCE, RQ-LLIE, ExCNet, and PairLIE using some computational metrics, PSNR, SSIM, MS-SSIM, LPIPS, MSE, MAE, and loss. Our experimental results show that PhoenixNet consistently achieves superior performance with its lowest error rates and the highest perceptual quality scores. The model has the highest validation PSNR (36.40), the highest SSIM (0.9764), low perceptual loss LPIPS (0.0656), and low loss values (0.0115). These outcomes ensure the enhanced object detection in low-light conditions. The PhoenixNet model could be a possible solution for enhancing low-light images in real-world autonomous driving applications.

title: Pragmatic Lossless Compression: Fundamental Limits and Universality
authors: Andreas Theocharous, Lampros Gavalakis, Ioannis Kontoyiannis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: The problem of variable-rate lossless data compression is considered, for codes with and without prefix constraints. Sharp bounds are derived for the best achievable compression rate of memoryless sources, when the excess-rate probability is required to be exponentially small in the blocklength. Accurate nonasymptotic expansions with explicit constants are obtained for the optimal rate, using tools from large deviations and Gaussian approximation. When the source distribution is unknown, a universal achievability result is obtained with an explicit “price for universality” term. This is based on a fine combinatorial estimate on the number of sequences with small empirical entropy, which might be of independent interest. Examples are shown indicating that, in the small excess-rate-probability regime, the approximation to the fundamental limit of the compression rate suggested by these bounds is significantly more accurate than the approximations provided by either normal approximation or error exponents. The new bounds reinforce the crucial operational conclusion that, in applications where the blocklength is relatively short and where stringent guarantees are required on the excess-rate probability, the best achievable rate is no longer close to the entropy. Rather, it is an appropriate, more pragmatic rate, determined via the inverse error exponent function and the blocklength.

title: Privacy-Preserving Bipartite Consensus in Aperiodic Sampled-Data Multiagent Systems: A Discrete Differential Privacy Scheme
authors: Lingyu Wang, Te Yang, Jianwei Xia, Zhichuang Wang, Guoliang Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article investigates the bipartite consensus control problem for aperiodic sampled-data multiagent systems (MASs) with ε-differential privacy. First, a discrete-time time-varying state space model is constructed for heterogeneous MASs, in which the sampling interval is time-varying within a certain known range, which can better describe the segmented continuum features caused by aperiodic sampled-data. Second, a differential privacy average output bipartite consensus algorithm for sampled MASs is given. This dynamic distributed algorithm safeguards the confidentiality of its initial configuration while minimizing the need for constant controller adjustments and real-time data exchanges. A time-varying control gain is given to ensure that the convergence point’s expectation precisely matches the average of the initial states, with the convergence accuracy bounded by an explicit expression. Furthermore, a carefully designed noise mechanism is introduced in the privacy analysis, enabling agents to flexibly adjust their privacy levels. To validate the proposed approach, a multiagent collaborative control experimental platform is applied, incorporating both uncrewed ground vehicles (UGVs) and quadcopter uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs). The developed methodology effectively computes the required control gain, with experimental results conclusively demonstrating the successful implementation of our algorithm.

title: Extended Gramian Angular Field: An Efficient Method for Encoding Time Series as Images and Its Application in Power Converter Open Circuit Fault Diagnosis
authors: Yang Zhou, Jin Zhao, Yang Liu, Kang Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The data-driven-based fault diagnostic methods have continued to emerge in power electronics systems in recent years. Among them, though the methods that convert one-dimensional (1-D) electrical signals into two-dimensional (2-D) images for classification have shown promise, they often suffer from information loss or excessive computational burden. This article refines the Gramian angular field (GAF) theory and proposes a lossless and efficient method for transforming time series into images, termed extended Gramian angular field (EGAF). Building upon EGAF, an efficient and robust fault diagnostic method is proposed, with implementation demonstrated on a two-level (2L) three-phase (3P) voltage-source-inverter (VSI). The diagnostic method first extracts the current time series under different faults through Clarke transformation and zero-crossing detection, then converts the windowed time series into 2-D matrices using EGAF to construct the diagnostic dataset, and finally implements fault classification through a lightweight convolutional neural network (CNN). The proposed diagnostic method achieved an accuracy of 98.64%, and the experimental results demonstrate its high performance.

title: SAE: Subgraph-Aware Edge Classification Framework for Monitoring Suspicious Financial Transactions in Anti Money Laundering Environments
authors: Rejuana Sushmi, Maisha Alam Mim, Sadia Islam, Raqeebir Rab, Abderrahmane Leshob, Ashikur Rahman
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Money laundering continues to be one of the toughest challenges faced by financial institutions. It involves systematically transferring illicit funds through complex, indirect paths of transactions designed to obscure their origin. Traditional AML systems rely heavily on predefined rules and transactionlevel thresholds, leading to massive numbers of false positives while often missing multi-step laundering behaviors. Recently, graph neural network-based approaches have shown promise by modeling transaction networks; however, most prevailing methods primarily focus on identifying suspicious accounts rather than specific transactions that allow for illicit financial movement. This paper proposes the Subgraph-Aware Edge (SAE) Classification Framework for Monitoring Suspicious Financial Transactions in Anti-Money Laundering Environments. This approach first represents transactional data as a directed graph and then applies an ISMAGS-based subgraph matching for the detection of laundering-specific cyclic motifs. The transactions belonging to these motifs are labeled using domain-driven temporal and monetary rules and utilized for training edge-level GNN models. Four models, GraphSAGE, GAT, GCN, and GATConv, are evaluated, while feedback-based model refinement with a consensus voting strategy is employed to enhance prediction reliability. Experimental results obtained on a synthetic transaction dataset demonstrate that the proposed method effectively identifies laundering patterns; GAT and GATConv yielded superior performance in terms of $\mathbf{F 1}$ score of $95 \%$ and $89.12 \%$, respectively. While the feedback refinement model decreased false positives rate by $14.40 \%$ in the GAT model and $53.23 \%$ in GTAConv. The framework provides interpretable, transaction-level insights, which are well-suited for practical AML investigations.

title: Companion of the 45th Symposium on Principles of Database Systems
authors: N/A

authors: Karl Bringmann

title: Guide for the redesign of websites not accessible to blind people: MiUV study case_supp1-3690147.docx
authors: Jose Rafael Rojano-Caceres
publish: N/A
abstract: This research proposes a guideline that outlines the redesign process of a web system by compiling a set of tools and techniques aimed at improving accessibility for users with visual functional diversity (blindness). It is worth mentioning that the study adheres to the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) standards using automatic evaluation programs and screen readers. The paper presents the selection process for these tools and techniques, which constitute the guideline composed of five stages: (1) training on accessibility issues, (2) initial system exploration, (3) evaluation with users and experts, (4) requirements analysis, and (5) recoding. It also specifies the artifacts to be produced at each stage and how they are used in subsequent phases. The guideline was applied to the institutional system “MiUV” which is used by the Universidad Veracruzana community, including faculty members, administrative staff and students—some of whom have visual functional diversity. The results showed that the proposed process was effective in guiding the redesign to improve accessibility. However, opportunities for improvement were also identified in each phase of the redesign process.

title: IntentQA: Intent Question Answering in Videos by Cognitive Context Reasoning_supp1-3690561.pdf
authors: Ping Wei
publish: N/A
abstract: Video understanding requires intelligent agents to transcend mere recognition of visual facts and comprehend the underlying intents behind human actions-often termed the “dark matter” of social intelligence. To bridge the gap between visual observation and intent reasoning, we introduce a novel task, IntentQA, and contribute a large-scale VideoQA dataset specifically tailored for this purpose. However, recognizing that standard metrics may overestimate capabilities due to dataset biases, we go beyond simple accuracy to rigorously evaluate model robustness. We augment the benchmark by generating five distinct contrast sets via Large Language Models (LLMs) and introducing a “Contrast Performance Decline” metric. We propose the X-CaVIR (eXplainable Context-aware Video Intent Reasoning) framework, which leverages three types of “Cognitive Context” to enhance video analysis: i) Situational Context via a cross-modal Video Query Language (VQL) module, ii) Contrastive Context via a Contrastive Learning module, and iii) Commonsense Context via a Commonsense Reasoning module. Crucially, to overcome the opacity of traditional black-box models, we refine the integration of LLMs within X-CaVIR by employing a transparent pipeline that synergizes video captions with VQA model outputs. This approach not only improves performance by effectively utilizing rich commonsense knowledge but also renders the reasoning process explicitly interpretable. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our components, the superiority of X-CaVIR over state-of-the-art baselines, and its stability against perturbations on the contrast sets. The dataset and codes are open-sourced at: https://github.com/JoseponLee/IntentQA.

title: Evaluating and Enhancing Large Language Models for Islamic Question Answering
authors: Md. Naimul Haque, Mostahid Hasan Fahim, M. Reza Selim, Md. Minhajul Haque, MD. Fuad Al Amin, Md. Ataullha, M. Shahidur Rahman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This study investigates how large language models (LLMs) perform when applied to Islamic question answering. We investigate two models, Mistral-7B and LLaMA-3.2-3B, assessing their ability to answer multiple-choice and short-answer questions derived from a curated dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 3, 0 0 0}$</tex> Islamic questions. Unlike general-purpose evaluations, our work emphasizes the unique challenges posed by religious content, including the necessity to align with authentic sources and avoid interpretive errors. The study applies prompt engineering, targeted fine-tuning and Retrieval Augmented Generation(RAG) to enhance output quality. Evaluation was conducted using manual and LLM-as-a-judge approach. Results show significant improvements with prompt engineering: LLaMA-3.2-3B improved from 38.78 % to 64.19 %, while Mistral-7B improved from 41.84 % to 70.05 %. Fine-tuning further raised Mistral-7B performance to 68.11 %. Still, the accuracy is not acceptable in such a sensitive field. Hence, we implemented a RAG pipeline to supply grounded context from authentic religious sources. However, retrieval quality, chunking limitations, and embedding mismatch led to frequent irrelevant or missing evidence. These issues also caused mistakes in nuanced religious concepts. As a result, RAG produced only a modest accuracy improvement, remaining far below expectations.

title: PoultryNet: A Lightweight CNN Student Model for Chicken Disease Classification via Knowledge Distillation from Vision Transformer
authors: Md Parvez Kabir, Rasedul Islam, Jhion Adbar Opi, Md Aminur Rahman Joy, Arefin Ahmed
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Poultry diseases seriously endanger the poultry industry, leading to economic losses, as well as undermining food security. While there are various precise heavyweight disease detection models, there are few fast, lightweight, as well as deployable, solutions that are appropriate for real-world farm deployments. This research bridges this gap by introducing PoultryNet, a tailored lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained as a student network through Knowledge Distillation (KD) from a Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) teacher. While the teacher attains 98.83 % accuracy on a Kaggle chicken disease dataset containing four disease classes, PoultryNet achieves a reasonable test accuracy of 95.93%, presenting a fast and resource-optimized alternative. Extensive experiments confirm that PoultryNet not only surpasses the best classification performance on all four classes of diseases but also requires a much smaller model size (15.6 MB) as well as a smaller number of parameters (4 million) yet attaining over 95 % teacher- network compression. It is contrasted with baseline CNN-based architectures, including MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, SqueezeNet, ShuffleNet, as well as EfficientNet, demonstrating higher efficiency as well as comparable or improved performance. Weighted precision, recall, F1-score, as well as Cohen's Kappa, also affirm its high strength. This work presents the capability of distillation-based knowledge aiding fast, precise, as well as deployable, poultry disease identification, offering an efficient farmer-deployed tool capable of scaling up to extensive health monitoring.

title: Design and Evaluation of an Anonymous Mobile Mental Health Application for College Students
authors: Kurikala Hasini, Mokshita Vp, Rajanikanth Aluvalu, Mallellu Sai Prashanth
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This article develops an evaluation and design plan for a mobile application that offers instant, anonymous, and personalised mental health support for college students. The existing college counselling services face challenges from stigma and lack of available time slots, and existing mental health mobile applications do not provide engagement and retention features and do not smoothly transition to support from a formal institution. Our solution is a mobile application, "Mentally Aware," which incorporates three core features: an AI-driven triage and recommendation system; an anonymous, encrypted peer-to-peer chat network staffed by trained student volunteers; and direct API integration with campus counselling systems for seamless support scheduling. This paper outlines a comprehensive human-centred design process which includes qualitative user interviews (n=20), iterative design with prototypes, and a formal usability study (n=15). We anticipate high usability metrics, a significant increase in students’ willingness to seek support, reduced time and effort to book a professional appointment, and decreased effort to book a professional appointment. We aim to provide a scalable hybrid human-AI support model, an operational framework for anonymous peer communications, and a secure, validated pathway for integrating digital tools into the traditional university mental health systems.

title: Comparison of Feature-Based Clustering Methods on Unlabeled Marine Acoustic Images
authors: Omar Mammadov, Rajab Iskandarli, Ismayil Kheyirbayov, Nahla El Zant El Kadhi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Marine acoustic images present unique challenges for clustering due to heterogeneous textures, irregular object boundaries, and strong noise artifacts. The absence of ground-truth labels further necessitate unsupervised approaches. In this work, we perform a comparative evaluation of feature-based clustering methods on unlabeled acoustic imagery. The dataset consists of 10,600 marine acoustic images. Four feature extraction strategies are investigated: flattened grayscale vectors, embeddings from a pretrained ResNet-50, unsupervised convolutional autoencoder features, and manually extracted low-level statistical descriptors. These representations are combined with dimensionality reduction techniques (PCA, UMAP, t-SNE) and clustering algorithms (k-means, DBSCAN, HDBSCAN, AHC, and SOM). Performance is assessed using the Silhouette coefficient, Calinski–Harabasz index, and Davies–Bouldin index. To provide a balanced evaluation across these metrics, we introduce a unified comparison score, which integrates the normalized forms of all three indices. For each feature extraction strategy, we identify the five most effective combinations and analyze their outcomes in detail. The results demonstrate that manually engineered features combined with UMAP and k-means deliver the most robust clusters, while deep embeddings from ResNet-50 and autoencoders achieve comparable performance when paired with manifold reduction. Notably, flattened pixel representations consistently underperform, confirming the necessity of feature learning for acoustic imagery.

title: NextRAG: A Bilingual Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Financial Question-Answering
