title: Research on Digital Twin Modeling and Multi-Domain Co-simulation Technology for Mechatronics Automated Production Lines in Smart Manufacturing
authors: Xiangheng Kong
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Addressing the pain points of traditional production lines, such as low design and debugging efficiency, high operation and maintenance costs, and insufficient accuracy of multi-domain coupled simulation, in the context of smart manufacturing and the need for digital transformation of mechatronics automated production lines, this paper proposes a full-element digital twin modeling and multi-domain co-simulation technology for production lines. The core objective is to construct a high-precision, dynamically calibrated digital twin model for engineering implementation, achieving seamless integration and co-simulation optimization of multi-domain models based on the systematic integration of well-established methodological frameworks. Key technologies include hierarchical digital twin architecture design, multi-physical attribute modeling, data-driven model calibration, and multi-domain time synchronization and joint solution optimization. Experimental verification was conducted using an automotive parts mechatronics production line as an example. This research provides technical support for the digital upgrade of automated production lines and has significant engineering application value.

title: Interannual Linkage Between LOD and ENSO: Insights From Geodetic Observations, Geophysical Models, and Climate Indices
authors: Małgorzata Wińska, Dominika Staniszewska, Justyna Śliwińska-Bronowicz, Jolanta Nastula
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: This study addresses interannual linkages between variations in Earth rotation (Length of Day, LOD), atmospheric dynamics (AAM), and key climate indices related to El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) events, highlighting that the mechanisms and strength of these couplings at interannual scales are still not fully quantified or consistently characterized across different datasets. This study investigates interannual linkages between Length of Day (LOD), Atmospheric Angular Momentum (AAM), and key climate indices, including the Southern Oscillation Index (SOI), Northern Oscillation Index (NOI), Global Mean Temperature (GMT), and Global Mean Sea Level (GMSL). Using advanced statistical and spectral methods, we analyze interannual oscillations (with periods between 2 and 8 years) along with variations of geodetic, atmospheric, and climatic time series to quantify correlations, lead–lag relationships, and phase coherence with respect to El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) events. Strong dynamical coupling and significant bidirectional Granger causality is observed between LOD and AAM, as well as robust in-phase correlations with major climate indices, particularly during pronounced El Niño and La Niña episodes. Our findings highlight the potential of LOD and AAM as integrative geodetic indicators for monitoring and forecasting the impacts of climate variability, underscoring the necessity of considering Earth rotation dynamics in climate system analysis.

title: Flow-Induced Vibration-Based Liquid Flow Rate Measurement With Optical Carrier Microwave Interferometry
authors: Songlin Li, Ting Xue, Yan Wu, Bin Wu
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Accurate liquid flow rate measurement is essential for efficient energy management and sustainable development. In this paper, a novel non-intrusive liquid flow rate measurement method based on optical carrier microwave interferometry (OCMI) combined with flow-induced vibration (FIV) is developed. The theoretical principle of FIV is presented, and the liquid flow rate mathematical model based on FIV is established. In the experiment, the sensing fiber is helically wrapped around the outer surface of the pipeline to effectively capture FIV signals. The Fabry-Perot interferometers (FPIs) are categorized into two types: sensing FPIs incorporating the helical fiber and reference FPIs without it. Experimental results demonstrate that sensing FPIs exhibit a strong response to liquid flow rate variations through pipeline vibrations, with a pronounced quadratic relationship between the dip frequency and the flow rate. The fitted curve achieves high accuracy, yielding a mean absolute error and root mean square error of less than 4 kHz and 5 kHz, respectively. The comparative analysis between sensing and reference FPIs not only corroborates the validity of the derived theoretical model but also confirms the effectiveness and reliability of the proposed approach, which presents a promising, non-intrusive solution for liquid flow rate monitoring in practical industrial applications.

title: Multi-Class MRI-Based Brain Tumor Classification Using EfficientNet-B0 with Enhanced Interpretability and Clinical Applicability
authors: Md. Abu Johab, Ahmad Al Arian, Nakib Aman
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: One of the most serious neurological disorders is brain tumors, and the patients with these disorders must be identified appropriately and with precision as quickly as possible. The paper compares 15 -class brain tumor classification based on the MRI image using four deep learning architectures, namely, EfficientNet-B0, ResNet50, Vision Transformer and DenseNet121. The dataset, consisting of 4,456 MRI images across 15 tumor classes, was preprocessed and augmented to address class imbalance before model training. Of the models assessed, EfficientNet-B0 provided the most accurate performance with an accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$98.35 \%, 0.9853$</tex> precision, 0.9580 recall, and 0.9686 of F1-score and provided the best performance in both the accuracy and performance levels. ROC, precision-recall charts, and Grad-CAM visualizations further demonstrate its robustness and reliability, and thus have a great potential of being used in clinical decision support when diagnosing brain tumors in a multi-class approach. Future work is to incorporate multi-modal and 3D MRI data, use more sophisticated domain adaptation methods and to do a lot of real world clinical validation. Furthermore, the integration of the emerging XAI brings an additional view of clarity and exposes the healthcare professional to its trustworthiness and widespread adoption in the various clinical setups.

title: SafeRide: A Real-Time IoT-Based Smart Helmet with Road-Condition Intelligence
authors: Mohammed Sakib Hasan, Md Abdur Rakib, Jihadul Islam, Md Mohidul Alam, Md Abdul Jobayer, Md Mahbubur Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Motorcycle riders are among the most vulnerable road users, often facing a high risk of accidents and fatalities. These risks arise from unsafe riding habits, poor road conditions, and delays in emergency response. Many existing smart helmet systems focus on single features such as alcohol detection or accident alerts, but lack a complete and scalable solution. To address these issues, this paper proposes a low-cost, realtime IoT-enabled smart helmet system that combines multiple safety and awareness features into one framework. The system integrates helmet-wear detection, alcohol monitoring, drowsiness detection, rear-vehicle proximity alerts, accident and emergency notifications, and weather-based warnings. A new road-condition prediction model using machine learning is introduced, which classifies upcoming roads as excellent, good, poor, or very poor based on GPS data. The helmet and motorcycle units are synchronized to control ignition and share data in real-time, while hazard information is uploaded to Firebase for community alerts. A smartphone application provides riders with live sensor data, navigation, and voice-based notifications to reduce distractions. Additionally, the SafeRide dataset of road conditions has been developed to support large-scale road-quality prediction across Bangladesh. Experimental results show that the proposed system is effective in improving rider safety and enabling intelligent transportation solutions.

title: Common-Mode Analysis and Suppression for Asymmetrical Six-Phase PMSM Drive Under Single-Phase Open-Circuit Fault
authors: Guangyu Wang, Hongyang Liu, Zicheng Liu, Junnan You, Rongpei Zhu, Changhao Huang, Dong Jiang, Ronghai Qu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Compared with healthy electric drive systems, open-circuit faults break the symmetry of the electric drive system, thereby inducing unique common-mode (CM) characteristics. As a result, traditional definitions and analyses of CM voltage (CMV) for health-symmetrical electric drive systems are difficult to quantitatively evaluate the CM noise of fault-asymmetric electric drive systems or guide the design of suppression methods. Therefore, this article models and analyzes the CMV under single-phase bridge open-circuit fault, revealing the formation mechanism of CMV in fault-asymmetric electric drive systems and achieving an accurate definition of CMV. On this basis, an optimized CMV suppression method suitable for faulty electric drive systems is proposed. It employs a dynamic zero-sequence injection scheme to actively cancel the CMV generated by the remaining healthy bridges, and adjusts the carrier phase based on the unique CMV characteristics of the faulty phase to suppress the CMV of the system. The experimental results verify the effectiveness of the proposed CMV modeling and analysis method for the fault-asymmetric system. Meanwhile, compared with traditional methods, the proposed CMV suppression method achieves lower CMV and CM electromagnetic interference (EMI) under single-phase open-circuit fault, providing a reference for the CM evaluation and optimal design of fault-asymmetric electric drive systems.

title:
D
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    FT-Net: Frequency-Spatial Dual Domain Fine-Tuning of Vision Foundation Models for Remote Sensing Domain Generalization Semantic Segmentation
authors: Zimeng Shen, Jue Wang, Tianyu Wei, Wenchao Liu, Xiaoyan Lu, Liang Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In recent years, remote sensing domain generalization semantic segmentation has attracted increasing attention due to significant domain shifts caused by variations in sensors, imaging conditions, and geographic regions. Vision foundation models (VFMs) possess strong general-purpose feature extraction capabilities and can be effectively transferred across diverse data, showing great potential for remote sensing domain generalization semantic segmentation. However, existing VFM-based spatial domain parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods struggle to handle pronounced cross-domain intra-class variations in remote sensing. To address this issue, the Adaptive Frequency Aware Adapter (AFA-Adapter) is proposed, which adaptively selects frequency components to improve cross-domain intra-class feature consistency. Building upon this, the Spatial Multi-Prototype Adapter (SMP-Adapter) is proposed, which clusters multiple prototypes for features of each land-cover category to model complex intra-class diversity. Land-cover features are then weighted by their nearest intra-class prototype, thereby enhancing the discriminability of easily confused features at feature cluster boundaries. By integrating these two modules, we propose a Frequency–Spatial Dual Domain Fine-Tuning Network (D<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>FT-Net), which effectively alleviates cross-domain intra-class variations and improves the generalization capability of VFMs for remote sensing domain generalization semantic segmentation. Extensive experiments under four cross-domain settings demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed D<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>FT-Net, which achieves an average mIoU improvement of 1.09% over state-of-the-art methods, with the best gain reaching 1.64%. The source code will be released at https://github.com/ssshen0315/D2FT-Net.

title: AgenticVM: Agentic AI for Adaptive Software Vulnerability Management
authors: Asrul Arifin, Hussain Ahmad, Yiyao Zhang, Diksha Goel
publish: IEEE Software
abstract: As software systems grow in complexity, vulnerability management faces increasing challenges due to high alert volumes, fragmented tools, and manual triage processes. This paper presents AgenticVM, an engineering-focused multi-agent system that integrates large language models with security tools to automate vulnerability aggregation, enrichment, prioritisation, and reporting. Across four evaluation scenarios, AgenticVM reduces raw scanner outputs into compact actionable queues, achieving up to 98% alert reduction (e.g., from 3,983 findings to 82 high-priority items), while predicting missing CVSS attributes with 89.3% accuracy. These results demonstrate improved prioritisation and reduced analyst workload without compromising risk coverage. We further distil key engineering lessons on agent decomposition, tool–LLM boundaries, failure containment, observability, and human-in-the-loop governance. Overall, AgenticVM shows that agentic architectures can enable scalable, coordinated, and practical vulnerability management while providing actionable design guidance for AI-enabled cybersecurity systems.

title: Toward Mitigating Surface Degradation of TOPCon Passivation: Understanding the Effects of Fast-Firing Temperature and Poly-Si Doping
authors: Benjamin Hammann, Wolfram Kwapil, Jana-Isabelle Polzin, Jörg Horzel, Anna Damm, Florian Schindler, Martin C. Schubert
publish: IEEE Journal of Photovoltaics
abstract: We investigate the degradation of n- and p-type tunnel oxide passivating contact (TOPCon) under illumination and elevated temperatures to elucidate the degradation mechanism and identify mitigation strategies. Because the understanding regarding surface-related degradation (SRD) differs between n- and p-type TOPCon, we address different aspects for each layer type. Regarding p-type TOPCon, the focus is on the impact of the fast-firing peak temperature, whose correlation has already been investigated for n-type TOPCon but not for p-type TOPCon. It is found that the degradation extent of p-type TOPCon is inversely correlated with the peak temperature of the fast-firing step. This is consistent with previous reports on n-type TOPCon, making a common degradation mechanism for n- and p-type TOPCon likely. For n-type TOPCon, samples processed by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition and low-pressure chemical vapor deposition (LPCVD) exhibit markedly different degradation extents. We attribute the lower degradation extent observed for LPCVD layers to its higher phosphorus concentration. We propose a unified mechanism for the degradation of both, n- and p-type TOPCon, that explains the various experimental data: hydrogen diffuses from a hydrogen containing dielectric layer (e.g., silicon nitride, SiNx) through the highly doped poly-Si layer toward the Si/SiO2 interface, where interface defects are formed. Based on this pathway, the degradation can be mitigated either by reducing the hydrogen content within the dielectric layers or by hindering the diffusion of hydrogen to the Si/SiOx interface—for example, by increasing the poly-Si dopant concentration.

title: A Distributed System to Analyze the Impacts of Aircraft Predictive Maintenance
authors: Samridhi Girdhar, Peash Ranjan Saha
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Predictive maintenance for an aircraft enforces unscheduled repairs or replacement of any mechanical components. It complements routine maintenance for an aircraft. Predictive maintenance is crucial, as it utilizes real-time data while an aircraft is in operation, unlike scheduled maintenance. However, it may have a significant impact on passengers’ experiences, such as an unexpected flight delay. It also connects airports’ external services, such as personal support for passengers. It is not certain that every airline always performs predictive maintenance, as it may have long-term consequences for their operation. Some airlines may prioritize short-term or immediate fixes instead. An analysis to unfold the passengers’ experiences due to predictive maintenance is paramount. In this research, a distributed system is designed to analyze real-time maintenance and operational data for airports. The system constructs a database for each region with one or multiple airports. It deploys a dedicated server for an airport in a distributed fashion and performs partitioning of the central data for the region. Airport-specific external factors are also merged in the impact analysis. Some analytics is performed to explore the patterns of passengers’ experience for a region. We implemented the system in the Hadoop distributed database framework (HDFS) integrated with MongoDB, and experimented with synthetic instances. The results show some insights into passenger impact for different aircraft and flight types. A complete system would be a useful guide for airlines to make informed decisions on improving passengers’ experiences while performing predictive maintenance.

title: Multi-Label Thoracic Disease Detection and Weakly Supervised Localization in Chest X-Rays Using DenseNet121 and Probabilistic CAM Pooling
authors: Ahmad Fahmid, Abdul Ahad, Sanjida Rahman, Shaharia Ferdausi, Aashish K C, S M Sabbir Hossain Pranto, Kazi Tanvir Akter, Dewan Aminul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Chest X-ray (CXR) interpretation is essential for diagnosing multiple thoracic diseases, yet it requires specialized expertise and is susceptible to human error. This paper presents a deep learning system for multi-label disease classification and weakly supervised disease-related region localization in CXRs, utilizing DenseNet121 with Probabilistic Class Activation Mapping (PCAM) pooling. Trained on the CheXpert dataset, the model predicts five key conditions—cardiomegaly, edema, consolidation, atelectasis, and pleural effusion—with high performance (AUC scores ranging from 0.8703 to 0.9436), while localizing disease-relevant regions without pixel-level annotations. Deployed via a Flask web application, the system demonstrates both diagnostic accuracy and practical usability in clinical work-flows. We evaluate its classification and localization performance, visualize interpretability results, and discuss its clinical relevance and future potential.

title: Teleportation of Coherent-State Qubits with Asymmetric and Lossy Entanglement Distribution
authors: Davide Andromari, Andrea Giani, Moe Z. Win, Andrea Conti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum teleportation enables transferring quantum states by leveraging entanglement. The distribution of entangled states is challenged by decoherence, which degrades the teleportation performance. Continuous-variable teleportation with coherent-state qubits has gained particular interest because it can provide better performance than discrete-variable teleportation with polarization-encoded qubits and is more resilient to decoherence. However, most existing schemes employ a specific configuration of coherent-state qubits, namely Schrödinger cat states, and assume symmetric photon-loss channels. This work explores the teleportation of qubits based on coherent states through asymmetric quantum channels impaired by photon loss. First, we characterize the teleportation protocol for arbitrary coherent-state qubit configurations considering asymmetric and photon-loss channels. Then, we present case studies under both ideal entanglement distribution and lossy conditions. Results reveal a trade-off between state distinguishability and teleportation fidelity, providing insight into the optimal design of coherent-state qubits in practical teleportation protocols.

title: High-Order Epistasis Detection Using Factorization Machine with Quadratic-Optimization Annealing and MDR-Based Evaluation
authors: Shuta Kikuchi, Shu Tanaka
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Detecting high-order epistasis is a fundamental challenge in genetic association studies due to the combinatorial explosion of candidate locus combinations. Although multifactor dimensionality reduction (MDR) is a widely used method for evaluating epistasis, exhaustive MDR-based searches become computationally infeasible as the number of loci or the interaction order increases. In this paper, we define the epistasis detection problem as a black-box optimization problem and solve it with a factorization machine with quadratic-optimization annealing (FMQA). We propose an efficient epistasis detection method based on FMQA, in which the classification error rate (CER) computed by MDR is used as a black-box objective function. Experimental evaluations were conducted using simulated casecontrol datasets with predefined high-order epistasis. The results demonstrate that the proposed method successfully identified ground-truth epistasis across various interaction orders and the numbers of genetic loci within a limited number of iterations. These results indicate that the proposed method is effective and computationally efficient for high-order epistasis detection.

title: TCAD-Based Variability Analysis of Cylindrical NVDRAM and VNAND Architectures with 3D Multi-Granular Polycrystalline Ferroelectric–Dielectric Phases
authors: Bellala Sai Sampath, Sarita Yadav, Sourav De, Govind Bajpai, Anand Bulusu, Kai Ni, Nitanshu Chauhan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: This work investigates the influence of random fluctuations and the interaction between randomly shaped dielectric (DE) and ferroelectric (FE) phases, modelled using Voronoi grains within the ferroelectric layer of cylindrical Metal-Ferroelectric-Metal (MFM) capacitors, Non-Volatile DRAM (NVDRAM), and vertical NAND FeFET structures. The study reveals: 1.) The inclusion of a DE phase introduces non-uniformity and leads to a reduction in the overall polarization of the FE layer. 2.) Larger Voronoi FE grain sizes show decreased variability (low <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sigma / \mu$</tex>) in MFM capacitor performance. 3.) The presence of a DE phase induces additional variability in NVDRAM, causing a decrease in the bitline read margin for bit '0' and bit '1'. 4.) Despite DE phase introduction, NVDRAM retains a superior bitline read margin compared to conventional DRAM, even at 50% DE phase. 5.) The effect of the DE phase on the memory window characteristics (low and high <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{th}}$</tex>) of vertical NAND FeFET is also analyzed.

title: BIE: Bipedal Robots Using Implicit-Explicit Learning Framework
authors: Cheng Xing, Xiaopeng Chen, Yuhan Qiu, Qi Deng
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Bipedal robots possess the unique capability to adapt human-centric environments, yet achieving agile and robust locomotion on complex terrains remains a formidable challenge due to their inherent instability and the noise associated with visual perception. Current approaches often struggle to effectively integrate high-dimensional visual data with precise motor control, leading to conservative behaviors or failure in harsh environments. In this paper, we propose a novel learning-based framework that synergizes a Teacher-Student architecture with a dual-level Implicit-Explicit learning mechanism to address these limitations. Our method leverages a teacher policy trained with privileged information to guide a student policy that operates solely on proprioceptive and noisy visual inputs. Crucially, we incorporate an implicit-explicit estimation module within the student network: the explicit component reconstructs terrain features to provide a direct understanding of the environment, while the implicit component captures latent dynamic parameters to compensate for actuation and sensing uncertainties. This hybrid approach enhances the student's ability to generalize across diverse terrains by aligning both behavioral policies and state representations. We validate our framework through extensive simulation and real-world experiments on a bipedal robot. Results demonstrate that our method achieves great performance in traversing challenging terrains with high agility and robustness, successfully bridging the sim-to-real gap without requiring complex pre-mapping.

title: Three-Class Logistic Regression for Mental Health Risk Detection on Social Media
authors: Farzana Afrin Hafsa, Sunjida Khaton, Golam Md. Muradul Bashir
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: People often share their deepest emotions on social media, including loneliness, depression, and even thoughts of self-harm. Many of these posts remain unnoticed, leaving individuals without the support they may urgently need. This study presents a simple but effective three-class model for identifying mental health risks in social media posts. Using two real Reddit datasets, we classify posts into Safe, Warning, and Harmful categories to capture both early distress and severe risk. After preprocessing the text and applying TF-IDF features with a multinomial Logistic Regression classifier, the model achieved 98% accuracy and strong F1-scores across all classes. Introducing the Warning class allows the system to detect early signs of emotional struggle that traditional two-class methods often miss. The proposed approach is lightweight, interpretable, and suitable for large-scale monitoring. Our findings highlight the importance of early-detection tools in creating safer online spaces and supporting timely mental-health interventions.

title: Building a Balanced Deepfake Dataset: Aligned Faces for Robust Model Training and Evaluation
authors: Md Raisul Islam, Mohammad Monirul Islam, MD. Aminul Islam Rakib, Md. Shihab Mahmud Anik, Amit Kumar Kundu, Md. Golam Morshed Tuhin
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Deepfake media—highly realistic AI-generated face swaps—pose a growing threat to the authenticity of digital content. As a contribution towards research into detecting such forgeries, we introduce a deepfake video dataset that is large-scale and well-annotated. It comprises 480 videos (110,694 frames) which are equally split between genuine and forged content. Deepfake videos were generated with two modern face-swap workflows (open-source Roop Face-Swapper and commercial Akool AI tool), worked on the videos of 30 volunteers (15 males, 15 females). Frames were downsampled at 5 fps, and a face detector (MTCNN) was used for cropping and aligning a main face to extract <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{3, 7 4 6}$</tex> real-face images and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0 6, 9 4 8}$</tex> fake-face images. It is demographically and generation-method balanced which can serve as a diverse reference for deepfake detection. Ethical clearance was gained from Daffodil International University. The whole dataset, including images, labels and metadata, is accessible to the research community. Our dataset is unique from previous works such as FaceForensics++ and Celeb-DF due to the demographic diversity, multi-pipeline generation, and transparent creation pipeline. To illustrate its effectiveness, a custom CNN classifier trained on the dataset achieved an accuracy of 97.8% at differentiating between real and fake faces. For the sake of transparency and reproducibility, we present full information of the recording, generation, and preprocessing pipeline.

title: WASTEWISE: Towards an Eco-Friendly Sustainable Waste Management System for Decomposable and Long-Life Waste
authors: Sirajum Munira, Ohidujjaman, Mahmudul Hasan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Waste management is a growing environmental concern worldwide today. Every day, Bangladesh generates increasing amounts of waste. But ineffective waste segregation directly undermines environmental sustainability and public health outcomes. Addressing this issue, this study represents a deep learning approach to automatically classify waste according to its decomposition nature. We evaluated CNN, VGG19, and InceptionV3 models with a custom, real-world dataset collected from households, streets, and the local environment of Bangladesh. Among these, InceptionV3 showed the best overall accuracy and generalisation, at 98.76 %. The findings of this study highlight how effectively deep learning can help to develop low-cost automated waste-sorting systems that can improve smart waste handling, recycling efficiency and sustainable environment.

title: Quantum Circuit for Quantum Fourier Transform for Arbitrary Qubit Connectivity Graphs Based on 1-Cover Path
authors: Kamil Khadiev, Aliya Khadieva, Vadim Sagitov, Kamil Khasanov
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: In the paper, we consider quantum circuits for the Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) algorithm. We present a generic method for constructing quantum circuits for QFT on quantum devices with restrictions. Many quantum devices (for example, based on superconductors) have restrictions on applying two-qubit gates presented by a qubit connectivity graph. Typically, researchers consider only the linear nearest neighbor (LNN) architecture, but current devices have more complex graphs. We present a method for arbitrary connected graphs that minimizes the number of CNOT gates in the circuit for implementing on such architecture.

title: W-State Leader Election for PBFT Based Quantum Leader Election
authors: Juan Pablo Robledo Meza, Jelena Mišić, Vojislav B. Mišić
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We propose a quantum leader election protocol for high-traffic permissioned blockchains, leveraging multipartite <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$W$</tex>-states combined with a CSMA/CA-style INTENT/ACK handshake. Our design distributes a full W-state locally to each validator, enabling collision detection through single-qubit measurement and ensuring fairness without requiring global entanglement distribution. We formalize three approaches to improve performance, Exponential Backoff, Fixed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$W$</tex>-state, and Closing Window, respectively, and analyze their expected termination times, showing that the Closing Window variant provides the best convergence behavior. Using Qiskit simulations, we evaluate correctness and demonstrate that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$W$</tex>-state-based election integrates naturally with PBFT-style consensus layers.

title: Dissolved Hydrogen Prediction in Oil-Immersed Transformers Using FEM-Based Fault-Scenario Data Augmentation and TCN-Informer
authors: Jinrui Kang, Hanbo Zheng
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Dissolved gas analysis (DGA) is important for transformer condition assessment, but real fault samples are scarce and fault-state scenarios are difficult to cover, which limits purely data-driven prediction methods. To support prediction-oriented functions in transformer digital twins (DTs), this paper develops a finite element method (FEM)-based electro-thermal-fluid multiphysics model to generate dissolved-hydrogen evolution data under representative fault durations and ambient temperatures. Based on the resulting simulation-generated fault-state dataset, a hybrid predictor combining a temporal convolutional network (TCN) and Informer is constructed for one-step-ahead forecasting of hydrogen concentration at the oil sampling port. The TCN extracts local temporal features and short-term dependencies, while Informer captures longer-range sequence correlations, enabling joint modeling of local fluctuations and global evolution trends. Experimental results under rolling sequential evaluation show that the proposed method achieves the best performance among the compared models, with a root mean square error (RMSE) of 2.713347 and a mean absolute error (MAE) of 2.312037. Compared with the second-best model, TCN-LSTM, RMSE and MAE are reduced by 6.54% and 12.56%, respectively. The results demonstrate that physics-based fault-scenario data generation combined with a hybrid sequence predictor is a feasible technical path for DT-oriented dissolved-hydrogen prediction.

title: A Time/Utility Function for Scheduling Quantum Applications
authors: Luca Marchese, Bethany Davies, Stephanie Wehner
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We introduce the concept of quantum time/utility functions (TUF) to enable effective scheduling decisions when executing quantum applications. The quantum TUF captures the relationship between the projected execution quality of an application and its execution time, enabling real-time, noise-aware scheduling. We consider different strategies for computing the TUF while addressing practical constraints, and introduce the Haar TUF, a simple, application-agnostic formula for random circuits. We implement the Haar TUF for a delegated quantum computing application, where we showcase both its ability to capture meaningful behaviour and its limitations in describing application-specific instances.

title: Hybrid AI-FACTS Optimization for Nova Scotia Grid: A Physics-Informed Deep Learning Approach for Real-Time Voltage Stability Enhancement
authors: Mahmoud Kiasari, Hamed Aly
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: The rapid decarbonization of modern power systems coupled with the necessity to integrate large amounts of intermittent renewable energy sources (IRES) (Like wind power) into transmission grids under a reduction of synchronous inertia. This transition causes severe problems in power quality, such as voltage instability, reactive power shortage, harmonics, and short-circuit strength. This paper proposes an extensive hybrid AI -- FACTS control framework specifically tuned to the Nova Scotia (NS) Power Grid, which is a representative weak grid system that experiences voltage deviation at major load centers because of variable wind production from geographically spread sites. The proposed architecture involves coupling of physics-informed neural network (PINN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) recurrent units to provide robust 15 min Ahead Voltage Magnitude Forecasting and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) controller for coordinated real time dispatching of Static Var Compensators (SVC) and Static Synchronous Compensators (STATCOM). Unlike traditional black box models for machine learning, PINN incorporate the fundamental laws of AC power flow (i.e., Kirchhoff’s Current Law and nodal power balance) directly into the training goal, which means that the predictions inherently meet the physical constraints and safety bounds. Extensive simulations on a detailed 6-Bus reduced-order model of Nova Scotia transmission backbone, validated with actual historical data (load profiles collected from PJM Interconnection and wind speed time series collected from NREL Wind Integration Database) demonstrate that the hybrid AI-FACTS framework reduces average voltage deviation by $67.7 \%$, decreases transmission power losses by $7.7 \%$, and maintains the total harmonic distortion (THD) within the compliance limits of the international standards, standard 519 (IEEE-519-2014) significantly more than the original decentralized PI-based FACTS control. The system achieves a computational efficiency appropriate for edge deployment with an inference latency of less than 50 ms per control cycle on commodity hardware.

title: Intelligence-Assisted Site Selection for Military Installation Planning
authors: Connie N. Summers, Mary Margaret Mitchell, Hyeyon Y. Bastian, Patrick R. Ables, Randy K. Buchanan, Susan Wolters
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: The inherently complex, time-intensive, and manual nature of military planning often results in inflexible master plans ill-suited to rapidly evolving mission requirements. This disconnect hinders effective adaptation and can compromise mission success. Recent advances in automated data flows and artificial intelligence (AI) offer a promising solution. An AI-enabled tool that incorporates mission requirements, restrictions on developmental land, and planning and facility design standards would empower installation planners to rapidly identify optimal locations for asset development, sustainment, restoration, and modernization on military installations, significantly reducing planning time while enabling a more agile and responsive investment decision support, ultimately enhancing mission effectiveness at the speed of relevance.

title: Autonomous Exploration Planning using Directed Single-Source Shortest Paths
authors: Fabrício C. S. Xavier, Sérgio R. Barros Dos Santos, Sidney N. Givigi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Autonomous exploration requires mobile robots to continuously decide where to navigate next and plan how to get there. Traditionally, these decision-making and path-planning tasks are handled separately to save computational power, relying on straight-line distance estimates to choose the best destination. However, in obstacle-dense environments, these simple estimates often fail, trapping the robot in inefficient paths. Relying on exact topological distances instead would solve this, but standard exact methods hit a mathematical bottleneck-scaling as $\mathcal{O}(m+n \log n)$, where n is the number of grid vertices and m is the number of edges. This complexity makes them too slow for high-resolution mapping. This paper presents a Unified Exploration Planner that overcomes this limitation by integrating a directed single-source shortest path algorithm with a sublogarithmic complexity of $\mathcal{O}\left(m \log ^{2 / 3} n\right)$. By computing the exact travel cost for the entire mapped area in a single pass, our architecture eliminates the need for rough distance estimates and merges destination selection with optimal trajectory generation. Simulations in CoppeliaSim demonstrate that this approach successfully navigates complex layouts by generating trajectories that adhere to the true topological cost. Furthermore, we demonstrate an inverse stabilization effect: as the robot maps more of the environment, the computational effort per node actually decreases, ensuring scalable and efficient long-term operations.

title: Multi-Shot Quantum Sensing for RF Signal Detection with MIMO Rydberg-Atom Receivers
authors: Saman Atapattu, Harini Hapuarachchi, Nathan Ross
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Rydberg-atom quantum receivers (RAQRs) enable electric-field sensing with quantum-noise-limited performance, yet their optical readout provides only magnitude measurements whose fluctuations follow Rician statistics governed by atomic projection noise, optical shot noise, reference-field injection, and short coherence times. These non-Gaussian, phase-blind measurements invalidate classical single-shot RF detectors and necessitate multi-shot quantum sensing strategies. This work develops a physically consistent multi-shot statistical model for RAQRs and derives both the optimal genie-aided likelihood-ratio test (LRT) and a practical phase-averaged LRT that removes dependence on the unknown RF-field phase. Closed-form test statistics and thresholds are obtained for both detectors, and the limits imposed by finite quantum shots-due to atomic dephasing and measurement backaction-are explicitly quantified. A fully non-coherent energy detector is also analysed, with exact detection probability derived using noncentral chi-square models. Monte Carlo results show that only 5-10 quantum shots yield major gains: the phase-averaged LRT closely approaches the genie bound and RAQR detection markedly outperforms classical RF energy detection under comparable received power. The proposed framework provides the first unified statistical basis for multi-shot Rydberg-based weak-field detection and underscores the potential of RAQRs for quantum-enhanced signal detection.

title: Horizontal Thruster-Based VTOL Imposition for Universal Flight Control System of Existing Vehicle
authors: Meer Shadman Shafkat Tanjim, Ashrafun Nushra Oishi, M Nafees Emtiaz Chowdhury
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Swamped metropolitan area always faces the daily rush and high vehicle usage. Emergency transportation, road blockage, armed security become an issue in that circumstance. Roads or highways' planning and public transportation sector never remain hassle-free from the problematic issues along with the traffic jam. Overpassing few significant vehicles with the proposed system of this research will subdue the problems and increase the height of comfort in current vehicle's flight system concept. Bernoulli's principle and duct theory are the major inspiration for this research. By this universal Flight Control System (FCS), a balanced, portable, comfortable and instant flight facilitation have been introduced. With a prototype, the overall system has been demonstrated. Data have been analyzed for the final implementation on any mid-weighted vehicle. With a preliminary feature of instant flight and forward-backward movement along with left or right turns, the system can be installed on any existing vehicle. The driver can move that vehicle on the air, like a comfortable jerk-less vehicle on the road and seamlessly land wherever it is needed. A major solution for national emergency, military activity, and luxury sector can be facilitated with this horizontal thruster-based Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL) imposition in some existing vehicle on demand along with hybrid driving license generation from corresponding authority.

title: Predictive Modeling of Wildfire Risk in California, North America, using Machine Learning Ensembles
authors: Walid Hakimzada, Colton Carvalho, Craig Beaubien, Thanh Le, Bonaventure Chidube Molokwu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Wildfires in California are increasing in frequency and severity due to climate change and prolonged drought, highlighting the need for accurate daily ignition prediction. This study presents a weather-only machine learning framework using a 41-year (1984-2025) statewide daily meteorological dataset to predict whether any wildfire ignition occurs on a given day. The approach addresses seasonal cyclicity, lagged weather effects, and moderate class imbalance through temporal feature engineering, cyclic encoding, normalization, SMOTE balancing, and stratified 5-fold cross-validation. Among evaluated models, a tuned Random Forest performed best, achieving Precision $=0.6032$, Recall $=0.7646, \mathrm{~F} 1=0.6744, \mathrm{MCC}=0.4913$, and ROC-AUC $=0.8211$ on the test set, with minimum temperature and seasonal signals as key predictors. Results show that combining temporal features with imbalance-aware ensemble methods substantially improves ignition forecasting, offering a scalable foundation for daily wildfire risk awareness and potential integration into operational workflows.

title: Folding the Vision: Towards Efficient Global Context Representation on Edge Devices
authors: Ang Li, Shouxiang Ni
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Traditional efficient CNNs face limitations in capturing global dependencies due to local receptive fields, while ViTs suffer from quadratic complexity. Recent works attempt to combine convolution and attention mechanisms, yet still struggle with computational overhead that limits inference speed on mobile devices. In this work, we propose FoldNet, a pure convolutional architecture that achieves global context representation through hardware-efficient operations. First, we propose the Fold Convolution that reorganizes spatial pixels into the channel dimension, capturing global spatial attention via channel interactions, with lower computational costs compared to ViTs. Second, we design the Partial Re-parameterization Module that applies the structural re-parameterization technique to a subset of feature channels. This approach achieves nearly identical performance compared to full-channel processing while significantly minimizing redundant computations and memory accesses. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of FoldNet over existing state-of-the-art methods. For example, on ImageNet-1K, our Foldnet-S1 achieves 79.0% Top-1 accuracy, while only has 1.0 ms inference speed on mobile devices. Comprehensive experimental results on high-resolution downstream tasks show that FoldNet outperforms existing efficient models, maintaining a superior trade-off between inference speed and accuracy.

title: GenoPIM: Enhancing Genome Alignment using Digital Processing-in-Memory
authors: Dhruv Kulkarni, Sai Manoj Pudukotai Dinkarrao
publish: IEEE Embedded Systems Letters
abstract: Genome alignment is an emerging application in the field of genomics. There have been several hardware chips that have been introduced that aim to optimize Genome sequencing, but none of them utilize Look-up table based approaches. We implement a Look-up-Table based PIM (Processing-in-Memory) chip for Dynamic Programming based algorithms, such as Needleman-Wunsch. Our results indicate up to 4.5× higher substrate-level throughput potential than a CPU baseline under an architectural upper-bound model, with substantially lower estimated energy per DP cell than contemporary PIM designs and a naive GPU baseline.

title: Semi-Supervised Model-Free Bayesian State Estimation from Compressed Measurements
authors: Anubhab Ghosh, Yonina C. Eldar, Saikat Chatterjee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing
abstract: We consider data-driven Bayesian state estimation from compressed measurements (BSCM) of a model-free process. The dimension of the temporal measurement vector is lower than that of the temporal state vector to be estimated, leading to an under-determined inverse problem. The underlying dynamical model of the state’s evolution is unknown for a ‘model-free process.’ Hence, it is difficult to use traditional model-driven methods, for example, Kalman and particle filters. Instead, we consider data-driven methods. We experimentally show that two existing unsupervised learning-based data-driven methods fail to address the BSCM problem in a model-free process. The methods are – data-driven nonlinear state estimation (DANSE) and deep Markov model (DMM). While DANSE provides good predictive/ forecasting performance to model the temporal measurement data as a time series, its unsupervised learning lacks suitable regularization for tackling the BSCM task. We then propose a semi-supervised learning approach and develop a semi-supervised learning-based DANSE method, referred to as SemiDANSE. In SemiDANSE, we use a large amount of unlabelled data along with a limited amount of labelled data, i.e., pairwise measurement-and-state data, which provides the desired regularization. Using benchmark chaotic dynamical systems, we empirically show that the data-driven SemiDANSE provides competitive state estimation performance for BSCM using a handful of different measurement systems, against a hybrid method called KalmanNet and two model-driven methods (extended Kalman filter and unscented Kalman filter) that know the dynamical models exactly.

title: A Framework for Energy-Efficient Hybrid Transceiver Design in Multi-Hop Communications
authors: Hanyu Yang, Heng Liu, Shiqi Gong, Chengwen Xing, Nan Zhao, Dusit Niyato
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: In this paper, we propose a general energy efficiency (EE) optimization framework for the hybrid analog-digital transceivers design in multi-hop communication systems. The analog and digital beamforming matrices are jointly optimized considering two kinds of practical power constraint models, i.e., sum power with box eigenvalue constraints (SPBECs) and multiple weighted power constraints (MWPCs), and unit-modulus constraints on analog beamforming matrices. For both the SPBECs and MWPCs cases, to tackle the challenging problem involving highly-coupled variables, an effective decoupling approach is first proposed. Specifically, a set of auxiliary variables are introduced to equivalently transform the original problem into a decoupled form with respect to the variables of each node. Then, for each node, we propose an efficient two-stage analog and digital beamforming optimization algorithm. To be specific, we optimize the analog beamforming matrices in the first stage by jointly exploiting the matrix-monotonic optimization framework and channel-alignment strategy. Then, we optimize the digital beamforming matrices in the second stage based on the multi-node water-filling methodology. Furthermore, in order to compute the parameters involved in the multi-node water-filling solutions for the SPBECs case, we propose two novel strategies, i.e., the Dinkelbach based strategy and the per-node penalty based strategy, which derive the parameters in closed-forms and offer clear physical interpretations. Moreover, the per-node penalty based strategy is effectively extended to the MWPCs case by additionally employing the Lagrangian duality theory. Simulation results demonstrate the superior performance and high efficiency of our proposed algorithms.

title: Bio-Inspired Divergent Stereo SLAM with Enhanced Rotational Robustness_supp1-3690993.mp4
authors: Bin Han
publish: N/A
abstract: Robust visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (vSLAM) is essential for autonomous navigation, yet a limited field of view (FoV) leads to feature loss during fast rotations, degrading tracking and localization performance. Traditional parallel stereo vSLAM offers limited FoV due to high camera overlap, resembling monocular systems. Existing FoV extension methods, based on wide-angle lenses or multi-camera setups, suffer from distortion or high system complexity. Inspired by navigation-oriented species, which use divergent eye placements to expand their FoV, we propose a divergent stereo vSLAM system that uses two outward-facing cameras with partial overlap, thereby extending the overall FoV. Traditional stereo systems use the left camera for primary vision and the right for depth estimation, limiting the use of the divergent configuration. To overcome this, we propose divergent stereo modeling, treating the cameras as virtual cameras to improve depth estimation and wide-angle perception. Although multi-camera systems can adopt a divergent configuration, existing methods overlook inter-camera correlation. We address this with a cross-camera map point association and a dual-map bundle adjustment (BA) with fixed extrinsics to maintain consistency and reduce redundancy. Experimental results on simulated and real-world datasets show that our framework improves localization accuracy (ATE) by 65.8% over parallel stereo and achieves superior rotational robustness. All code will be open source.

title: Movable Antenna-Enabled Reliable Multi-User Uplink Transmission Under Jamming Attacks
authors: Zhihui Shang, Zhiqiang Xiao, Zhi Lin, Xiaoqiang Qiao, Hao Wu, Tao Zhang, Guan Gui
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Movable antenna (MA) technology has emerged as a promising solution to enhance wireless reliability against malicious jamming. It achieves this by exploiting spatial degrees of freedom through dynamic antenna repositioning within a confined area. This paper investigates the max-min fairness problem in an MA-enabled reliable multi-user uplink system under jamming attacks. The objective is to maximize the worst-case signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) at the base station (BS) by jointly optimizing the positions of the MAs and the receive beamforming (RB) vector, while satisfying the SINR requirement for each user equipment. The formulated optimization problem is non-convex and thus challenging to solve directly. To address it, we first consider a special case where the antenna positions of the BS are fixed, and derive a closed-form optimal RB based on the minimum mean square error (MMSE) criterion. Building on this insight, we develop an alternating optimization framework coupled with successive convex approximation (SCA) to iteratively update the MA positions and RB design in the general scenario. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed scheme significantly outperforms conventional fixed-antenna systems in terms of both communication efficiency and anti-jamming robustness, highlighting the practical potential of MAs for wireless communications in adversarial environments.

title: A Routh Zero-Location Testing Method for Integer Polynomials With Any Singularity Profile
authors: Yuval Bistritz, Ruth Weinberg
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: The paper presents an integer-preserving algorithm to determine the zero location (ZL) with respect to the imaginary axis for any polynomial with Gaussian-integer coefficients. It extends the so-called optimal integer Routh stability test, previously limited to normal conditions, to integer polynomials with any singularity profile. The primary advantage of integer arithmetic lies in its inherent error-free quality. This characteristic is particularly crucial for the general ZL case because it involves workarounds for abnormal and singular cases that are triggered by correct identification of the value 0 - an occurrence that can be obscured by rounding errors in floating-point arithmetic. Running the algorithm with integers, prevents the risk of false acceptance or rejection of these workarounds. Since in practice any non-integer polynomial can be scaled into an integer polynomial, the new algorithm paves the way for an error-free solution to the classical Routh-Hurwitz problem in its most general possible setting.

title: Aging Aware Body Biasing Technique for NBTI-Mitigation in Digital LDO Regulator
authors: Kartikay Mani Tripathi, Madhav Pathak, Sanjeev Manhas, Anand Bulusu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: Negative bias temperature instability (NBTI) is a significant reliability issue with the PMOS devices, increasing their threshold voltage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text{TH}}\right)$</tex> over time. Digital Low-Dropout Voltage Regulators (DLDOs), favored for their compatibility with low supply voltages and ease of integration, are vulnerable to aging-induced degradation. This paper analyzes the degradation of load transient performance and load current capacity of DLDOs due to NBTI-induced aging. We also propose an Aging-Aware Body Biasing (A-ABB) technique that utilizes Forward Body Bias (FBB) to reduce the body potential and compensate for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{TH}}$</tex> degradation in the PMOS array. The A-ABB technique is implemented in a commercial 22nm FDSOI CMOS process and is validated through circuit-level simulations for a 5-bit DLDO under pre- and post-aging stress equivalent to five years of NBTI degradation. Results show that without A-ABB, the DLDO experiences a 10% reduction in maximum load current capacity and a 9.6% increase in voltage droop during transients. However, these metrics are restored to near pre-aging conditions with the proposed A-ABB at a minimal area/power overhead.

title: FCS-MPC Based Dual-Mode Control Strategy for Stability Enhancement of Multi-Converter System
authors: Lingyu Wu, Li Zhen, Chengbo Zhang, Jiawei Liu, Yingqi Wang, Zhenbin Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of renewable energy, power systems frequently exhibit weak grid characteristics. Although dual-mode control enables adaptive operation, line impedance mismatches in multi-converter clusters often induce oscillatory instability after the system transitions to the gridforming mode. This paper identifies that these mismatches generate circulating currents, creating a destabilizing positive feedback loop with high grid impedance. To address this, a composite control framework is proposed. It features an improved gridforming strategy integrating reactive power sharing and voltage recovery loops to decouple control objectives, alongside Finite Control Set Model Predictive Control for fast inner-loop dynamics. Simulation results confirm that the proposed strategy achieves precise power sharing and smooth mode switching, ensuring robust stability even under extremely weak grid conditions.

title: A Compact mmWave Inset-Fed Rectangular Microstrip Patch Antenna for High-Efficiency Breast Tumor Detection
authors: Shamiul Ashraf Shoishob, Monowar Mahmud, Atikur Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: This paper proposes a compact inset-fed rectangular microstrip patch antenna <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(4.7 \times 4.7 \times 0.508 \text{mm}^{3})$</tex> for non-invasive breast tumor detection in the mmWave Q-band <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(30-40 \text{GHz})$</tex>. Designed in CST Studio Suite 2019, it achieves high radiation efficiency (91.65%), wide bandwidth (14.1 GHz), and a low SAR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0.445 \mathrm{W} / \text{kg})$</tex>. Simulated within a realistic three-layer breast phantom, the antenna effectively detects tumors as small as 1.5 mm by analyzing return loss, transmission coefficient, and signal attenuation. Its compact size, high efficiency, and diagnostic precision make it well-suited for integration into mmWave biomedical imaging systems.

title: Explainable Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Class Medical Disease Classification Using DenseNet121
authors: Al Arian Ahmad, Salehin Ahmad, Durjoy Kumar Dutta, Md. Abdul Wadud, Nymur Rahaman Antor, Nakib Aman
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Accurate identification of rare and clinically significant disease categories is a critical challenge in modern medical diagnostics. In this study, a deep learning-based framework is presented for automated detection and classification of 25 disease categories, encompassing diverse syndromes and pathological conditions. We evaluated two state-of-the-art convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures, DenseNet121 and MobileNetV2, in combination with Explainable AI (XAI) techniques to ensure both high predictive performance and model interpretability. Experimental results reveal that DenseNet121 achieves superior performance with an overall classification accuracy of 99.82%, outperforming MobileNetV2 in diagnostic precision. The integration of XAI provides meaningful visual explanations of model decisions, enhancing transparency and clinical trustworthiness. These findings demonstrate the potential of deep learning coupled with explainability to provide robust and interpretable diagnostic support tools, paving the way for improved early detection and better clinical decision-making across multiple disease categories.

title: HQCPA: A Hybrid Quantum-Classical Parallel Architecture for Quantum Reinforcement Learning
authors: Xinliang Wei, Kejiang Ye, Cheng-Zhong Xu, Yu Wang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: This paper presents a novel hybrid quantumclassical parallel architecture (HQCPA) designed specifically for quantum reinforcement learning. The architecture effectively combines the strengths of both quantum and classical computing paradigms, aiming to develop a more efficient hybrid quantum reinforcement learning model. The concept of this architecture is founded on the hybrid sequential architecture, where the classical neural network serves as either a pre-processing or postprocessing module. By partitioning the intermediate sequential quantum layer into multiple parallel layers, the proposed model enables more precise control over the model's architecture. This not only enhances the flexibility in model design but also mitigates the computational errors that typically arise from an increase in the number of qubits and circuit depth. In addition, a flexible data re-uploading structure for HQCPA, denoted as HQCPADRU, is introduced to enhance the inference performance. Furthermore, a hybrid quantum-classical reinforcement learning (HQCRL) framework is implemented. This framework integrates the proposed HQCPA and HQCPA-DRU models, along with other classical RL algorithms. Comprehensive simulation results validate the efficacy of the proposed HQCPA and HQCPA-DRU. In terms of average training and inference performance, these models outperform other benchmark models by up to 18.7 %.

title: Reducing PM-to-AM Noise Conversion in Atomic Vapors
authors: Kaitlin Harpenau, Nate Ristoff, James Camparo
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Ultrasonics, Ferroelectrics, and Frequency Control
abstract: Laser phase noise (PM) to transmitted intensity noise (AM) conversion is a fundamental aspect of the field/matter interaction, and a major noise process that limits the frequency stability of next-generation atomic clocks. It arises from the absorption cross section’s dependence on laser phase and is especially pernicious for vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers (VCSELs) given their relatively broad singlemode linewidths (e.g., 40 MHz). This is unfortunate, since VCSELs (with their very low operating power and low probability for mode hops) are otherwise ideal for quantum devices. Here, we demonstrate that atomic spectroscopy with N VCSELs reduces the total PM-to-AM noise by a factor of ~N<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1/2</sup>. Consequently, atomic clocks with (for example) five VCSELs would have a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sub>) approximately 120% higher than the same device operating with one VCSEL (SNR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub>) at the same total power: SNR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sub>/SNR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> ≌ 2.2.

title: Real-time Dual Sensing of cTnI and Myoglobin via Printable Graphitized Electrodes
authors: Suhaib Imtiyaz, Mohsina Afrooz, Parvathy Nair, Sonal Fande, Khairunnisa Amreen, Sanket Goel
publish: IEEE Transactions on NanoBioscience
abstract: Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) has emerged as a serious global health concern. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), health authorities report approximately 100 million cases annually worldwide, with about 9 million associated mortalities. As AMI can affect individuals regardless of their age or prior health status, making early and pre-symptomatic detection is crucial for clinical intervention. Targeting AMI biomarkers such as cardiac troponin I (cTnI) and myoglobin is therefore essential for timely diagnosis and treatment. Hence, the development of ultra-sensitive and highly specific sensors is crucial. Herein, a laser-induced graphene (LIG)-based electrochemical biosensor capable of dual detection of two pivotal AMI biomarkers, cTnI and myoglobin, is presented, hence addressing the need for simultaneous biomarker monitoring. Electrochemical analytical techniques such as Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques were employed, achieving limits of detection (LoD) and quantification (LoQ) of 0.035 ng/mL and 0.105 ng/mL for cTnI, and 1.39 ng/mL and 4.25 ng/mL for myoglobin, respectively. The fabricated electrodes exhibited excellent stability and reproducibility, supported by physicochemical characterizations confirming detailed electrode morphology. Furthermore, this dual-detection platform was successfully tested for real-time detection in human blood serum, yielding high recovery values and demonstrating its potential for practical, real-world applications.

title: Quantum Circuit-Based Adaptation for Credit Risk Analysis
authors: Halima Giovanna Ahmad, Alessandro Sarno, Mehdi El Bakraoui, Carlo Cosenza, Clément Bésoin, Francesca Cibrario, Valeria Zaffaroni, Giacomo Ranieri, Roberto Bertilone, Viviana Stasino, Pasquale Mastrovito, Francesco Tafuri, Davide Massarotti, Leonardo Chabbra, Davide Corbelletto
publish: IEEE Transactions on Quantum Engineering
abstract: Noisy and Intermediate-Scale Quantum, or NISQ, processors are sensitive to noise, prone to quantum decoherence, and are not yet capable of continuous quantum error correction for fault-tolerant quantum computation. Hence, quantum algorithms designed in the pre-fault-tolerant era cannot neglect the noisy nature of the hardware, and investigating the relationship between quantum hardware performance and the output of quantum algorithms is essential. In this work, we experimentally study how hardware-aware variational quantum circuits on a superconducting quantum processing unit can model distributions relevant to specific use-case applications for Credit Risk Analysis, e.g., standard Gaussian distributions for latent factor loading in the Gaussian Conditional-Independence model. We use a transpilation technique tailored to the specific quantum hardware topology, which minimizes gate depth and connectivity violations, and we calibrate the gate rotations of the circuit to achieve an optimized output from quantum algorithms. Our results demonstrate the viability of quantum adaptation on a small-scale, proof-of-concept model inspired by financial applications and offer a good starting point for understanding the practical use of NISQ devices.

title: Hybrid Framework for Tomato Leaf Disease Detection Employing Densenet121 and Ensemble Learning Techniques
authors: Iffat Shamia Shairy, Ajmy Alaly, Airin Akther, Aklima Akther Akhi, Ainea Esrat Esika, Md Saef Ullah Miah
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Tomato leaf diseases significantly reduce yield and quality in commercial production, making early and reliable diagnosis essential. This paper presents a hybrid tomato leaf disease classification framework that combines deep feature extraction using a pre-trained DenseNet121 model with multiple traditional machine learning classifiers and soft-voting ensemble. The dataset consists of 622 tomato leaf images from six classes (five disease categories and one healthy class), which are augmented through rotations, flips, brightness adjustment, and random zooming to address class imbalance and increase variability. Each image is resized to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$224 \times 224$</tex> pixels and normalized before being passed through DenseNet121 to obtain high-level feature representations. These features are then used to train five classifiers: k-nearest neighbors, support vector machine, multilayer perceptron, LightGBM, and random forest. Their probabilistic outputs are fused using a soft-voting strategy to obtain the final prediction. Experimental results show that the ensemble model achieves 99.49% accuracy, outperforming all individual classifiers and several recent state-of-the-art methods on the same task. The proposed framework demonstrates that combining deep feature extraction with lightweight traditional classifiers provides an accurate and computationally efficient solution for practical tomato leaf disease detection and decision support in precision agriculture.

title: Advancements in Image Captioning: A Comprehensive Survey on Techniques, Modalities, and Applications
authors: Ashokkumar Janarthanan, Arjun Paramarthalingam, Shanmugam Sundaramurthy, Yokeshvaran S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Recent advances in image captioning come from deep learning methods. These methods connect visual understanding with natural language generation. Early CNN– RNN models had some success, but they struggled with making sense and reasoning in context. To fix these issues, researchers added multi-attention mechanisms, hierarchical designs, and multimodal pre-training. These changes greatly improve caption quality. In medicine, models like MedViLL use BERT designs to improve performance on various tasks. Also, using patient background information shows the value of specific medical knowledge. Generative adversarial and knowledge-enhanced frameworks, like RAGAN and EURAIC, improve feature representation. Also, fine-grained techniques such as ASP help achieve a deeper semantic understanding. This paper highlights recent developments that show the need to combine vision and language models. It shows we need more research on common-sense reasoning, fairness, and real-time captioning. These areas will help create more human-like and context-aware image descriptions.

title: Generative AI Meets Responsible AI and Affective Computing
authors: Kamran Hassan Shomrat, Gazi Arman Islam, Atkea Fauzia Eva, Md. Saiful Islam, Jamilatun Subarna, Anika Tahsin, Md. Golam Rabiul Alam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)

title: Towards Climate-Resilient Farming: A Critical Review of Adaptive Decision Support Systems and User-Centered Design Approaches
authors: Nayab Nadeem, Amina Khalid, Marium Jalal Chaudhry, Awais Yasin
publish: 2026 Global Conference on Wireless and Optical Technologies (GCWOT)
abstract: Due to climate change, Pakistan's agriculture has become critically vulnerable to climate related hazards, yet limited adoption has been seen regarding existing digital advisory tools due to their topdown nature and poor configuration with farmers' localized requirements. Identifying key barriers to effective decision support, this paper reviews the landscape of climate-resilient farming practices and digital agriculture in Pakistan. It has been found out that current systems are largely static, they also fail to incorporate indigenous knowledge and lack mechanisms for contextual adaptation. To address this gap, specifically for Pakistani farmers, we propose an integrated framework for an Adaptive Decision Support System (ADSS). The features of proposed farmer-centric framework are: (1) participatory incorporation of indigenous and scientific data; (2) tailored, context aware advice provided by a dynamic recommendation engine; and (3) an iterative feedback loop empowering continuous system learning from farmer decisions. By putting the ADSS as a collaborative platform rather than a prescriptive tool, farmers as active co-creators of resilience strategies are empowered by this framework. This paper delivers an actionable roadmap for developing comprehensive, adaptive digital tools to build genuine climate resilience in Pakistan's agricultural division.

title: Transparent Quality Assurance for Low-Code/No-Code Workflows via Explainable AI and Automated Test Generation
authors: W. M. N. S. K. Yatigammana
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Low-code/no-code (LC/NC) platforms speed up application delivery through visual workflows and reusable components, but their abstraction and limited observability make rigorous quality assurance challenging, especially for boundary conditions, exception handling, and stakeholder-friendly defect diagnosis. This paper presents an AI-driven Explainable Testing Framework for systematic and transparent validation of LC/NC workflows without relying on traditional source-code-centric methods. The framework (i) extracts visual workflow logic into a platform-neutral intermediate representation, (ii) automatically generates and executes functional, boundary, and exception-oriented test cases for comprehensive path exploration, and (iii) converts execution traces into multi-modal explanations using an explainable AI reasoning engine and role-specific interfaces for citizen developers, QA engineers, and executives. A prototype implemented on Microsoft PowerApps workflows shows strong extraction reliability across <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N=147$</tex> workflows and generates an average of 24 test cases per workflow while reducing redundant executions. The explainability module also achieves high-quality results in readability, trace fidelity, decision-point coverage, and workflow-to-visual correspondence, helping stakeholders understand not only what failed, but why. Comparative evaluation against manual and baseline scripted validation shows better defect detection, lower testing and resolution time, and improved stakeholder understanding. Overall, the approach strengthens LC/NC quality assurance by combining automated rigor with stakeholder-oriented transparency, supporting governance-aligned adoption of citizen-developed workflows in enterprise settings. In doing so, it helps bridge the gap between automated workflow validation and stakeholder interpretability, enabling more evidence-driven release readiness in LC/NC systems.

title: From Threat Reports to Security Knowledge: Building an LLM-Based Pipeline for AI Systems
authors: Takuma Tsuchida, Yuya Fujiwara, Hironori Washizaki, Naoyasu Ubayashi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Building structured and reusable security knowledge from unstructured threat reports is a challenging and predominantly manual task in the AI security domain. Although existing knowledgebases systematically organize adversarial threats, their reliance on manual curation by security experts limits scalability and responsiveness to new threats. Automating the structuring and maintenance of such knowledgebases not only improves scalability but also bridges the gap between threat intelligence and software engineering practices, facilitating the secure-by-design development of AI systems. We propose a pipeline based on a large language model that automatically extracts, refines, and classifies threat actions from incident reports into structured security knowledge. Our pipeline encompasses four critical phases: (1) threat-action extraction, (2) quality-guided refinement, (3) mapping to an established threat classification framework, and (4) structured knowledge construction with metadata generation. Evaluation shows 0.85 precision in extraction, with refinement further enhancing coverage. The classification phase effectively maps actions to known threat patterns while also identifying potentially novel threats that have yet to be documented, supporting the continuous evolution of the knowledgebase. These results lay the groundwork for scalable, automated construction and maintenance of AI security knowledge.

title: Social Media Analytics for Sustainable Educational Decision-Making: A Case Study of TikTok’s Impact on Student Learning Behavior
authors: Majid Lateef Abdulrazaq, Ahmed Basil Suwaidan, Mustafa Maad Hamdi, Noor Raji Mohammed, Marwan Abdeldayem, Ibrahim Hamad Shabeeb, Saeed Hameed Aldulaimi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study aims to discover the reality of using the TikTok application by elementary, middle and high school students. A questionnaire was conducted to describe the reality of students’ use of the TikTok application (information, comments, and questions. The method used in this research is a quantitative method with a pre-experimental design. Therefore, this is often called a "quasy experiment. In this research, the researchers used a pretest and posttest approach with a non-equivalent control group design. This design requires two sample classes, namely the experimental class and the control class. The results indicate a double effect of gender and duration of use on the level of intellectual extremism, which is consistent with a number of recent studies. This study recommends increasing resources for raising awareness of the dangers of social networking sites, and methods for their optimal use for digital citizenship.

title: XGception: Unveiling Mosquito Larvae Patterns with XAI
authors: Saif Mostasir Rohan, Sadman Samir Rafith, Md. Sayem Kabir, Maksudul Hasan Pranto, Touhid Bhuiyan, Kazi Tanvir
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Mosquito larvae, the aquatic life stage of mosquitoes, play a crucial role in the transmission of deadly diseases such as malaria, dengue, and Zika virus. Early detection and control of mosquito larvae are vital for effective disease management, yet traditional methods often struggle with inefficiencies, resistance, and limited scalability. Deep learning offers a promising alternative, but challenges such as limited annotated data, environmental variability, and class imbalance hinder many existing approaches. This study introduces a novel hybrid deep learning framework, combining the Xception model for feature extraction with XGBoost for classification.
