title: Modeling and Simulation of Seven-Phase Induction Motor Driven by Electric Power
authors: Sinan Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: The seven-phase induction motor for electric propulsion systems demonstrates outstanding performance with high system stability, reliability, and high-power output at low voltage, along with minimal torque pulsation. It is widely used in aerospace, marine propulsion, and related fields. This paper first introduces the structural characteristics and working principles of the seven-phase induction motor, then establishes its mathematical model based on coordinate transformation and electromagnetic theory. Using MATLAB software, simulation programs were developed to analyze the motor's performance under various operating conditions, including normal startup with propeller load, startup with asymmetric power supply, and startup with phase loss. The simulation results indicate that the seven-phase induction motor maintains stable operation even under asymmetric power supply or phase loss conditions, with minimal impact on speed and torque. This study provides a theoretical foundation for the engineering application of seven-phase induction motors in electric propulsion systems.

title: Research and Application of Voltage Optimization Technology for Active Distribution Networks Considering the State Regulation of Distributed Resources
authors: Xi He, Huawei Hou, Wei Chen, Xia Li, Fengtao Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Driven by the “Dual Carbon” goal, distribution networks have become the core carrier connecting distributed new energy to power systems. However, the uncertainty and intermittency of new energy generation cause voltage fluctuations, over-limits, and increased network losses, while traditional Reactive Power and Voltage Control (VVC) methods are limited by reliance on accurate mechanism models. To address these issues, this paper proposes a voltage optimization method for active distribution networks based on the Multi-Agent Soft Actor-Critic (MASAC) algorithm. Built on the “Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE)” framework, it integrates edge computing and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to form a cloud-edge collaborative system, zoning the distribution network into edge agents trained with the goal of minimizing network losses. Simulations on the IEEE 33-bus system show that the method achieves collaborative control via local information collection, reducing communication costs significantly. It coordinates photovoltaics, wind turbines, and energy storage devices, effectively controlling node voltages within the safe range with reduced network losses and fluctuations, and exhibits excellent generalization and robustness without relying on accurate models.

title: PMSM Asymmetric Model Predictive Current Control Based on Trajectory Error Minimization
authors: Kejie Feng, Bin Liu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Model predictive current control (MPCC) with an optimal duty cycle exhibits excellent performance in permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drives. Recent studies indicate that the introduction of asymmetric pulse sequences can significantly reduce current harmonics in DMPCC. However, existing methods primarily rely on the complex integration of the single-period RMS current ripple to obtain the optimal time allocation. The involved high-order polynomial calculations impose a heavy computational burden on digital signal processors (DSPs), limiting their industrial applications. To overcome this computational bottleneck, this paper proposes a low-complexity asymmetric double-vector MPCC (ADV-MPCC) strategy based on a simplified trajectory cost function (STCF). By analyzing the geometric characteristics of the error trajectory, the proposed method abandons the cumbersome continuous-time integration over the entire period, and instead minimizes the sum of squared errors at critical discrete points. This transformation converts the complex functional optimization into a simple quadratic extremum problem, thereby rapidly deriving the optimal duration ratio. Simulation results verify that the proposed strategy significantly reduces algorithmic complexity while perfectly preserving the harmonic suppression advantages of asymmetric pulses.

title: Enhancing Security in Near-Field Location Division Multiple Access by Variational Quantum Circuit
authors: Quan Minh Nguyen, Bhaskara Narottama, Minh-Hien T. Nguyen, Vishal Sharma, Quang Nhat Le, Trung Q. Duong
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Location division multiple access (LDMA), enabled by extremely large-scale antenna arrays (ELAAs), allows user distinction in near-field communications (NFC) based on their spatial locations. By exploiting signal orthogonality in both the angular and distance domains, LDMA achieves precise signal focusing, with unique beamforming vectors assigned to the corresponding users. Leveraging these spatial degrees of freedom to enhance physical-layer-security, we propose a sequential quantum-classical (SQC) workflow to optimize the hybrid beamforming architecture. This workflow jointly maximizes the sum secrecy rate and enables efficient power allocation under quality-of-service (QoS) constraints. To address the resulting high-dimensional, and non-convex optimization problem, the proposed approach incorporates the metaheuristic differential evolution algorithm upon the existing variational quantum circuits (VQC). This integration alleviates quantum computational resource demands while maintaining effective solution-space exploration. Simulation results demonstrate the robustness of the proposed method against eavesdropping attacks, even in worstcase scenarios where legitimate users and eavesdroppers share identical angles of departure (AoD).

title: A Surface Temperature Model of Transmission Lines Based on Heat Balance Theory
authors: Xukang Cui, Rundong Shen, Hongwei Liu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: The surface temperature of transmission lines is a key indicator for dynamic line rating, operating condition assessment, and safety early warning. Accurate calculation of this parameter is of great significance for exploiting the latent transmission capacity of lines and ensuring the secure operation of the power grid. Based on heat balance theory, this paper establishes a calculation model for the surface temperature of transmission lines by using COMSOL software. First, the basic principles of the heat balance equation for transmission lines are described, and the heat-exchange components, including conductor Joule heating, solar radiation intensity, convective heat dissipation, and radiative heat dissipation, are analyzed to establish a temperature calculation framework under steady-state and transient conditions. Second, according to the mechanisms by which complex meteorological conditions, such as wind speed, ambient temperature, and solar radiation, affect the heat balance process, the parameter calculation method of the traditional model is revised. The results show that the surface temperature model based on heat balance theory can effectively reflect the thermal dynamic characteristics of conductors. Future studies should further focus on radial temperature distribution modeling, heat-transfer characteristics under extreme operating conditions such as icing, and the adaptive identification of model parameters.

title: Agree to Disagree: Consensus-Free Flocking under Constraints
authors: Peter Travis Jardine, Sidney Givigi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Robots sometimes have to work together with a mixture of partially-aligned or conflicting goals. Flocking coordinated motion through cohesion, alignment, and separation - traditionally assumes uniform desired inter-agent distances. Many practical applications demand greater flexibility, as the diversity of types and configurations grows with the popularity of multi-agent systems in society. Moreover, agents often operate without guarantees of trust or secure communication. Motivated by these challenges we update wellestablished frameworks by relaxing this assumption of shared inter-agent distances and constraints. Through a new form of constrained collective potential function, we introduce a solution that permits negotiation of these parameters. In the spirit of the traditional flocking control canon, this negotiation is achieved purely through local observations and does not require any global information or inter-agent communication. The approach is robust to semi-trust scenarios, where neighbouring agents pursue conflicting goals. We validate the effectiveness of the approach through a series of simulations.

title: FaceLiVTv2: An Improved Hybrid Architecture for Efficient Mobile Face Recognition_supp1-3690540.pdf
authors: Chi-Chia Sun
publish: N/A
abstract: Lightweight face recognition is increasingly important for deployment on edge and mobile devices, where strict constraints on latency, memory, and energy consumption must be met alongside reliable accuracy. Although recent hybrid CNN-Transformer architectures have advanced global context modeling, striking an effective balance between recognition performance and computational efficiency remains an open challenge. In this work, we present FaceLiVTv2, an improved version of our FaceLiVT hybrid architecture designed for efficient global–local feature interaction in mobile face recognition. At its core is Lite MHLA, a lightweight global token interaction module that replaces the original multi-layer attention design with multi-head linear token projections and affine rescale transformations, reducing redundancy while preserving representational diversity across heads. We further integrate Lite MHLA into a unified RepMix block that coordinates local and global feature interactions and adopts global depthwise convolution for adaptive spatial aggregation in the embedding stage. Under our experimental setup, results on LFW, CA-LFW, CP-LFW, CFP-FP, AgeDB-30, and IJB show that FaceLiVTv2 consistently improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off over existing lightweight methods. Notably, FaceLiVTv2 reduces mobile inference latency by 22% relative to FaceLiVTv1, achieves speedups of up to 30.8% over GhostFaceNets on mobile devices, and delivers 20-41% latency improvements over EdgeFace and KANFace across platforms while maintaining higher recognition accuracy. These results demonstrate that FaceLiVTv2 offers a practical and deployable solution for real-time face recognition. Code is available at https://github.com/novendrastywn/FaceLiVT.

title: Defense Energy Allocation for Networked Control Systems Under False Data Injection Attacks
authors: Xiao-Hui Liu, Guang-Hong Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: This article studies the defense energy allocation problem for networked control systems based on a Stackelberg game framework. Different from the existing works, an active false data injection (FDI) attack defense scheme is introduced, which can reduce the impact of undetectable FDI attacks on the estimator. The optimal energy allocation strategy in the active defense scheme is derived from the framework of the dynamic Stackelberg game. When the attacker's reward function is unknown, a sufficient condition for the existence of the optimal defense energy allocation strategy is derived. Under this condition, the original problem is transformed into a linear programming problem without loss of optimality. Finally, a numerical example illustrates the validity of the proposed results.

title: A Bit-Level Loosely Coupled Spiking Neural Network Accelerator With Fast Inference and Hybrid Early Termination
authors: Junchuan Gu, Yiwen Gu, Xinran Li, Haibin Shen, Kejie Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Deep spiking neural networks (SNNs) tend to suffer from long inference latency. While existing encoding schemes are insufficient, this work applies a bit-level loosely coupled (BLC) SNN in which output bits are generated sequentially from the current input bit. Consequently, the time steps can be further reduced with minor accuracy loss. The BLC SNN is optimized for hardware implementation and configured based on quantized artificial neural networks (ANNs). A hybrid early termination (ET) scheme is employed to skip redundant computation cycles without accuracy degradation. A pipelined digital accelerator architecture is designed to implement the BLC SNN, in which line buffers are utilized to maximize data reuse. Simulation results show that the proposed hybrid ET scheme reduces computation cycles by 28.31% on LeNet-5. Simulated in SMIC 28 nm technology, the accelerator consumes <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.10~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>J/img at 500 MHz with 30.49 TSOPS/W and is adaptable to 8/4 time steps of inference.

title: A New Approach to the Data-Driven Output-Based LQR Problem of Continuous-Time Linear Systems
authors: Liquan Lin, Haoyan Lin, Jie Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: A promising method for constructing a data-driven output-feedback control law involves the construction of a model-free observer. The Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) optimal control policy can then be obtained by both policy-iteration (PI) and value-iteration (VI) algorithms. However, this method requires some unknown parameterization matrix to be of full row rank and needs to solve a sequence of high dimensional linear equations for either PI or VI algorithm. In this paper, we first show that this matrix is of full row rank under the standard controllability assumption of the plant, thus removing a hurdle for applying this method. Then we further modify the existing method by defining an auxiliary system whose LQR solution will lead to a data-driven output-feedback control law. By this new method, the rank condition of the unknown parameterization matrix is not needed any more. Moreover, we derive a new sequence of linear equations for either PI or VI algorithm whose number of unknown variables is significantly less than that of the existing PI or VI algorithm, thus not only improving the computational efficiency but also relaxing the solvability conditions of the existing PI or VI algorithm. Further, since the existing PI or VI algorithm only applies to the case where some Riccati equation admits a unique positive definite solution, we prove that both the PI algorithm and the VI algorithm in the literature can be generalized to the case where the Riccati equation only admits a unique positive semi-definite solution.

title: Training-Free Vision-Language Model for Substrate Glass Defect Detection
authors: Jiaxuan Liu, Hui Zhang, Hai Wang, Bingrui Zhao, Yang Zhao, Yingtian Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Substrate glass is essential for advanced applications, such as displays and semiconductors, where microscopic defects can critically degrade yield and reliability. Automated optical inspection for this task faces a tripartite challenge: 1) severe data scarcity, stemming from industrial confidentiality and evolving defect morphologies; 2) multiillumination complexity, which requires synthesizing information from complementary bright-field and dark-field imaging; and 3) multifocal sequential dependency, arising from defects located at varying depths within the glass. Conventional supervised methods are infeasible under such data constraints, while existing zero-shot vision-language models (VLMs) are ill-equipped to handle these domain-specific imaging characteristics. To address this gap, we propose Training-Free framework for Substrate Glass Defect Detection (TFSGD). Without requiring any weight updates, TFSGD introduces a novel pipeline that first leverages illumination-specific prompt engineering and clustering-based text embedding selection to generate robust classifiers from a frozen VLM. It then fuses multiillumination information through a novel structure-sparse low-rank fusion module. Finally, it resolves focal dependency using a clarity-guided sequence refinement strategy. Extensive experiments on a real-world industrial dataset demonstrate that TFSGD establishes a new state-of-the-art in zero-shot defect segmentation and localization, offering a practical, readily deployable solution that circumvents the prohibitive costs of data collection and annotation in industrial settings.

title: Infrared and Visible Image Fusion Using Bimodal Neuron and Dynamic Receptive Field Mechanisms_supp1-3689405.pdf
authors: Shaobing Gao
publish: N/A
abstract: Infrared and visible image fusion (IVIF) significantly enhances scene interpretation by integrating broad-spectrum information. Drawing inspiration from specific snakes that possess an evolutionarily optimized bimodal sensory system capable of parallel processing infrared and visible radiation, we propose a novel IVIF framework incorporating two key elements: nonlinear cross-modal interactions across six distinct classes of snake bimodal neurons and dynamic center-surround receptive field organization. These biological principles are mathematically formalized and integrated within a deep neural network (DNN), optimized through an object detection region-guided loss and a frequency-dependent fusion loss that enable data-driven fusion strategy learning. Experimental results demonstrate that the optimized model effectively emulates the infrared-visible information integration observed in snake bimodal neurons. Critically, the nonlinear bimodal neurons capture a significantly greater amount of edge information and finer mid-to-high-frequency details, which are essential for the subsequent reconstruction of the fused image. Furthermore, a comprehensive evaluation of visual quality, encompassing both qualitative and quantitative assessments on six datasets, along with extensive object detection and semantic segmentation experiments using the fused images in both daytime and nighttime scenarios, demonstrates that our model outperforms traditional biologically-inspired IVIF algorithms, achieving performance comparable to SOTA DNN-based methods.

title: Fault-Tolerant Consensus Control for T-S Fuzzy Multiagent Systems Under Aperiodic Time-Constrained Sampling Communication and Stochastic Actuator Faults
authors: Pratap Anbalagan, Yibin Tian, Zhiguang Feng, Tingwen Huang, Yukang Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: The main objective of this study is to develop a fault-tolerant fuzzy time-constrained memory-sampled-data control (TCMSDC) mechanism for analyzing the consensus performance of nonlinear multiagent systems (MASs) affected by stochastic actuator faults and external disturbances. To achieve this, the nonlinear MASs are first converted into quasi-linear subsystems using the Takagi–Sugeno (T-S) fuzzy model. In contrast to existing memory-sampled-data consensus methods, the constructed TCMSDC signals vary over time within each sampling period, thereby enhancing consensus performance. Thereafter, a Markov variable process is applied to model multiple stochastic actuator faults in the considered MASs. Furthermore, an aperiodic-sampling-dependent asymmetric dual-looped functional (ASADLF) is constructed, which incorporates different sets of matrices designed for each sampling instance. Subsequently, a new nonorthogonal polynomial integral inequality (NOPII) is introduced to approximate the integral quadratic terms. By leveraging this ASADLF along with the proposed NOPII technique, less conservative consensus criteria are derived in the form of linear matrix inequalities (LMIs), and the TCMSDC gain parameters are obtained to guarantee mean-square asymptotic consensus with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H_{\infty }$</tex-math> </inline-formula> performance for the considered MASs. Finally, the effectiveness and advantages of the proposed TCMSDC approach in enhancing fault tolerance and achieving reliable consensus under stochastic actuator faults are validated through numerical simulations on multi-ship steering autonomous surface vehicles (SSASVs) and mass–spring systems.

title: Dynamic Voltage Sag Compensation in Smart Distribution Networks with D-STATCOM Integration
authors: Shourabh Halder, Mejbahul Islam Jim, Lam Ya Islam, Md. Shohanur Rahman, Md Mahamudur Rahaman Shamim, Md Shakil Sharker
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Power quality has become a critical concern for both electricity consumers and utilities, as issues such as voltage imbalance, waveform distortion (including DC offset, harmonics, inter harmonics, notching, and noise), voltage fluctuations, and power-frequency variations significantly affect the performance of sensitive electronic equipment. Industrial sectors relying on high-cost, precision machinery face considerable financial losses due to these disturbances. To mitigate such problems, compensating devices such as Static Synchronous Compensators (STATCOMs), Dynamic Voltage Restorers (DVRs), and Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPSs) have been developed. However, the performance of STATCOMs is limited by inherent operational constraints. This study investigates the capability of a Distribution Static Synchronous Compensator (D-STATCOM) to mitigate voltage sag through voltage regulation. A simulation model of the system was developed in PSCAD to analyze its performance under overload conditions, where excessive line currents induce voltage sag. The system's response was evaluated with and without D-STATCOM integration to assess its effectiveness in improving voltage stability.

title: Day-Ahead Scheduling of Distributed Energy Resources in Net-Zero Energy Houses with Multi-Day Operation Using Soft Actor-Critic
authors: Chao-Hsien Hsiao, Wei-Chen Lin, Wei-Tzer Huang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a reinforcement learning–based day-ahead scheduling framework for distributed energy resources in a net-zero energy house equipped with photovoltaic panels, a wind turbine, a battery energy storage system, and optional electric vehicle flexibility. The net-zero energy house is connected to a low-voltage grid (three-phase four-wire 380/220 V and single-phase three-wire 220/110 V). A soft actor–critic controller generates continuous 24-hour schedules at 15-minute intervals while enforcing converter power limits and state-of-charge constraints. A cross-day mechanism carries the terminal state-of-charge of each day into the next, ensuring physically consistent multi-day operation. The reward formulation is designed to reduce grid import and enhance the temporal matching between household load and distributed energy resource generation through netload shaping and state-of-charge-safe operation, with feasibility ensured via action projection under energy headroom constraints. The soft actor–critic policy is trained using measured 2023 operation profiles and evaluated on measured 2024 winter (January) and summer (July) data under two asset configurations: (i) a 30‑kWh battery energy storage system without electric vehicle and (ii) a 16‑kWh battery energy storage system with electric vehicle support. Across both seasons and configurations, the proposed scheduler increases the self-sufficiency rate and self-consumption rate to approximately 0.72 and reduces total grid-import energy by more than 50%, demonstrating robust month-long continuous operation suitable for practical net-zero energy house deployment. These results demonstrate the practical feasibility of soft actor–critic-based day-ahead scheduling with multi-day operation for intelligent residential energy management.

title: Malware Detection and Encryption: A Meta-Survey with a Unified Analytical Framework
authors: Aleksandra Pawlicka, Marek Pawlicki, Paweł Rajba, Rafał Kozik, Wojciech Mazurczyk, Luca Caviglione, Michał Choraś
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a meta-survey of the current state of the art in malware detection and the use of cryptographic evasion and obfuscation techniques in malicious software. Based on a corpus of over 70 selected studies, it synthesises heterogeneous survey findings via a unified analytical framework. Then, as the topic of crypto-malware is largely absent from the academic survey corpus, a narrative review of those mechanisms is provided, drawing on primary technical literature and industry threat intelligence. The paper identifies four principal findings: malware detection is increasingly framed as a representation-aware learning pipeline; robustness must be treated as a first-order qualifier of any state-of-the-art claim; the cryptographic and steganographic dimension of malware is underrepresented in the survey literature relative to its operational relevance; and persistent white spaces remain in the literature, particularly for IoT graph-based detection, Android image-based detection, and crypto/stego-aware detection pipelines.

title: STAR-RIS Aided Fluid Antenna System: A Joint Optimization with Discrete Positions and Phases
authors: Rui Shi, Haisu Wu, Hong Ren, Cunhua Pan, Jiaju Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: In this paper, we investigate a simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (STAR-RIS)-aided fluid antenna downlink communication system, where the antennas’ locations are limited to discrete positions. Specifically, the beamforming and the positions of fluid antennas at the base station (BS), the transmission and reflection coefficients of the STAR-RIS are jointly optimized to maximize the minimum signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) of the users, while satisfying the transmit power constraint at the BS and the discrete phase constraint at the STAR-RIS. To address the non-convex optimization problem, an alternating optimization (AO) algorithm is proposed. Within this framework, the non-convex SINR constraints for the BS beamforming subproblem are tackled using the successive convex approximation (SCA), while the STAR-RIS coefficients are optimized via a penalty-based method. Furthermore, to handle the discrete antenna position variables, an iterative approach based on the McCormick envelope technique is developed to find the optimal locations. Numerical results are provided to confirm the effectiveness of our proposed algorithm.

title: A Semantically-Guided Optimization Framework for Few-Shot HRRP Recognition via Vision-Language Priors
authors: Lingfeng Chen, Panhe Hu, Shuanghui Zhang, Xiangfeng Qiu, Zhen Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Few-shot Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) based on High-Resolution Range Profiles (HRRP) is crucial for non cooperative scenarios, as high-quality labeled data is scarce in practical application. Since projecting 3D targets onto 1D radar echoes inevitably introduces information loss, the feature distribution in HRRP becomes highly complex, making it difficult to learn a generalized recognition model with limited samples. In this work, a Semantically-Guided Optimization Framework (SGOF) is proposed to address this challenge by incorporating external knowledge priors. Specifically, the few-shot ATR task is first converted into a network parameter optimization process guided by a frozen Vision Language Model (VLM). Subsequently, a semantic regularized update strategy is further introduced to ensure discriminative feature extraction, by proposing the Physics-Aware Semantic Prior Generation (PSPG) and Dual-Constrained Manifold Optimization (DCMO) modules. In PSPG, Large Language Models (LLMs) are prompted to generate physics-aware semantic anchors that encode explicit electromagnetic scattering and geometric properties, regularizing the optimization landscape in DCMO. Within the DCMO framework, the Manifold Adaptation Operator (MAO) is then optimized via a dual-objective function. This function balances the semantic consistency term and the discriminative data-fidelity term, which are employed for physical prior alignment and inter-class separability enforcement, respectively. Finally, extensive simulations based on both aircraft electromagnetic simulation and measured datasets are conducted to demonstrate the superior robustness and generalization capabilities of the proposed method.

title: Extending the Measurement Range of CP-φOTDR with Two-Step Cross-Correlation Algorithm
authors: Jiangjiang Lin, Junjie Zhu, Pengbai Xu, Hua Zheng, Kunhua Wen, Xinyong Dong, Songnian Fu, Jun Yang, Yuwen Qin
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Acoustic wave amplitude variations often exceed the measurement range of chirped pulse phase-sensitive optical time-domain reflectometry (CP-φOTDR), resulting in outliers in demodulated waveforms. As the measurand increases, the cross-correlation spectrum of CP-φOTDR degrades into a noise-like pattern. When the main peak amplitude falls below that of spurious peaks induced by correlation degradation, conventional cross-correlation methods fail to search the true peak. This paper proposes a two-step cross-correlation (TCC) algorithm that employs an intermediate reference trace to bridge two adjacent Rayleigh backscattering (RBS) traces. By performing cross-correlation operations sequentially, the algorithm accurately identifies the true peak in the degraded spectrum, thereby eliminating outliers caused by spurious peaks and extending the demodulation range of CP-φOTDR. Theoretical simulations and experimental results validate that the extended demodulation range is strongly dependent on the RBS signal-to-noise ratio, with a twofold improvement verified under sufficiently high SNR. The proposed approach thus offers a practical route to enhance the measurement range performance of CP‑φOTDR, showing highly potential for applications in ocean observation, seismic monitoring, oil-gas exploration, and other fields requiring large dynamic range sensing.

title: Exponential-type variable parameter finite-time ZNN for solving joint-drift-free inverse kinematics of redundant robots
authors: Yu Zhang, Peng Chen, Xiongxiong He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper proposes an exponential-type variable parameter finite-time zeroing neural network (EVPFTZNN) that incorporates a finite-time bounded activation function (BAF). The proposed variable-parameter function exhibits exponential growth dependent on the error signal within prescribed bounds, while its boundary parameters remain fully configurable to accommodate specific application requirements. For example, most parameters are time-varying and are subject to physical constraints, meaning they cannot exceed certain maximum values. Consequently, unbounded activation functions and variable parameters that grow infinitely with time may be somewhat challenging in actual hardware implementation. The EVPFTZNN model studied in this paper is capable of circumventing this problem. Theoretical derivation proves the finite-time convergence of the EVPFTZNN and provides the analysis of its disturbance rejection capability. Furthermore, to verify the practicality of the EVPFTZNN, this paper applies it to solve the joint-drift-free inverse kinematics problem of redundant robots. Compared with several existing ZNNs, computer simulations and physical experiments validate the superior performance of the EVPFTZNN for solving joint-drift-free inverse kinematics of redundant robots.

title: A Deep Learning and IoT-Enabled Smart Healthcare with Hospital Information System Integration for Real-Time Patient Monitoring
authors: Akram Hossain, Md. Abu Sayed, Md Jubaer Rahman Tayef
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Traditional patient monitoring systems are essential in clinical practice but often lack the ability to perform real-time abnormality detection and seamless integration with hospital and radiology information systems. This study presents the design and evaluation of an IoT-enabled Smart Healthcare Monitoring System (SHMS) that enhances continuous monitoring, real-time abnormality detection, and clinical decision-making. The SHMS employs an ESP32-based hardware platform integrated with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{ECG}^{\circ}, \text{SpO}_{2}$</tex>, body temperature, and moisture sensors. Physiological data are transmitted via Wi-Fi to a cloud server and visualized through a web dashboard and mobile application. A real-time alert system notifies healthcare providers immediately of abnormal values, along with the patient's bed number and physiological parameters. To enhance real-time diagnostic capability, a hybrid CNN–BiLSTM–Attention–MLPNet (CLAMNet) model is implemented on a microcontroller for automated arrhythmia detection, following compression with TensorFlow Lite. The CLAMNet model demonstrated excellent performance, achieving 99.68% accuracy, 99.28% precision, 98.79% recall, and a 99.04 % F1-score across six heartbeat classes. The system further supports interoperability with hospital information systems (HIS), enabling comprehensive patient record management. Experimental validation confirmed high sensor accuracy, with mean errors of 2.68% for pulse rate, 0.71% for body temperature, and 0.86% for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SpO}_{2}$</tex>, while the moisture sensor achieved <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0 0 \%}$</tex> accuracy in wet/dry detection. The SHMS maintained a latency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.5-4.5$</tex> seconds from acquisition to visualization and sustained robust performance during a 48-hour stress test without data loss. By combining IoT-based monitoring with deep learning-based arrhythmia detection and HIS integration, the SHMS provides accurate, real-time monitoring and clinical insights, contributing to timely alerts, remote accessibility, and more efficient, patient-centered healthcare delivery.

title: An Autonomous Low-Cost Vision-Based Sorting System for IoT-Enabled Micro-Fulfillment
authors: Humayra Binte Monwar, Ahnaf Rashid, Nafisa Anjum Promi, Mushfiqur Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Micro-fulfillment centers are essential for facilitating quick last-mile deliveries. Subsequently, the need to position themselves closer to end consumers has led to their establishment in crowded metropolitan settings. This has meant that these centers often suffer from acute financial and spatial limitations. For many institutions, there is also a considerable automation deficit due to a heavy reliance on manual labor and the high capital costs associated with the implementation of large-scale autonomous systems. To tackle this challenge, this paper proposes a novel low-cost autonomous vision-based sorting bot. The bot leverages: (i) an intelligent vision-based technology to detect items via QR codes for accurate pick-and-place operations, (ii) an easy-to-setup and reliable line-following methodology to navigate its environment and (iii) an integrated dashboard with operational insights implemented using Blynk IoT 2.0. This design serves as a proof-of-concept for industrial automation, offering a flexible and scalable solution for high-density, low-throughput micro-fulfillment tasks.

title: Annealing-Based Approach to Solving Partial Differential Equations
authors: Kazue Kudo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Solving partial differential equations (PDEs) using an annealing-based approach involves solving generalized eigen-value problems. Discretizing a PDE yields a system of linear equations (SLE). Solving an SLE can be formulated as a general eigenvalue problem, which can be transformed into an optimization problem with an objective function given by a generalized Rayleigh quotient. The proposed algorithm requires iterative computations. However, it enables efficient annealing-based computation of eigenvectors to arbitrary precision without increasing the number of variables. Investigations using simulated annealing demonstrate how the number of iterations scales with system size and annealing time. Computational performance depends on system size, annealing time, and problem characteristics.

title: FPGA Implementation of High-Speed LDPC Decoder with Cross-Layer Scheduling for CV-QKD
authors: Hao Cheng, Weicheng Yu, Yong Chen, Genlong Chen, Yi Yang, Xue-Qin Jiang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: This paper addresses the high-throughput requirements of information reconciliation in continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD) systems by proposing an FPGA-based quasi-cyclic low-density parity-check (QC-LDPC) decoder implementation utilizing cross-layer scheduling. Through a preprocessing approach involving base matrix row reordering and data merging, the scheme effectively resolves memory access conflicts in layered decoding, enabling conflict-free parallel processing. Operating at 250 MHz, the decoder achieves a throughput of 100.5 Mbps, representing a 20% improvement over conventional approaches while maintaining equivalent frame error rate performance.

title: Application-Specific Routing Strategies in SDN-Enabled Quantum Key Distribution Networks for 6G Security
authors: Haftay Gebreslasie Abreha, Francesco Vista, Intidhar Bedhief, Seid Koudia, Symeon Chatzinotas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) is a promising solution for quantum-safe security in 6G networks, enabling information-theoretic secure key exchange. In trusted-node QKD architectures, multi-hop key relaying is required to overcome rate-distance limitations; however, relaying consumes secret keys along every traversed link, leading to key resource depletion and increased session setup latency that may violate application delay constraints. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an application-aware routing framework for SDN-enabled QKD networks that maximizes fairness in key allocation across links while satisfying application-specific delay requirements. A greedy-based heuristic algorithm is developed to efficiently solve the key relay routing problem. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach demonstrate that the proposed solution outperforms the benchmarks by 17.5% in terms of key success rate.

title: Keras-based Grammatical Evolution for Convolutional Neural Network Architecture Search
authors: Jay Patel, Andrew R. McIntyre
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: This work introduces a newly re-tooled grammatical evolution (GE) framework<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup> for exploring the space of plausible deep convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures optimized for the task of image classification. Whereas artificial neural networks typically rely on endogenic learning mechanisms through backpropagation, this study emphasizes exogenic evolutionary processes that induce structural variation in network architecture and hyperparameters. The approach targets the MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets using a flexible Backus-Naur Form (BNF) grammar that evolves convolutional networks composed of user-specified layers and parameter options, while enforcing architectural validity through the GE mapping process. Results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 are reported for short evolutionary runs of $\mathbf{1 0 - 3 0}$ generations to demonstrate the exploratory capacity of the GE system. <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>https://github.com/armcnty/pyneurgen-0.3.1-armcnty

title: Total Ionizing Dose Induced Degradation of FinFET: A Trap-Based Compact Model and EDA Solution
authors: Hengyi Liu, Chunyang Li, Yu Li, Jiajun Qiu, Yanhui Li, Tianlei Guo, Alvin Chen, Jushan Xie, Runsheng Wang, Lining Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: In this paper, a Total Ionizing Dose (TID) induced degradation simulation framework is proposed considering separate calculation of interface traps (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{N}_{\text{it }}$</tex>) and oxide traps (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{N}_{\text{ot }}$</tex>) in Shallow Trench Isolation (STI) of FinFET technologies. The different trap generation rates for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_{\text{ot}} / N_{\text{it }}$</tex> and trap saturation effect causes the rebound effect of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{V}_{\text{th }}$</tex> shift and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{G}_{\mathrm{m}}$</tex>, as the total dose increases to ultra-high levels. The proposed TID-degradation models for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{V}_{\text{th }}, \boldsymbol{I}_{\text{leak }}$</tex> and effective width change <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\Delta} \boldsymbol{W}$</tex> are calibrated and fit well with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 6 n m}$</tex> FinFET experimental data. The model is implemented into the RelXpert/Spectre environment with the unified reliability interface (URI), and circuit simulations are demonstrated. The proposed TID reliability model and EDA solution enables circuit simulations under extreme conditions with various radiation types and dynamic stress.

title: Benchmarking Encoder–Decoder and Decoder-Only Models for Extremely Low-Resource English to Myaamia Translation
authors: Yogesh Chaudhary, Suman Bhunia, Arthur Carvalho, Douglas Troy
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Languages represent irreplaceable vessels of cultural knowledge and identity. Critically endangered Indigenous languages are languages spoken by Indigenous communities that face existential threats to their survival. This paper investigates the use of neural machine translation (NMT) frameworks to support the revitalization efforts of the Myamia (Miami-Illinois) language, a critically endangered Algonquian language. In this study, we train several large language models (LLMs), including both encoderdecoder and decoder-only architectures, on English-Myaamia sentence pairs compiled from archival and dictionary sources. We compare model architectures under conditions of extreme data scarcity and evaluate the impact of post-training quantization on translation quality and on-device deployment. The paper provides a benchmark for translation data and how several LLM models perform. Robust evaluation is conducted using the SacreBLEU and Character n-gram F-score (chrF++) metrics. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the mt5_large.pte model variant, an ExecuTorch-exported multilingual encoder-decoder baseline attained the highest translation quality, achieving a SacreBLEU score of 39.67 and a chrF++ score of 80.37 for English to Myaamia translation. This result paves the way for applying machine translation (MT) systems to the revitalization of other extremely low-resource languages. Finally, we present a fully offline Android prototype, highlighting both the promise and the current limitations of LLMs for community-focused endangered language revitalization.

title: Towards Understanding the Fast Degradation of the On-State Resistance in LDMOS Devices
authors: Sven Prigann, Markus Gruetzner, Jochen Bissinger, Manuel Kollmuss, Hans Reisinger, Marc Strasser, Markus Kleinhans, Lars Bartholomäus, Thomas Basler
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: This study investigates fast hot-carrier induced degradation in LDMOS devices, resolving trapping dynamics on the sub-μs timescale. Pre-amorphization with germanium worsens <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R_{\text {on }}$</tex> degradation by nearly one order of magnitude, suggesting Ge-influenced fast trapping. Photon emission microscopy (EMMI) reveals hot-carrier generation in the drift region rather than at the classical channel hot-spot.

title: Investigation of Read-Speed Limitation and Switching Dynamics of Ferroelectric Capacitors for Capacitor-Mode Compute-in-Memory Applications
authors: Hsien-Yang Liu, Wei-Cheng Peng, Chiung-Yuan Lin, Tian-Li Wu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: This work investigates the read-voltage and speed limitations of ferroelectric capacitors (FeCAPs) in capacitor-mode compute-in-memory (CIM) applications. An NLS-model-based Monte Carlo simulation, calibrated by experimental data, was developed to analyze frequency-dependent switching behavior. The simulation reproduces measured <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{P}-\mathbf{V}$</tex> characteristics and reveals that the coercive voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{c}$</tex>) decreases sharply beyond the critical time constant <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\tau_{0} / 10\right)$</tex> due to reduced dipole participation. Furthermore, annealing temperature is identified as a key factor balancing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2} \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{r}}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\tau_{0}$</tex>, providing an effective optimization path for high-speed and reliable capacitor-mode CIM operation.

title: Explainable Ensemble Machine Learning Framework with Optimized Feature Selection for Early Detection of Alzheimer's Disease
authors: Najmus Saadat, Ashfaqul Islam, Md. Ferdous Bin Hafiz, Abdul Kadar Muhammad Masum, Khandaker Mohammad Mohi Uddin
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Alzheimer's affects millions of people, especially the elderly. Cases are supposed to be quadrupled by 2050. Early diagnosis is crucial for managing symptoms and delaying progression. Conventional diagnostic methods are expensive, time-consuming, and prone to errors. This study utilizes a Kaggle dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{2, 1 4 9}$</tex> records including <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{3 5}$</tex> features to create a powerful machine learning model to identify the early signs of Alzheimer disease. Normalization, scaling, and label encoding were applied to improve model performance. These methods provided a consistent feature contribution and converted categorical data into numerical representations for faster learning. The class distributions were balanced using EasyEnsemble, with 20 % going toward testing and 80 % toward training. Recursive feature elimination and mutual information were used to choose the top 10 features. The results of every model were rated by its F1-score, recall, accuracy, and precision. Many models were employed, including Random Forest, AdaBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, Gradient Boosting, and MLP. CatBoost achieved 96.74% accuracy, while hard voting ensembles improved to 97 %. SHAP and LIME were employed to elucidate the model's predictions. This approach removes issues with interpretability, imbalance, and inconsistent results, in contrast to previous studies. Because of its accuracy, robustness, and explainability, the proposed machine learning approach raises the standard for AD prediction.

title: Distinguishing Pilot and Co-Pilot Voices for Voice Controlled Cockpit Using Cepstral Features and Probabilistic Model
authors: Md. Rifatuddin Romel, M M Tasin Ferdaous Galib
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: With the evolution of voice recognition technology, it is now high time to implement voice controlled cockpits in modern aircraft. Also, in high-stakes cockpit environments, clear and accurate communication between flight crew members and taking decisions in opposite inputs from pilot and crew members is critical. This research presents a novel approach to real-time speaker recognition in aircraft cockpits by distinguishing between pilot and co-pilot voices using voice biometric techniques and also proposes a novel architecture for the voice-controlled cockpit integrating various avionics modules. The proposed system explores and compares multiple feature extraction methods—Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC), Gammatone Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (GFCC), and Perceptual Linear Prediction (PLP)—in combination with classification models such as Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) and Vector Quantization (VQ). Experiments were conducted using the Free Spoken Digit Dataset (FSDD), representing six different speakers with 2400 training and 600 testing samples. Results demonstrate that MFCC combined with GMM achieves the highest classification accuracy of 98.17%, significantly outperforming GFCC and PLP features. This paper thus shows the feasibility of the use of MFCC and GMM system to support incident investigations, improve crew monitoring systems, and enhance flight safety by detecting cross-communication errors in real time by implementing real time voice controlled cockpit. This can act as a baseline for other feature sets or classification processes in the future.

title: When Digital Twins Meet Large Language Models: Realistic, Interactive, and Editable Simulation for Autonomous Driving_supp1-3683252.mp4
authors: Tanmay Samak
publish: N/A
abstract: Simulation frameworks have been key enablers for the development and validation of autonomous driving systems. However, existing methods struggle to comprehensively address the autonomy-oriented requirements of balancing 1) dynamical fidelity, 2) photorealistic rendering, 3) context-relevant scenario orchestration, and 4) real-time performance. To address these limitations, we present a unified framework for creating and curating high-fidelity digital twins to accelerate advancements in autonomous driving research. Our framework leverages a mix of physics-based and data-driven techniques for developing and simulating digital twins of autonomous vehicles and their operating environments. It is capable of reconstructing real-world scenes and assets with geometric and photorealistic accuracy (∼97% structural similarity) and infusing them with physical properties to enable real-time (>60-Hz) dynamical simulation of the ensuing driving scenarios. Additionally, it incorporates a large language model (LLM) interface to flexibly edit the driving scenarios online via natural language prompts, with ∼85% generalizability and ∼95% repeatability. Finally, an optional vision language model (VLM) provides ∼80% visual enhancement by blending the hybrid scene composition.

title: Research on Large Language Model Knowledge Question Answering System for Power Grid Dispatching and Control Field
authors: Run Lin, Haoran Li, Jingjie Bai, Yidan Ren, Zhongshan Qu, Zhili Yang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Driven by global energy transition and digital transformation, the complexity of power grid dispatching and control has increased sharply, making traditional rule-based question answering systems incompetent for professional accurate decision-making. Focusing on the core pain points of this field such as strong data professionalism, high real-time requirements, complex business scenarios, this paper researches and implements a large language model (LLM)-based knowledge question answering system. We first construct a multi-source heterogeneous data processing system and domain knowledge base, realizing efficient knowledge representation and retrieval via the BERT-MIL model and Milvus vector database. Then, based on the 20B power-specialized State Grid “Guangming LLM”, we optimize its domain adaptability through knowledge distillation and contrastive learning, and design a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture. Finally, a three-level technical framework (data-model-application) and a closed-loop question answering system are built. Experimental results show the system achieves 92% accuracy, 2.3s average response time, and over 90% knowledge coverage, significantly improving dispatching decision efficiency and providing technical support for intelligent upgrading of power grid dispatching and control in new power systems.

title: Coordinated Optimal Dispatch Strategy of Cascaded Hydro-Wind-Solar-Pumped Storage Multi-Resources for Enhancing Distribution Network Resilience
authors: Kaizhi Xiong, Jiancun Liu, Chengbo Du, Ting Liang, Jiangbei Li, Xiangyu Kong
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: In recent years, distribution networks with high renewable penetration face severe resilience challenges under extreme weather due to output stochasticity and low inertia. This paper proposes a resilience enhancement method for coordinated cascaded hydro-wind-solar-pumped storage systems, tailored for a typical basin-level integrated hydro-wind-solar base scenario. First, a Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) model is constructed based on a “Prevention-Restoration” multi-stage framework, incorporating hydraulic coupling and pumped storage constraints. Second, a proactive defense strategy is introduced to reserve regulation margins and form self-healing islands by leveraging resource complementarity and system spinning reserve constraints. Simulation results on an improved IEEE 33-node system demonstrate that the proposed strategy effectively provides multi-timescale energy support, minimizes critical load shedding, and enhances system resilience.

title: Explaining 3D CNN Decisions for Alzheimer’s MRI Classification: A SHAP-Based Analysis of Preprocessing Bias
authors: Hassen Gadacha, Lydia Bouzar-Benlabiod, Andrew R. McIntyre
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: This paper presents an explainable 3D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework for early detection of Alzheimer’s disease that addresses critical gaps in model interpretability for clinical deployment. Using T1-weighted MRI scans from the Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset, we developed binary classification models for Early Mild Cognitive Impairment (EMCI) detection against both Cognitively Normal (CN) and Maintenance of Cognitive Normal (MTCN) subjects. Our approach demonstrates that preprocessing consistency between background and test datasets is crucial for reliable SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) interpretability. The EMCI vs CN model achieved $98.10 \%$ accuracy, while the preprocessing-consistent EMCI vs MTCN comparison yielded $78.44 \%$ accuracy but produced significantly more reliable explanations. This work establishes preprocessing consistency as a fundamental requirement for explainable AI in medical imaging, providing practical guidelines for deploying transparent diagnostic systems that balance predictive performance with clinically trustworthy interpretations essential for early Alzheimer’s intervention.

title: Reliability Assurance Risks from BTI Recovery Effects
authors: Narendra Parihar, Stephen M. Ramey, Jeffrey Hicks, Souvik Mahapatra
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: Circuit aging simulations face the challenge of correctly accounting for BTI recovery effects in the context of various AC and DC workloads. Many previous studies have shown the cyclical nature of BTI aging with the application of intermittent periods of DC stress. However, scant attention has been paid to the aging behavior for more realistic mission profiles comprised of alternating periods of AC and DC stress. Furthermore, applying the transistor-level understanding of aging behavior for realistic, complex waveforms to a practical simulation approach has not been well addressed in the literature, leading to possible underestimation of circuit-level aging. This work addresses this gap by quantifying transistor aging behavior under realistic conditions, then merges this understanding with circuit-level evaluations, and ultimately concludes that NBTI recovery benefits should not be universally assumed to apply to circuit aging reliability assessments.

title: Metal Interconnects for Electromigration Suppression Inspired by Tesla Valve
authors: K. Sasagawa, T. Tomabechi, K. Fujisaki, K. Miura
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: In this study, we designed a novel interconnect geometry inspired by the Tesla-valve structure and investigated its effectiveness against electromigration (EM) damage through accelerated current loading tests. Al/Cr interconnects with the Tesla-valve-inspired geometry and conventional straight interconnects were fabricated by conventional photolithography under identical conditions, and accelerated current tests were conducted at current densities of 0.10, 0.12, and 0.14 MA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, a substrate temperature of 573 K, and a current loading time of 8 hours. As a result, the drift velocities in the Tesla-valve-inspired interconnects were lower than those of the straight interconnects under all conditions. In addition, threshold current density analysis showed that the Tesla-valve-inspired interconnects had higher threshold current densities than the straight interconnects. These results indicated that the proposed interconnect geometry effectively suppressed EM-induced damage.

title: Towards Adaptive and Energy-Aware Task Offloading in Edge–Cloud Environments
authors: Muhammad Minhajuddin, Faria Khandaker, A B M Bodrul Alam
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Task offloading strategies must evolve to meet the growing demand for energy efficiency and low-latency services in edge-cloud computing environments. This paper proposes an Energy-Aware Dynamic Offloading Algorithm (EDOA), an adaptive strategy for dynamic task offloading that jointly considers system resource availability, delay sensitivity, and energy consumption at the edge device. An energy-time threshold parameter is introduced to enable adaptive decision-making, allowing the system to prioritize either energy conservation or service latency based on current workload and network conditions. Although prior research works have effectively addressed latency optimization, resource management, and cost efficiency in task offloading, the joint consideration of energy consumption, delay sensitivity, and system resources remains insufficiently explored. To address this gap, the proposed EDOA dynamically balances energy savings and latency by optimizing task execution decisions between edge and cloud nodes. For performance evaluation, EDOA is compared with three benchmark task offloading strategies under varying network conditions. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves superior performance compared to the benchmark schemes in terms of service time and task failure rates, while maintaining lower energy consumption. Overall, the performance analysis reveals that jointly optimizing energy efficiency, latency, and resource utilization enhances system adaptability and operational performance in edge-cloud computing environments.

title: EMBC Special Issue: CogniFuse and Multimodal Deformers: An Extended Study on Benchmarking and Modeling Biosignal Fusion
authors: Anthony Richardson, Michael Beetz, Tanja Schultz, Felix Putze
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Human physiological signals reflect complex biological processes and provide important insights into physical and mental states. Extracting such information from biosignal data collected during everyday activities holds great potential for real-time monitoring of physical and mental states, but is challenging due to noise and artifacts. To address this, we introduce CogniFuse, the first publicly available multi-task benchmark for multimodal biosignal fusion in such unconstrained environments. In addition, we develop a comprehensive benchmarking pipeline that emphasizes comparability, reproducibility, accessibility, and usability, while demonstrating robustness across architectures, tasks, and model sizes. For many biosignals, particularly electrophysiological signals, information in different frequency bands is critical for assessing physiological states. Motivated by this, we propose a family of Multimodal Deformer models that capture multi-level power features along with both long- and short-term temporal dependencies across multiple biosignal modalities. In particular, our Multi-Channel Deformer achieves the highest average benchmark score, outperforming all models of comparison. By advancing multimodal biosignal fusion in everyday settings, this work supports real-time monitoring of physical and mental states outside highly controlled clinical conditions. To ensure full transparency and reproducibility, and to facilitate future research, all code and data are made publicly available.

title: Low-Frequency Shock Dynamics in a Self-Gravitating Opposite-Polarity Complex Plasmas
authors: L. Rahman, S. Sultana, A. Mannan, A. A. Mamun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: An investigation on the nonlinear dynamics of gravito-electrostatic dust-acoustic shock waves (DAShWs) propagating in a dusty plasma medium containing inertial positive and negative dust, as well as inertialess Maxwellian ions and electrons, has been carried out, incorporating external magnetic-field and dust-temperature effects. Adopting the reductive perturbation method, the Korteweg–de Vries–Burgers (KdVB) equation is derived to describe the nonlinear evolution of DAShWs. It is observed that the system supports stable propagation of dust-acoustic waves (DAWs) under gravity. The basic properties of DAShWs are found to be significantly affected by intrinsic plasma parameters, including the positive-to-negative dust charge, positive-to-negative dust density, negative-to-positive dust mass, dust temperatures, and the magnetic field, among others. A comparative analysis, considering and neglecting the self-gravitational field, is also presented to demonstrate the extent to which shock structures are modified by self-gravity. The results reported herein have potential implications for the characterization of the gravito-electrostatic dynamics associated with DAWs in laboratory plasmas and astrophysical environments (e.g., planetary rings and interstellar clouds).

title: Pinning Model of Wake-Up and Fatigue in Hf-Based Ferroelectrics
authors: B. Truijen, Y. Higashi, B. Kaczer, B. O'Sullivan, P.J. Roussel, A. Chasin, J. Bizindavyi, S. Mukherjee, M. Popovici, N. Ronchi, M. Rosmeulen, A. Belmonte, J. Van Houdt
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: We provide evidence of domain pinning as the main contribution to early fatigue, i.e. the loss of remnant polarization (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P_R$</tex>) with field cycling in ferroelectric (FE) La-doped hafnium zirconium oxide capacitors. Additionally, depinning is strongly linked to wake-up, the initial increase of P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">R</inf> with cycling, and fatigue recovery. Deeper understanding of (de)pinning is crucial for tackling reliability questions related to FE endurance. We provide an empirical model capable of capturing the P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">R</inf> changes with cycling due to (de)pinning. The model accurately describes the evolution of the full polarization-field loop with a single mechanism. It is calibrated and verified for a wide range of cycling fields and number of cycles and provides better extrapolations than the common linear one.

title: Fixed-Time State Observer and Curvature Guidance for Robust Path Tracking of High-Speed USV
authors: Long Si, Weiting Liu, Xiaoqiang Dai, Jinxing Liu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: Path tracking of high-speed unmanned surface vessel (USV) is critical for military interception, emergency search and rescue, and other marine applications. However, USV often suffer from trajectory deviation and tracking delay during highspeed navigation, which degrades their operational performance significantly. To resolve these critical issues, Fixed-time State Observer - Curvature-adaptive Dynamic Forward Line-of-Sight - Adaptive Robust Path Tracking Controller(FxTSO-CDFLOS-ARPTC) is proposed in this study. Specifically, the FxTSO is designed to to achieve accurate estimation of unknown timevarying large drift angles, CDFLOS method is correlated with the tracking error. Additionally, an adaptive robust path tracking controller is designed, which resolves the “computational explosion” problem and mitigates the adverse impact of actuator constraints on system stability. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed FxTSO-CDFLOS-ARPTC achieves a faster convergence speed. It can improve the overall tracking accuracy by about 38.4 %, thus providing a reliable control approach for high-speed USV path tracking.

title: A Theory for Systems Engineering
authors: Haifeng Zhu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Systems engineering (SE) has long been developed and used in industry. However, the quantitative aspect of its process has not been well established, which makes the SE process largely remain in the manual management domain and sometimes ad hoc, qualitative and experience-based. Attempts at quantitative modeling were made in different aspects but were not successful with the whole system process. This paper proposes a way to model the SE process with control theory, solving this theoretical problem. This enables a lot of analysis, management and quality control capabilities and makes the SE a discipline that can be quantitatively reasoned, without which a scientific discipline cannot be considered mature. We further model and analyze adaptable systems engineering with this theory.

title: A Hetero-functional Network Minimum Cost Flow Formulation to Resource Constrained Project Scheduling
authors: Amirreza Hosseini, Amro M. Farid
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: In engineering management, project scheduling is an indispensable function in the planning, monitoring, and control of projects. The presence of scarce resources and precedence relationships among activities makes project scheduling inherently complex. Although the resource-constrained project scheduling problem (RCPSP) lies at the core of project management, it remains largely disconnected from the broader literature on model-based systems engineering (MBSE), thereby limiting its integration into the design and management of enterprises as complex systems. This paper specializes the hetero-functional network minimum-cost flow (HFNMCF) formulation to the RCPSP context. Using an illustrative instance with renewable resources, the specialized HFNMCF formulation reproduces identical schedules to those of the resourceconstrained project scheduling problem while providing additional explicit insight into project states, facilitating more precise project monitoring and control. Therefore, these results show that model-based systems engineering and hetero-functional graph theory (HFGT) are a formal generalization of the RCPSP that are amenable to systematic extension through comprehensive taxonomies, enabling the incorporation of a wide range of real-world conditions and project scenarios. Overall, the framework preserves the strengths of the classical RCPSP while accommodating realworld constraints and enterprise-level decision processes encountered in large, complex megaprojects.

title: Parallel Multi-Circuit Quantum Feature Fusion in Hybrid Quantum-Classical Convolutional Neural Networks for Breast Tumor Classification
authors: Ece Yurtseven
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum machine learning has emerged as a promising approach to improve feature extraction and classification tasks in high-dimensional data domains such as medical imaging. In this work, we present a hybrid Quantum-Classical Convolutional Neural Network (QCNN) architecture designed for the binary classification of the BreastMNIST dataset, a standardized benchmark for distinguishing between benign and malignant breast tumors. Our architecture integrates classical convolutional feature extraction with two distinct quantum circuits: an amplitude-encoding variational quantum circuit (VQC) and an angle-encoding VQC circuit with circular entanglement, both implemented on four qubits. These circuits generate quantum feature embeddings that are fused with classical features to form a joint feature space, which is subsequently processed by a fully connected classifier. To ensure fairness, the hybrid QCNN is parameter-matched against a baseline classical CNN, allowing us to isolate the contribution of quantum layers. Both models are trained under identical conditions using the Adam optimizer and binary cross-entropy loss. Experimental evaluation in five independent runs demonstrates that the hybrid QCNN achieves statistically significant improvements in classification accuracy compared to the classical CNN, as validated by a one-sided Wilcoxon signed rank test (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}=0.03125$</tex>) and supported by large effect size of Cohen's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{d} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{2. 1 4}$</tex>. Our results indicate that hybrid QCNN architectures can leverage entanglement and quantum feature fusion to enhance medical image classification tasks. This work establishes a statistical validation framework for assessing hybrid quantum models in biomedical applications and highlights pathways for scaling to larger datasets and deployment on near-term quantum hardware.

title: Design and Optimization of a Distributed Low-Visibility Mesh Network in Contested Environments
authors: Logan Dosan, Ryan Monagle, Karthik Kottapalli, Ethan Slick, Benjamin Lynch, Vikram Mittal
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Modern field teams operating in contested electromagnetic environments require low-profile, energyefficient computing and communication nodes to maintain situational awareness and support local network operations. Traditional high-power radios and centralized command links are effective but often compromise stealth, consume excessive energy, are vulnerable to physical tampering, or become degraded under jamming. This practitioner paper presents the design and prototyping of a modular, low-visibility network of edge computing nodes intended for cyber sensing and local network interaction in bandwidth-denied or spectrum-constrained environments. The system integrates a single-board computer platform with a multi-layer communication architecture that includes a cellular uplink and long-range low-power radio mesh. To save power and reduce hardware costs, we separate the communication module from the main computing load, using simple microcontroller relays alongside more capable edge processors. Secure peer-to-peer communication is enabled through an open-source cryptographic routing protocol. In parallel, we present a modeling approach for node placement that frames coverage, connectivity, energy efficiency, and physical/radio-frequency exposure as a constrained set-cover and graph-connectivity optimization problem. Experimentation will include evaluating performance across open, wooded, and urban terrain and validating assumptions about communication reliability and stealth. The research demonstrates a scalable approach for resilient, low-profile cyber and sensing networks for field deployment.

title: MPS-JuliQAOA: User-Friendly, Scalable MPS-Based Simulation for Quantum Optimization
authors: Sean Feeney, Reuben Tate, John Golden, Stephan Eidenbenz
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We present MPS-JuliQAOA, an open-source, Julianative framework for simulating the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) using Matrix Product States (MPS). The tool supports general cost Hamiltonians that are diagonal in the computational basis and integrates tensornetwork simulation with automatic differentiation for parameter optimization within a unified workflow. Built on the ITensor ecosystem, MPS-JuliQAOA replaces exact statevector simulation with an MPS backend, enabling QAOA simulations beyond the practical limits of full statevector methods when entanglement growth remains controlled. We demonstrate simulations of up to 512 qubits and 20 QAOA layers on 3-regular Max-Cut instances, and analyze runtime scaling and approximation accuracy as functions of bond dimension and circuit depth. The framework abstracts tensor-network and differentiation details from the user, allowing high-level Hamiltonian specification without requiring expertise in MPS methods. Source code is publicly available at https://github.com/lanl/JuliQAOA.jl/tree/mps.

title: Efficient Quantum Generic Key-Recovery Attack on Feistel Ciphers
authors: Kun Zhang, Tao Shang, Yuanjing Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The development of post-quantum cryptography requires cryptographic primitives that remain secure against quantum adversaries. Quantum key-recovery attacks constitute a fundamental class of quantum cryptanalytic techniques, and the resistance of a cryptosystem to such attacks serves as an important indicator of its overall security. Since the Feistel structure is a core component in the design of many block ciphers, efficient quantum key-recovery attacks on Feistel ciphers provide valuable insights into evaluating and strengthening their security. In this paper, we propose an efficient generic quantum key-recovery attack on Feistel ciphers. Our attack integrates universal hash families with Simon's and Grover's algorithms to perform key-recovery. We rigorously analyze the correctness of the proposed attack and derive its time and quantum resource complexities. A comprehensive comparison with existing keyrecovery attacks shows that the proposed approach improves both quantum resource usage and dominant query complexity, and therefore yields a more efficient quantum key-recovery attack. Our work enhances the understanding of Feistel ciphers under quantum threats and informs the design of robust cryptosystems for the post-quantum era.

title: Turbo Equalization for Thermal States Based Wireless Quantum Optical Multipath Communications Using Polar Codes
authors: Peter Jung, Kushtrim Dini
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Light emitting diodes (LEDs), which likely emit thermal states, are easy to implement alternatives to the usually taken lasers when deployed at quantum transmitters of wireless quantum optical communications. However, using LEDs leads to increased bit error and block error ratios in the said wireless quantum optical communication concepts. These increases of the error ratios can be mitigated by applying sophisticated channel coding, e.g. polar coding, which has been included in the Fifth Generation New Radio (5 G/NR) standard. In addition, applying turbo equalization, proposed for classical communications in the 1990s, seems attractive when striving for improved robustness of wireless quantum optical communications. In this manuscript the recently emerged laser based polar coded wireless quantum optical communication concept with turbo equalization, which has been applied to multipath reception scenarios, will be extended to the case of LED based polar coded wireless quantum optical multipath communications. We will demonstrate the viability of the proposed concept with selected simulation results.

title: Message from the Technical Program Chairs: QCNC 2026
authors: N/A
