About 29 million U.S. adults approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx \text{1 4 \%}$</tex> of the total population suffer from cholesterol issues and this indicate that the high demand of alternative screening testing method. To address these major issue we proposed non-invasive method by using ML models to predict the cholesterol level by using method using the NHANES survey data as an input. The data of patient include demographics (age, sex and race/ethnicity), lifestyle factors (dietary intake, physical activity and smoking), anthropometric measures (BMI, waist circumference, blood pressure) and self-reported health history (diabetes, hypertension). The digital data outcome is defined by clinical thresholds (total cholesterol <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geqslant 240 \text{mg} / \text{dL}$</tex> or <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{LDL} \geqslant 160 \text{mg} / \text{dL}$</tex>). The data preprocessing involves inputting missing values, encoding categorical variables and scaling numeric features. In first stage we train five classifiers (logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, SVM, k-NN) using stratified 5 -fold cross-validation, tuning hyperparameters. The performance was tested based on accuracy, sensitivity, specificity and area under the ROC curve (AUC). The results outline that XGBoost model shows the highest discrimination (AUC/ROC ~ 0.85) with high sensitivity, outperforming logistic regression (AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 0.68$</tex>) as compared to others. The main data related to predictors included age, BMI, blood pressure, and diet quality, consistent with known risk factors. These results shows that by using survey data meaningfully stratify cholesterol risk can be reduce. Finally, we outline the public health implications based on a non- invasive ML-based screening tool for testing and improving preventive care.

title: Class-Wise Streaming for Detecting Minority Class Drift in Machine Learning Classifiers
authors: Dileesha Kannangara, Uthayasanker Thayasivam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Timely and effective detection of concept drift is essential for maintaining machine learning model performance in non-stationary environments. Among these models, classification systems are the most prevalent, and they often face a unique challenge: class imbalance. Typically, the minority class is not only underrepresented but also the most critical to predict accurately, as misclassifications of the minority class can lead to severe consequences.For example, in cancer detection, malignant cases are both rare and vital to identify. Therefore, it is crucial to pay special attention to drifts affecting the minority class. Despite this, most existing drift detection methods overlook changes affecting only the minority class, and this challenge remains largely underexplored in current research. To address this gap, we propose a heuristic methodology that can be applied to any existing concept drift detector through class-wise streaming.Our experiments show that in 84.26% of all evaluations, our method either outperformed or matched the performance of the conventional approach.Through this, we offer an important initial step toward a practical approach for improving the efficiency of drift detection, particularly when the drift occurs in the minority class.

title: An LLM-Based Framework for Generating Synthetic Legal Contract Benchmark Datasets: A LowResource Language Case Study
authors: Zofran Aqsha, Nur Hanis Sabrina Suhaimi, Said Ah Saad, Muhamad Helmi Md Said, Muhammad Harieth Zaini, Afrina Rosa Mohamad Sattar, Maisarah Mustaffa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: The advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) in low-resource legal domains is impeded by the scarcity of high-fidelity benchmarks required for rigorous evaluation. While generic web-scraped datasets exist, the reasoning density and domain-specific accuracy required for high-stakes legal drafting are often lacking. To address this, a novel Human-in-theLoop (HITL) framework for constructing high-fidelity legal benchmark datasets is presented, utilizing a case study on Malay Tenancy Contracts. A methodology similar to process supervision is employed via directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to codify expertdefined contract drafting workflows. Subsequently, a state-of-theart (SOTA) LLM iteratively amends a legal contract template according to these workflows, with the outputted clauses validated by legal practitioners at every intermediate DAG node. The efficacy of this framework is demonstrated through the construction of a pilot benchmark, MaLe-400, consisting of 400 synthetically generated, expert-verified Malay Tenancy Contracts. Preliminary analysis using Google Gemini 2.5 indicates that this approach demonstrates SOTA LLM reliability in producing Malay legal clauses while systematically capturing localized legal nuances often missed by SOTA models, thereby establishing a reproducible methodology for creating gold-standard benchmarks in resource-constrained legal domains.

title: Trustworthy Smishing Detection in Code-Mixed Bangla-English SMS: Dual-View XLM-R With Calibration, Selective Classification, and Open-Set Detection
authors: Ahammad Hossen Eazaz, Md Intishar Ahmed, Golam Md. Muradul Bashir
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The rise of mobile phishing via SMS (smishing) has become a pressing cybersecurity concern, particularly in multilingual regions like South Asia where messages frequently appear in Bangla, English, or their noisy code-mixed forms. Traditional models often deliver high accuracy in controlled settings but fail when confronted with real-world challenges such as spelling variations, transliteration, distributional shifts, or out-of-distribution (OOD) attacks. In this work, we present a dualview XLM-R framework that jointly learns from both original and romanized Bangla SMS, enforcing prediction consistency across views to build resilience against orthographic variation. To enhance trustworthiness, we incorporate probability calibration through temperature scaling, evaluate selective classification with risk-coverage analysis, and benchmark multiple OOD detection strategies including Energy, MSP, MC-Dropout, and Mahalanobis distance. Experiments on a newly curated Bangla-English code-mixed dataset show that our model achieves over 99.06 % accuracy, near-perfect calibration (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E C E \approx 0.005$</tex>), and reliable selective abstention. While Energy and MSP struggle under OOD conditions, Mahalanobis distance reaches AUROC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.996$</tex>, offering strong open-set robustness. These findings highlight not only superior performance over strong baselines but also improved reliability and transparency, positioning our approach as a practical and trustworthy solution for smishing defense in low-resource, multilingual contexts.

title: URes2Net: U-Net Architecture with Res2Net for Skin Lesion Segmentation
authors: S¸Eyma Karagozoglu, Fırat Hardalac
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: In this paper, we present URes2Net, a novel and efficient architecture developed for medical image segmentation. The architecture of our model is based on a U-shaped network, in which both the encoder and the decoder part are improved by a proposed residual block, called Res2Block. Each Res2Block consists of a one-stage encoder-decoder pair to capture multiscale contextual information. Furthermore, the backbone of each Res2Block is built upon Res2Net, which enables hierarchical residual connections within the block. This design allows the network to effectively capture local and global contextual information while extracting features at different scales. URes2Net was evaluated on the ISIC-2017 skin lesion segmentation dataset. Quantitative results demonstrate that our model achieves a Dice loss of 0.819, MAE of 0.084, and a maximum F-measure of 0.897, which clearly outperforms comparable U-shaped architectures. Qualitative results show that the predicted probability maps align closely with ground truth masks across lesions of varying sizes. Thus, the network preserves fine details while accurately segmenting complex structures. By integrating multi-scale contextual information through hierarchical residual connections, the network demonstrates good results in detecting lesions with diverse shapes and sizes. The proposed model can also be extended to other biomedical image segmentation tasks, making it a promising solution for real-world clinical applications. The source code is available: https://github.com/sym-codes/URes2Net.

title: Code Smell Detection Framework Using Self Attention CNN: An Explainable Feature Selection Approach
authors: Fayzul Noor Tabeeb, Kazi Nusrat Jahan Suchi, Dipto Das, Nazneen Akhter
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Code smell detection is essential for preserving software quality and ensuring long-term maintainability. However, most existing approaches either struggle with high computational costs or fail to achieve both accuracy and interpretability simultaneously which leaves a gap in building efficient and transparent models. To address this gap, this study introduces a hybrid model that combines feature selection with an attention-based convolutional neural network (CNN) to enhance the accuracy, efficiency, and interpretability of multiple code smell classification. The feature selection module filters out irrelevant and redundant attributes that leads to a substantial reduction in computational time; for instance, the average detection time for God Class reduced from 4 m 26.9 s to 2 m 42.8 s, and for Long Method from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{5 m} 21 ~\mathrm{s}$</tex> to 2 m 56.4 s. The attention mechanism further strengthens the model's ability to prioritize significant patterns within the code representations, boosting key metrics such as precision, recall, and F -measure, which reached up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.975,0.990$</tex>, and 0.983, respectively for Long Method Code Smell Detection. In addition to performance gains, the model incorporates LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) for visualizing feature importance which provides transparent insights into the internal decision-making processes of the classifier. The proposed approach demonstrates promising potential for real-world integration, and future work may extend this framework to multi-label smell detection, support for multiple programming languages, and real-time deployment in intelligent development environments for proactive, context-aware feedback.

title: Transformer-Assisted Recommendation System with Apriori-Enhanced Behavior Mining in Decision Models for Engineering
authors: Mariyam Ahmed, Jainish Roy
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Recommender systems still have a long way to go before they can accurately and consistently predict users’ future preferences. To address these challenges, this study proposes a Transformer-Assisted Recommendation System based on Association and Enhanced Behavior Modeling (TARSAEBM). The core objective of this approach is to integrate deep learning transformers with association rule mining to better capture and interpret complex user behavior patterns and dynamic preferences. By leveraging the sequential modeling power of transformers and the relational insights of association rules, TARS-AEBM enhances the understanding of contextual dependencies and item correlations. Experimental results demonstrate that TARS-AEBM significantly outperforms traditional recommendation methods in terms of F1-score, recall, and accuracy, proving its effectiveness in providing more personalized, adaptive, and precise recommendations across diverse user environments in decision model.

title: Physics–AI-Driven Design of GaN P-MISFETs with InGaN/AlN/AlGaN Heterostructures
authors: Linkun Sun, Zhehao Hu, Qiyi Guo, Bowen Li, Kain Lu Low, Wen Liu
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid physics–AI framework for the design of CMOS-compatible p-type GaN MISFETs based on a polarization-engineered InGaN/AlN/AlGaN/AlN/GaN heterostructure. The workflow combines TCAD simulations, design of experiments (DOE), clustering analysis, and SHAP-informed feature evaluation to identify key design parameters and trade-offs. Results reveal that recess depth is the dominant factor, improving subthreshold swing (SS) and boosting I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</inf> but shifting V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> negatively. In contrast, an AlN interlayer and higher Al/In compositions raise V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> via stronger polarization but degrade transport through reduced hole velocity and screening. SHAP analysis confirms these trade-offs, underscoring the need for co-optimization of recess depth, material composition, and interlayer design to balance I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</inf>, SS, and V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> in GaN CMOS technology.

title: Robust Epileptic Seizure Detection via Multi-Artifact EEG Denoising and SE-CNN
authors: Tanvir Ahsan Showrov, Md. Julkar Nain Siam, Najmus Shakif Ayaan, Md. Sakir Hossain, S.M. Sadakatul Bari
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Epilepsy is a persistent neurological disorder distinguished by recurrent seizure episodes, making prompt detection crucial for treatment and preventing injuries. Machine Learning (ML) models trained on electroencephalogram (EEG) signals have great promise for automating seizure detection, but their reliability is seriously hampered by artifacts introduced during EEG acquisition. Despite significant research on artifact removal, the challenge of addressing a wide variety of artifacts simultaneously remains unexplored. In this paper, seven different types of realistic motion and environmental artifacts are considered to investigate their impacts on the performance of epilepsy seizure detection. We leverage wavelet decomposition, ensemble empirical mode decomposition (EEMD), and a hybrid FFT Masking + EEMD method for artifact suppression. The squeeze-and-excitation convolutional neural network (SE-CNN) is exploited as a classifier. The results demonstrate that motion noise notably degrades the classification accuracy of the seizure detection model, reducing it from 98.75 % (clean signals) to 88.75 % (contaminated signals). The EEMD methods provide the most consistent improvements by preserving crucial EEG features, achieving an accuracy of 96.25 %, which is a 7.5 % improvement compared to the contaminated case. Finally, the proposed SE-CNN model achieves an AUC of more than 99 %, which is 0.27 % and 2.95 % higher than the temporal convolutional network (TCN) and random forest (RF), respectively.

title: Bengali Cyberbullying Detection in Social Media Comments Using Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Md. Abdul Hai, Sultana Jahan Soheli, Md. Tanvir Ahmed, Abu Rayhan, Ashikur Rahman Khan, Md Mahbubul Alam, M. Shamim Kaiser
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Facebook and Twitter are the most popular social media sites where cyberbullying is common. It is defined as the exploitation of digital communication to harass, intimidate, or degrade people, and this may cause serious psychological and social harm. Cyberbullying can occur at any time and is more difficult to avoid as opposed to traditional bullying. In this paper, we aim to identify cyberbullying in Bengali social media comments, a lowresource language, and to detect abusive content in depth. To address the problem, we trained and evaluated five models: Bangla-BERT, m-BERT, LSTM, Bi-LSTM, and SVM with a labelled dataset of Bengali social media comments. The models were trained to label the comments as abusive and non-abusive. The models were trained to classify comments as abusive or non-abusive. Experimental results show that Bangla-BERT achieved the highest accuracy (94 %), followed by m-BERT (91 %), Bi-LSTM (89 %), LSTM (87 %), and SVM (76 %). Although the Bangla-BERT is better than the other models, it can still be improved with bigger and more varied datasets. The study illustrates the usefulness of machine learning in cyberbullying detection in Bengali and also leads to safer online communication, although it also indicates future directions of low-resource language processing.

title: Design of an Ultra-Wideband Plane Wave Generator with High Aperture Efficiency for 1–10 GHz
authors: Xiaolong Wang, Weimin Wang, Le Yu, Zhangzhang Jiang, Wei Fan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Antenna Technology (iWAT)
abstract: This paper addresses the challenges of highfrequency beam narrowing and strong mutual coupling at low frequencies in a 1–10 GHz ultra-wideband plane wave generator (PWG) system with Vivaldi antennas. An array integration method based on the orthogonal arrangement of high- and lowfrequency tapered slot antenna (TSA) units is proposed. The method employs low-frequency (1–4 GHz) and high-frequency (4–10 GHz) TSA elements to cover different sub-bands, and combines CST full-wave simulation to extract the transfer function under actual coupling conditions for excitation optimization. This approach achieves wide beam radiation and high-quality quiet zone synthesis across the entire frequency range. The final prototype demonstrates that the PWG achieves a quiet zone utilization efficiency of 80% (a quiet zone (QZ) of 720 mm from a 900 mm aperture), with field perturbations limited to less than 1 dB in amplitude and 10° in phase.

title: A Multi-View Feature Fusion Approach with XLM-Roberta for Spam Email Detection
authors: Bipasha Majumder, Antu Chowdhury, A R M Mahamudul Hasan, Didarul Islam Didar, Bashirul Islam, Shekh Maruf
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Spam and phishing emails remain significant problems in email security and consumer privacy. This article proposes a spam classification model using XLM RoBERTa transformer and a Multi-View Feature Fusion approach to combine CLS token, mean-pooled, and attention-weighted embeddings to enhance the classification performance. Training and evaluation of the model were conducted using the Enron-Spam dataset and achieved its highest accuracy at 99.54%, with precision, recall, and F1-score all above 99%. This performance beats the previously reported performance on the same dataset. Data preprocessing included duplicates removal, cleaning, and tokenization using the XLM-RoBERTa tokenizer. The model underwent training for 10 epochs, incorporating hyperparameter optimization and early stopping techniques to mitigate overfitting. These results demonstrate the validity and suitability of the model for actual spam detection tasks. Future extensions include deploying the model on other datasets as well as deploying it for low-resource languages such as Bangla.

title: An Adaptive Confidence-Weighted Transformer Ensemble Framework for Tomato Leaf Disease Recognition
authors: Md. Fatin Nibbrash Nakib, Md. Shahid Uz Zaman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Global economic growth relies on agricultural output, as it ensures food security, raises incomes, and supports employment. However, crop diseases can drastically reduce yields, disrupt supply chains, and strain financial resources, especially in developing countries. Tomato leaf infections are among the most serious threats to the agricultural industry and local farmers. This study proposes a deep learning-based method for the early and precise identification of tomato leaf diseases, covering 10 distinct classes using 18,160 images from the PlantVillage dataset. Two pre-trained transformer-based architectures: Vision Transformer (ViT) and Data-efficient Image Transformer (DeiT) were used as feature extractors, followed by evaluation with multiple classifiers, including logistic regression, XGBoost, and a custom dense layer. Experimental results show that the custom dense layer achieved the highest classification performance among all classifiers. The outputs of the ViT and DeiT models were further combined using a confidence-based weighted ensemble, enabling dynamic prioritization of predictions with higher certainty. The proposed framework achieved 99.95 % classification accuracy with an exceptionally low error rate of 0.0005, outperforming individual models. These results highlight the effectiveness of transformer-based feature extraction combined with ensemble learning for reliable, scalable, and accurate plant disease detection, offering valuable support for sustainable agriculture and global food security.

title: Discovering Academic Pathways of Course Enrollment with Unsupervised Clustering and Rule Mining
authors: Mustakim Ahmed, Sourav Datto, Mohaimen-Bin-Noor
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Student course enrollment data often contains hidden patterns that can provide insights into academic behavior and curricular structures. However, detecting meaningful associations and clusters from such data remains challenging due to the overlap of courses across semesters and specializations. This study presents a combined framework of association rule mining and clustering for analyzing student enrollment records. A binary transaction matrix was created from the most frequent courses and analyzed using both pairwise co-occurrence measures and Apriori rules to capture complementary associations. For segmentation, K-Means, Mean Shift, and Gaussian Mixture Models were applied, with evaluation through silhouette score, Davies Bouldin index, and Dunn index. Results showed that Mean Shift achieved the highest cluster separation but produced too many clusters, while Gaussian Mixture provided fewer interpretable clusters aligned with curricular tracks. Pairwise methods identified strong but rare associations, while Apriori captured more frequent rules. Together these findings demonstrate that student enrollments are not random, but form organized trajectories based on semester timing and subject specialization. The framework supports curriculum design, targeted advising, and identification of academic pathways, providing a practical tool for institutions to enhance program management and student guidance.

title: Modeling of small-signal source and drain charges in ASM-HEMT model
authors: Shrayan Mukhopadhyay, Mohit Shukla, Amit Kumar Behera, Avirup Dasgupta
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: This paper is an attempt to start inclusion of the effect of small signal perturbations at any of the 3 HEMT device terminals specified in the ASM HEMT model. We propose formulae for calculation of small signal charge density at source and drain terminals in terms of device parameters and applied voltages. The proposed model is able to capture the non-quasi static perturbations more accurately and shows good agreement with the TCAD data.

title: GAF-DeepGCN: Enhanced Autism Spectrum Disorder Classification via Gramian Angular Field Augmented Deep Graph Convolutional Networks
authors: Sadman Israk Sporsho, Yuki Bhowmik, Naima Siddika Toha, Riasat Khan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) classification remains a significant challenge due to the disorder's heterogeneity and complex neurological underpinnings. This work introduces GAF-DeepGCN, a novel framework integrating Gramian Angular Field (GAF) encoded temporal dynamics with Deep Graph Convolutional Networks (DeepGCN) to enhance ASD classification performance. By transforming resting-state fMRI (rs-fMRI) time series into GAF images, we capture temporal dependencies and leverage the representational power of ResNet50 to extract salient features. Combined with functional connectivity matrices, these features form the node attributes in a population graph, which a DeepGCN subsequently processes. Our experiments on the Autism Brain Imaging Data Exchange (ABIDE) dataset demonstrate that GAF-DeepGCN achieves 83.65% accuracy and an AUC of 0.9116, surpassing existing methodologies. The results highlight the efficacy of integrating temporal dynamics with graph-based deep learning for improved ASD diagnosis.

title: Employing Machine Learning Algorithms for Intrusion Attack Detection
authors: Nadia Attiya Algaddar, Melad Mohamed Al-Daeef, Alhadi A. Klaib
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: As computer networks grow increasingly, complex and robust security mechanisms are essential and critically needed. This research is an attempt to evaluate four machine learning algorithms they are, Decision Tree, Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbor, and Support Vector Machine for real-time intrusion detection using the publicly available CICIDS2017 dataset. Based on performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, Decision Tree and SVM are expected to achieve the highest accuracy, demonstrating strong efficiency and reliability for real-time intrusion detection.

title: The Impact of Adherence to Corporate Governance Principles on Enhancing the Quality of Financial Reports : Applied Study on Non-Financial Sector Companies Listed on the Bahrain Bourse
authors: Nadya Alshareeda, Ammar Alsammarraee
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study investigates the effect of corporate governance compliance on the quality of financial reports in non-financial companies listed on the Bahrain Bourse. A descriptive-analytical method was applied, using a questionnaire to measure governance adherence and the accounting conservatism model to evaluate reporting quality. Data were analyzed through SPSS. The analysis indicates that the firms evaluated showed high compliance with the rules of good governance and the quality of corporate financial reporting significantly benefits from these norms. The study suggests further development of governance practices, training of companies to raise awareness of these matters, and implementation of an annual public ranking of levels of compliance. Firms which adhere to these governance standards should also receive incentives.

title: Deep Learning and Ensemble Techniques in Fake Image Detection
authors: Md. Nabil Hasan Mumat, Rakibul Hasan Showrov, Nashita Nawar Prapti, Md. Shahriar Hussain
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The growth of deep learning has resulted in the creation of deepfake images that are remarkably realistic, posing substantial risks to data integrity, security, and privacy. Detecting such manipulated content is crucial for preventing misuse in social media, journalism, and authentication systems. This study employed advanced feature extraction techniques and neural network architectures to explore machine learning-based deepfake detection. It analyzes spatial inconsistencies, such as artifacts and unnatural blending, through models like EfficientNet-B3, ConvNeXt, Swin Transformer, ViT, and ensemble architectures combining features from ConvNeXt, Swin Transformer, ViT, and EfficientNet-B3 with an NN-Classifier using soft voting. The models were trained, validated, and tested on comprehensive datasets, achieving the highest accuracy with soft voting: 99.43% in training and validation and 99.43% in testing. These findings illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods in enhancing detection accuracy and addressing generalization challenges, ultimately leading to reliable systems that combat misinformation and deepfakes.

title: Transformer-Based Multimodal Fusion Technique for Bengali Aggressive Meme Classification
authors: Anirban Barua, Binoy Das Gupta, Hasan Murad
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Memes have become a familiar feature on social media, but their quick rise has led to concerns about how easily they can be used to spread harmful content and hate speech. It becomes particularly challenging to handle such content in lowresource languages like Bengali due to script complexity, and existing approaches mostly use conventional multimodal fusion techniques. In this work, we present an efficient multimodal meme classification framework that identifies aggressive memes and categorizes them into five classes based on their targeted hate group. Our method combines textual embeddings from MuRIL with visual embeddings from CLIP to capture crossmodal relationships. Moreover, we utilize a compact transformerbased fusion strategy for aligning image and text features, achieving notable improvements over common fusion methods across multiple encoder combinations. We also explore the use of state-of-the-art multimodal large language models for this task. We achieve a weighted F1 score of 0.772 using our proposed method, indicating its effectiveness for aggressive meme classification in the Bengali language.

title: A Commercial-Grade Reliable 40nm eRRAM Macro Featuring a Dual-Oxide Resistive Switching Layer
authors: Zhenchao Sui, Chengxiang Ma, Kaimeng Liu, Xiangchao Ma, Jianshi Tang, Huaqiang Wu, Xing Zhang
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: This paper presents an embedded resistive random-access memory (eRRAM) macro fabricated on a commercial 40nm CMOS technology platform. Through a comprehensive product-level data validation, we first established a baseline HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>-based RRAM that optimizes the trade-off between the forming and pre-cycling times during the chip probing (CP) stage. Subsequently, a novel dual-oxide resistive switching layer (RSL), enabled by an optimized atomic layer deposition (ALD) process, was developed. This dual-oxide RSL significantly enhanced the retention performance of the RRAM macro, achieving a 38.9% improvement in the post-bake memory window on the wafer level without degrading other performance metrics. The optimized 40nm RRAM macro demonstrates commercial-grade performance for the display driver integrated circuit (DDIC) application, including endurance >1K cycles, data retention >10 years at 85°C, read disturb immunity >10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">9</sup> cycles, and passes the solder reflow test at 260°C for 900s according to JEDEC standard [1]. Therefore, this work provides a highly competitive embedded non-volatile memory (eNVM) solution and promotes the commercialization of RRAM technology.

title: Smart Computing Approaches for Pulmonary Tuberculosis Diagnosis: A Decision Support Perspective
authors: Mohammad Nishat Akhtar, Sheikh Mohamad Naim Shikh Shatir, Elmi Abu Bakar, A.Amudha, P.Jeevananthan, S. Varalakshmi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Pulmonary tuberculosis (PTB) is a worldwide health problem; hence, accurate, fast, and economical diagnostic methods are needed. Expert interpretation, expensive expenses, and long turnaround times are typical of conventional diagnostic methods. This research proposes an Intelligent Tuberculosis Diagnostic Support System (ITDSS) that leverages ML, AI, and IoT to address these constraints. Using a cloud-based multi-agent decision support system, ITDSS allows seamless data exchange and real-time diagnosis, optimizing healthcare operations. CNNs evaluate chest X-rays, and NLP interprets clinical data in the system. According to experiments, the ITDSS software helps doctors make quicker, more accurate diagnoses and streamline the identification process. Intelligent computing improves TB detection accuracy and reach, according to the findings. The ITDSS is a smart, scalable system that improves healthcare delivery in resource-limited locations and early illness detection.

title: Integrating Employer Branding Strategies To Attract Job Applications Through Company Reputation In The Healthcare Industry Among Generation Z
authors: Nova Nur Anisa, Velani Choirunisa, Oktavia Aini Budiningsih, Kartika Aprilia Benhardy
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Considering corporate reputation as a mediator variable, this study intends to examine how company branding tactics affect Generation Z's desire to seek for jobs in the healthcare sector. The healthcare industry in Indonesia is currently experiencing rapid growth, driven by increased public awareness of healthcare services and the growing dominance of Generation Z in the productive age group. This research uses a quantitative approach, utilizing a questionnaire distributed to more than 200 respondents aged 21– 28 years. The results show that employer branding has a positive and significant effect on both corporate reputation and the intention to apply for jobs. Moreover, corporate reputation is proven to mediate the relationship between employer branding and job application intention

title: Accurately Allocating Network Slice in 5G Networks with Explainability Analysis
authors: Avishek Chowdhury, Emon Palit, Dhrubajyoti Das
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Network slice allocation plays a pivotal role in optimising the Quality of Service (QoS) and delivery of differentiated services in modern communication systems. This study focuses on the development and evaluation of a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier to predict network slices based on Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) such as packet delay, packet loss rate, technology support, and other network parameters. The MLP model was designed with two hidden layers containing 8 and 4 neurons, using the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation function and adaptive learning with the Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam) optimiser. The model demonstrates rapid convergence, achieving a high level of accuracy in a minimal number of epochs. To interpret the model's predictions, SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) was utilised, providing both global and local explanations. SHAP force plots highlighted the impact of individual features on specific predictions, while summary bar and violin plots emphasised “Packet Delay” and “Technology Supported” as the most significant contributors across the dataset. These visualisations revealed how feature values influenced the model's output, improving transparency and interoperability. The integration of SHAP explanations offers valuable insights into model behaviour, ensuring trust and accountability in predictions. This study sets the groundwork for further exploration of interpretable machine learning in realworld network optimisation scenarios.

title: BERTopic for Disaster Response Needs Assessment: Insights from Twitter(X) Data
authors: Jessa O. Poe, Jeffrey S. Ingosan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Assessment of community needs is essential in disaster response, as it guides Local Government Units and responders in identifying priorities and delivering appropriate assistance to the affected communities. Social media platforms, particularly Twitter(X), were considered as a valuable tool for obtaining information on public concerns and needs during disasters. However, the large volume of data and noise found on social media posed challenges in accurately identifying relevant information. The emergence of unsupervised machine learning approaches, such as topic modeling, enabled the efficient analysis of large datasets. This study investigated the use of BERTopic, a topic modeling technique, to identify and analyze community needs expressed in Twitter(X) data. Historical data were preprocessed, embedded using SentenceTransformers, transformed with class-based TFIDF representations, and clustered using GPU-accelerated cuML to improve the efficiency of both UMAP and HDBSCAN. Several hyperparameter tuning experiments were conducted, and coherence scores were used to evaluate the performance and assess the quality of the generated topics. These results highlighted the importance of combining unigrams and bigrams in enhancing topic coherence. The identified categories were: “Shelter and Evacuation”, “Food and Water”, “Clothing”, “Health and Medical”, “Search and Rescue”, and “Power and Communications”. The study also integrated a Large Language Model (LLM) and manual validation to support topic labeling and enhance interpretability. These results highlight the potential of integrating BERTopic, a transformer-based topic modeling, into disaster response, providing valuable insights to support the assessment of community needs.

title: Prompt, Assert, Repeat: Test-Driven Prompting as a Quality Assurance Strategy for Code-Generating LLMs
authors: Brahma Reddy Korraprolu, Mohan Gangadhar Gudey, Y. Raghu Reddy, Dhiraj SM Varanasi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Code-generating LLMs are increasingly used in software development, but compiling code does not imply functional correctness; outputs must be tested. This paper studies a lightweight QA strategy where unit tests are embedded in the prompt to guide code generation, followed by execution against a fixed test suite. Using the Mostly Basic Python Problems (MBPP) benchmark (964 problems, 3 assertions each), we evaluate twelve open-source LLMs with (i) plain prompts and (ii) assertionaugmented prompts, generating five samples per configuration and measuring pass@1 and pass@5. We find that including assertions often yields large correctness gains (frequently 20-30 points in pass@5), but the effect is model and problem-type dependent, with notable degradations for some instruction-tuned models. Across problem types, conditional-logic tasks are consistently easier, while recursion-heavy categories (e.g., dynamic programming/tree traversal) remain challenging. Based on these results, we outline SQA guidance on when test-driven prompting and limited resampling are effective for improving correctness in LLM-assisted programming.

title: NextSeed: An IoT Based Precision Agriculture System for Soil Analysis and Crop Recommendation
authors: Md. Abdur Rakib, Md. Mohidul Alam, Dabasis Das, Golam Md. Muradul Bashir, Md. Mahbubur Rahman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Agriculture is an important sector for the economy and food security of Bangladesh, which is facing several challenges due to climate change, lack of cultivable land and inadequate access to advanced farming technologies. To overcome the above challenges, this paper proposes NextSeed, which is an IoT based precision agriculture system that integrates realtime soils monitoring with machine learning for intelligent crop suggestion. The system uses a 7-in-1 soil sensor to measure NPK nutrients, pH, moisture, temperature and electrical conductivity, and some environmental sensors for rainfall, water level and atmospheric conditions. Data goes through anomaly detection before the cloud processing where a Naive Bayes classifier performs 99.77% accuracy in crop prediction. The system also offers fertilizer recommendations and allows automated irrigation control depending on soil conditions and water availability. NextSeed helps to cover significant gaps in solutions on the market by providing integrated automation that combines soil nutrition analysis with crops prediction and water management for agricultural decision support.

title: Chip-to-Package Level Thermal Analysis of GaN HEMT with Thermally Conductive Epoxy Integration
authors: Puteri Nur Aisyah Abd Rahim, Amir Murtadha Mohamad Yussof, M.F. Abdullah, Norhana Arsad, Iskandar Yahya, Norazreen Abd Aziz
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: Gallium Nitride High Electron Mobility Transistor (GaN HEMT) is a transistor that is recognised for its faster switching speed and high efficiency in a compact size that can handle the high frequency and high power application. However, with the miniaturisation of the component, thermal management is still a challenge due to localised hotspot formation which can cause reduction in device reliability. In this study, thermal behaviour was studied at chip-level and package-level. Currently there’s no software that can support the thermal simulation from chip-level to package-level. To address this issue, the study of thermal behaviour in chip-level for GaN HEMT was carried out in Silvaco Atlas by simulation the hotspot formation in active region, which is the gate-drain access region. The package-level simulation was done in ANSYS Icepak where a block of heat source was replicated to behave as GaN and also a GaN chip model was placed in D2PAK package in the simulation. The maximum temperature when simulating a dummy block is lower compared to a modeled GaN HEMT which is 148.78 °C while the peak temperature with a modeled GaN chip is 166.7 °C. A novel thermally conductive epoxy was also introduced inside the package to increase the heat dissipation as a part of thermal management. Even though the exact composition of the epoxy is beyond the scope of this paper, the findings in this paper bridges the thermal behaviour of GaN HEMT on the chip-level and the package-level.

title: An Ease and Expedite Decision Support System for Optimized Fertilizers Application - The Case Study of a Blueberry Farm
authors: António D. Costa, João A. Padrão, Guilherme C. Inocêncio, Tânia M. Lima, Pedro D. Gaspar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This paper presents a decision support system for fertilizer optimization in blueberry farming, developed using Microsoft Excel Solver for broad accessibility. The methodology applies a linear programming model that integrates complete nutrient constraints and fertilizer-plant-soil interactions, tested in a real-world Portuguese blueberry farm. Results demonstrate how cost-effective fertilizer use can maintain optimal crop yield and quality for small-and medium-sized producers. The proposed system bridges the gap between research and practical application by offering an adaptable, low-cost solution. Contributions include a validated optimization model for fertigation, sensitivity analysis, and identified potential for wider deployment across varied agricultural contexts.

title: Research on Fault Section Location in Distribution Network Based on Improved Spider Wasp Optimization Algorithm
authors: Jiadong Su, Zhijian Liang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: In recent years, the topology of modern distribution networks has undergone continuous evolution and restructuring due to the large-scale integration of distributed energy resources. As a result, conventional intelligent optimization algorithms are increasingly unable to meet the requirements for rapid fault location in these networks. In response to the aforementioned challenges, this paper innovatively applies the Spider Wasp Optimization Algorithm (ISWO) to fault location in distribution networks. By leveraging its strong exploration and exploitation capabilities, global search performance, high search efficiency, and solution accuracy, the algorithm is further enhanced through three improvements: optimal point set initialization, sine-based search guidance, and adaptive mutation and crossover probabilities. These modifications significantly improve its effectiveness in addressing the optimization objectives of fault location. Extensive comparative experiments and data analysis demonstrate that the improved ISWO achieves superior performance in terms of fault location speed, accuracy, and fault tolerance.

title: A Unified Model-Based Framework For Whole Warship Engineering
authors: Pierluigi Gravili, Andrea Gualco, Gianluigi Mattia, Manuela Nardini, Elisa Nitoglia, Francesco Inglima, Francesco Nudo, Giuliano Pompeo, Antonella Persechino, Pasquale Polisi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: The increasing technological complexity of modern military ships calls for innovative methodologies capable of managing Systems-of-Systems with high integration and interdependence. This paper introduces the Whole Warship Unified Model-Based Framework, a novel Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) approach developed by Orizzonte Sistemi Navali in collaboration with Capgemini Engineering. The framework treats the warship as a single integrated system, consolidating functional, physical, logistic, and human-related aspects within a coherent digital model spanning the entire lifecycle. Acting as a model-centric backbone, it connects requirements, behaviors, and constraints from conceptual design to operation and maintenance, ensuring traceability and validation continuity. The Whole Warship model embeds systems engineering and integrated logistics support into a common digital environment, enabling consistent analysis of operability, crew workload, and human-machine interactions. Due to the incorporation of logistic and human factors from the outset, the framework supports lifecycle trade-offs in cost, availability, and maintainability. This unified approach represents a major step forward in warship design, providing a single authoritative source of truth that improves efficiency, transparency, and decision-making throughout lifecycle. In addition, its architecture establishes digital continuity across domains and facilitates the integration of AIassisted analyzes for improved design iteration and operational evaluation.

title: Development of Correlation for Assessment of BIPV Potential Under Shading Constraints
authors: Priyanka Rai, Raj Gupta, Jyotirmay Mathur, Amartya Chowdhury
publish: 2026 4th Odisha International Conference on Electrical Power Engineering, Communication and Computing Technology (ODICON)
abstract: Building Integrated Photovoltaic (BIPV) systems are increasingly considered for solar deployment in dense urban areas where rooftop availability is limited. However, adjacent buildings often obstruct solar access and cause substantial irradiation losses, leading to inaccurate energy yield estimates at early design stages. This study develops a geometry-based correlation to quantify shading-induced irradiation losses for building-integrated photovoltaic façades under urban obstruction conditions. A large parametric simulation framework was established using DesignBuilder with EnergyPlus as the simulation engine and Python-based automation. Obstruction height, width, and inter-building distance were systematically varied along with façade orientation and geographical location. More than 5,500 annual simulation cases were generated across multiple Indian cities covering a wide latitudinal range. Irradiation losses were evaluated relative to unobstructed reference cases and analysed using regression and feature-importance techniques. The results show that obstruction geometry dominates shading losses, while latitude and altitude have a secondary influence. East and west façades exhibit the highest sensitivity to shading, whereas north facades show comparatively lower losses. Orientationspecific polynomial correlations are proposed for rapid estimation of shading losses during early design. The developed correlations provide a practical and interpretable tool for realistic performance assessment of façade-integrated photovoltaic systems in compact urban contexts.

title: Structured Reasoning Frameworks for LLM-Based Venture Capital Evaluation: Integrating Systems Engineering and Business Analysis
authors: Dan Velarde, Stephen Gillespie
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Venture capital decision-making for early-stage startups involves high uncertainty and relies heavily on qualitative reasoning from pitch decks. This study investigates whether large language models (LLMs) equipped with structured knowledge frameworks can improve predictive accuracy in startup evaluation. We designed four evaluation personas using Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview: a baseline model, a venture capital-informed model, a systems engineering-guided model using established standards from IEEE, INCOSE, NASA, and MITRE, and a combined model integrating both frameworks. Testing on 14 historical pitch decks from companies including LinkedIn, Theranos, and WeWork, the combined persona achieved $85.7 \%$ accuracy in predicting startup outcomes, outperforming specialized and baseline configurations. The systems engineering rubric provided quantitative risk assessment while the venture capital knowledge stack enhanced market opportunity identification. Results demonstrate that LLMs can function as structured reasoning engines when constrained by domain-specific frameworks, offering transparent and auditable decision support for high-uncertainty investment environments.

title: Acute Effects of Exergaming Session and Play Mode on Physiological and Psychosocial Responses in Sedentary Young Adults: A Quasi-Experimental Study
authors: Yunlong Xu, Runting Zhong, Xinhui Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Games
abstract: Purpose: This study investigated the acute effects of Nintendo ARMS™ exergaming on physiological and psychosocial responses in sedentary young adults. Methods: Fifty-two male college students (20.14 ± 1.34 years) participated in a 2 (exergaming session: pretest vs. posttest) × 2 (play mode: single player vs. multi-player mode) experiment. Participants were assigned to single-player or multi-player modes based on their registration preference, with multi-player participants playing in pairs. All participants completed a 30-minute exergaming session. Energy expenditure and mean heart rate were recorded during gameplay, while reaction time, positive affect, and sociability were assessed before and after the session. Semi-structured interviews were conducted to collect participants' feedback. Results: Nintendo ARMS™ exergaming achieved moderate-intensity physical activity. Reaction time and positive affect improved significantly from pretest to posttest. No significant differences between play modes were observed for energy expenditure, heart rate, reaction time, and positive affect. Sociability increased significantly only in multi-player mode, whereas no significant pre-post change was observed in single-player mode. Participants generally reported positive attitudes toward exergaming and considered it an enjoyable and convenient form of home-based exercise. Conclusion: Nintendo ARMS™ can provide moderate physical activity, and short-term physiological and psychosocial benefits, with multi-player sessions showing particular potential for enhancing sociability.

title: A Fully Integrated Dual-Phase Switched-Capacitor Rectifier for Piezoelectric Energy Harvesting With High Power Extraction Enhancement
authors: Honglei Xu, Yuzhou Mao, Quan Sun, Yanzhao Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: This brief presents a fully integrated dual-phase switched-capacitor (DPSC) rectifier for piezoelectric energy harvesting (PEH). An on-chip reconfigurable switched-capacitor (SC) converter is implemented to eliminate the need for external inductors and capacitors, significantly reducing the overall system volume. The efficiency is enhanced by reusing the inherent capacitance (Cₚ) of the transducer as a flying capacitor of the SC converter through a dual-phase charge transfer technique. Furthermore, an all-digital flipping control scheme is employed to minimize system power consumption. The proposed circuit was fabricated in a 0.18μm BCD process. The measurement results show that a 76% flipping efficiency and a 6.7× energy extraction enhancement are achieved.

title: Development of a Bespoke Hybrid Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) Spectrometer for Simultaneous In Situ Neutron Diffraction Studies
authors: Kai Silver, Heungjae Choi, Michael Barter, Steve C. Cripps, Ron I. Smith, Emma Richards, Damien M. Murphy, Martin O. Jones, Adrian Porch
publish: IEEE Journal of Microwaves
abstract: We present the design, construction, and commissioning of the first hybrid Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) spectrometer engineered to operate within a Neutron Powder Diffractometer (NPD), enabling simultaneous measurements of spin properties and crystal structure. Developed for the Polaris instrument at the ISIS Neutron and Muon Source (U.K.), the system was specifically designed to function within the constraints of a neutron beamline. Achieving in situ EPR capability within the diffractometer required several necessary compromises to the diffraction geometry. The finite gap between the two halves of the electromagnet body reduces detector visibility, limiting coverage for a proportion of scattering angles. The retained magnetic return-path introduce restricted regions in 2<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\theta$</tex-math></inline-formula> that require reorientation of the instrument to access. Furthermore, the change in sample geometry from a conventional vertical cylindrical configuration to a skewed horizontal cylinder had a detrimental effect on diffraction peak shape and width. The aluminium microwave cavity, although thinned to 0.1 mm to maximise neutron transparency, introduces additional background Bragg reflections. Despite these trade-offs, simultaneous EPR and neutron diffraction data were successfully acquired. Key innovations include a bespoke electromagnet compatible with beamline access, a thinwalled X-band quasi-elliptical microwave cavity, a remotely tuneable waveguide coupling scheme, and a modular support structure optimised to minimise parasitic scattering. The system was benchmarked using standard paramagnetic reference samples, demonstrating EPR sensitivity comparable to commercial laboratory instruments. This work establishes a new class of multimodal instrumentation, enabling operando investigations of materials in which coupled spinlattice phenomena govern functionality, including batteries, catalysts, and quantum materials.

title: Online Lifelong Optimal Adaptive Control of Partially Uncertain Strict Feedback Discrete-Time Systems With Application to Quadrotor UAVs
authors: Maxwell Geiger, Sarangapani Jagannathan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: This article considers the infinite time horizon optimal adaptive tracking control of partially uncertain strict feedback discrete-time (DT) systems with application to quadrotor uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs). First, the strict feedback DT system is transformed into an equivalent affine nonlinear DT system in terms of the tracking error dynamics. The optimal adaptive tracking control problem is solved using an augmented system approach, where a horizon of future bounded reference trajectory points is used in the augmented state, when compared to using a single point. It is assumed that the internal dynamics of the strict feedback system are unknown, but the control coefficient matrix is known. By applying approximate dynamic programming (ADP) using exclusively multilayer neural networks (MNNs), the optimal control policy is obtained without any other stabilizing controller. Lastly, regularization penalties are introduced for both the actor and critic NNs to support multitask learning. Upper bounds for the multitask learning hyperparameter <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda $</tex-math> </inline-formula> are proved for the first time in the online learning regime. Under our proposed MNN weight update laws, the tracking and weight estimation errors are proved to be uniformly ultimately bounded (UUB) using Lyapunov analysis. In particular, we consider the case of a quadrotor UAV where the system drift dynamics are unknown, but the control input matrix is known, corresponding to knowledge of the mass and principal moments of inertia of the UAV. The efficacy and reliability of the method are demonstrated through computer simulations and hardware implementation on the Quanser QDrone2 UAV, where the cost is reduced as much as 68% without the regularization penalty when compared to the initial control policy. With the regularization penalty, the cost is reduced by 2% compared to without using the penalty.

title: Design of a Hall Signal Simulator for Three-Phase DC Brushless Motors
authors: Hengtian Yang, Fuyuan Deng, Lulu Chen, Liangmei Zhang, Kangjun Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: In the design and development of three-phase brushless DC motor drivers, using a physical motor as the load introduces issues like large mechanical inertia, high energy consumption, and safety risks; thus, high-power resistors are often used for static debugging to replace motor windings, but driver commutation logic relies heavily on Hall signals for rotor position feedback. To address this, this paper proposes a pure hardware Hall signal simulator based on CD4060 and CH250: it constructs a high-precision clock source with CD4060 (using a parameter-matched external crystal oscillator for stable output) and leverages the CH250's three-phase single six-step mode to generate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 0 0 ~ H z}$</tex> three-phase analog Hall signals with 120° mutual phase difference-these signals can directly replace a motor's built-in Hall outputs, meeting drive circuit debugging needs. Analysis shows the simulator accurately emulates physical motor position feedback, providing a reliable hardware solution for driver closedloop logic verification.

title: IPsec Based on Quantum Key Distribution: Adapting Non-3GPP Access to 5G Networks to the Quantum Era
authors: Asier Atutxa, Ane Sanz, Eire Salegi, Gaizka González, Jasone Astorga, Eduardo Jacob
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The advent of quantum computing threatens publickey cryptography mechanisms currently used in 5G networks, including IPsec-based tunnel establishment relying on Diffie-Hellman (DH) key exchange. This issue is especially relevant for non-3GPP access scenarios, where User Equipment (UE) connects to the 5G core through the Non-3GPP Interworking Function (N3IWF) using IPsec. This paper presents a Quantum Key Distribution (QKD)-based mechanism for non-3GPP access in 5G networks, replacing the classical DH exchange with QKD keys during IPsec tunnel establishment. The proposed system is experimentally validated in a testbed combining commercial QKD equipment and an open-source 5G core implementation.

title: Research on a Comprehensive Evaluation Method for Power Outage Losses of Users in the Power Grid
authors: Wei Liu, Shenglong Zhu, Jian Ge, Chao Zhen, Zhengkai Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: In order to enhance the resilience of the power grid under natural disasters and accurately quantify power outage losses, this study establishes a system for classifying outageaffected users and evaluating losses based on multidimensional data analysis. First, by collecting and integrating historical outage data caused by natural disasters such as storms, floods, and ice storms, a multidimensional feature set is constructed, and clustering analysis is applied to achieve refined user portrait classification within the power supply grid. Second, combining statistical and machine learning techniques, differentiated models for evaluating economic losses and social impacts of power outages are developed for different user categories (residential, commercial, industrial). Finally, by integrating regional grid structures, disaster scenarios, and user distribution differences, a natural disaster outage risk identification model is developed to identify weak points in the grid and high-risk user groups and areas, thereby providing scientific basis and decision support for optimizing pre-disaster prevention and post-disaster rapid restoration strategies.

title: Joint Scheduling of Use-Based and Calendar Inspections in Military Aircraft Fleet Planning
authors: Matteo Vescovi, Gianluca Massara, Valentina Breschi, Ten. Col. Roberto Valdambrini, Col. Andrea Mercurio, Mara Tanelli
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: The scheduling of flight operations and maintenance activities is commonly referred in military aviation as Flight and Maintenance Planning; and it is essential to guarantee military fleet availability and mission readiness for both routine and high-intensity operations. Although maintenance programs may vary significantly depending on configuration, usage patterns, or specific system requirements; in a general form, aircrafts follow a strict, predefined inspection schedule, including both use-based (in term of flown hours) and calendar-based inspection, that may differ from one aircraft to another within the same fleet. Moreover, it is common practice to merge inspections of different types when they are scheduled to occur in close succession, thereby reducing downtime of shared maintenance operations. Existing studies rarely address such mixed-type inspections and often rely on excessively simplified, unrealistic maintenance schemes. This work proposes an ad hoc Mixed-Integer Programming formulation that extends established models by integrating inspection data from lookup tables directly into the optimisation constraints, allowing the joint management of complex flight-hour-based and calendar-based inspection plans within a unified framework. Tests on a simple yet realistic fleet confirm the model’s ability to plan mixed inspections effectively while preserving operational feasibility and resource balance. The approach offers a flexible foundation for real-life preventive maintenance planning, even outside the aviation or military context.

title: Effect of ECRAM Temperature-Dependent Conductance on Analog In-Memory Computing Inference Accuracy
authors: M.A. Islam, A. Dozier, H.M.R. Farque, R.C. Dempsey, T.Q. Nguyen, M.J. Voegtle, M.N. Kozicki, M.J. Marinella
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: Analog in-memory computing (AIMC) based on electrochemical random-access memory (ECRAM) offers a promising route to energy-efficient and high-accuracy AI computing. However, the temperature-dependent conductance introduces error that degrades AIMC accuracy. This work characterizes device conductance over the temperature range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2}^{\circ} \mathbf{C}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0}^{\circ} \mathbf{C}$</tex> and develops a transport model based on nearestneighbor hopping. The temperature dependence degrades CIFAR-10 and ImageNet accuracy. To address this issue, we propose a temperature-aware scaling method that compensates for conductance variation. Applying this correction technique maintains CIFAR-10 accuracy within 2% of ideal through the entire temperature range, demonstrating a path to accurate AIMC inference across commercially relevant environments.

title: An Explainable Stacked Ensemble Framework for Credit Card Fraud Detection
authors: Protyush Das Gupta, Sourav Adhikary
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Credit card fraud continues to evolve with increasingly complex attack patterns which is creating serious challenges for financial institutions and consumers. This paper presents an explainable stacked ensemble framework that integrates Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) and a Deep Neural Network (DNN) as base learners with a Logistic Regression meta-classifier. Unlike prior works that focus solely on accuracy, our approach jointly addresses predictive performance and interpretability by incorporating SHAP for global feature importance and LIME for local decision explanations. Experiments on a large-scale Kaggle dataset demonstrate that the proposed ensemble achieves superior precision, recall, and F1scores compared to individual models and recent IoT-focused methods. Moreover, we introduce two SHAP configurations—fast and slow that allow trade-offs between computational cost and interpretability, making the framework adaptable for both research and real-time deployment. These results highlight the potential of explainable ensemble learning as a practical solution for secure and transparent fraud detection systems.

title: Towards Energy-Efficient Collaborative Inference and Fine-tuning: Matching Model Compression and Offloading with Resource Availability_supp1-3689748.pdf
authors: Lianbo Ma
publish: N/A
abstract: We consider the collaborative inference acceleration task via cloud-edge-end collaboration, which involves a series of tightly coupled decision-making steps, including which DNN model to be selected, how much to compress model, how to partition model, and where to offload partitioned submodels. In practical deployments, these decisions jointly affect both fine-tuning and inference performance, and must jointly account for such aspects as the model being used, the computational resources and local datasets available at each device, as well as network latencies, which significantly increases the complexity of optimizing the problem. Yet, no existing studies focus on such joint optimization problem for these tightly coupled decisions. In this paper, we model this problem as a multi-dimensional optimization problem, jointly optimizing collaborative inference and fine-tuning by selecting the DNN model, compression level, partition strategy, and computational resource allocation, with the objective of minimizing the overall energy consumption of the learning-inference process, subject to accuracy and latency constraints. To this end, we propose an algorithmic framework called JQODI combining a time-energy tree diagram to represent the learning process, a dynamic programming solution strategy, and a data-driven theoretical approach to predict the expected total number of training epochs that meet the accuracy requirements. We prove that JQODI approximates the optimal solution with polynomial complexity. Numerical results demonstrate that JQODI surpasses state-of-the-art methods in both energy efficiency and latency.

title: Information-Preserving Framework for Gas Leak Detection and Quantification With Uncooled Snapshot Infrared Spectral Imaging
authors: Tianxiang Xiong, Pengyu Wang, Tongtong Li, Yijie Wang, Hao Yan, Shijie Liu, Liyin Yuan, Chunlai Li, Jianyu Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Recent advances in infrared gas leak detection have substantially improved learning-based detection and quantification across different imaging instruments. However, achieving gas detection, classification, and quantitative analysis within a unified system remains challenging because of limited real-time capability, signal instability, and information loss caused by inter-channel inconsistency during normalization. To address these issues, this paper presents a framework with information-preserving preprocessing (IPP) in our uncooled snapshot multi-spectral infrared imaging system. A radiometric signal model is established to characterize information degradation in the raw-to-digital conversion process, and the Relative Background Radiance Contrast (RBRC) metric is introduced to preserve absorption-related spectral variations across channels after normalization. Methane and ammonia are used as representative gases to validate the quantitative performance in controlled gas cell experiments under different background temperatures. With ΔT ranging from 10 to 25 K, the proposed method achieves average relative errors of 11% for methane and 7.8% for ammonia at 3000 ppm·m, while the error increases under low-concentration conditions with weak absorption. Outdoor leakage experiments further show that the proposed preprocessing improves inter-channel consistency and enhances gas detection performance across multiple mainstream object detectors. In addition, the detector-guided post-processing enables quantitative plume segmentation and contrast-enhanced visualization for gas analysis.

title: Meta-Learning for Quantum Optimization via Quantum Sequence Model
authors: Yu-Cheng Lin, Yu-Chao Hsu, Samuel Yen-Chi Chen
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) is a leading approach for solving combinatorial optimization problems on near-term quantum processors. However, finding good variational parameters remains a significant challenge due to the non-convex energy landscape, often resulting in slow convergence and poor solution quality. In this work, we propose a quantum meta-learning framework that trains advanced quantum sequence models to generate effective parameter initialization policies. We investigate four classical or quantum sequence models, including the Quantum Kernel-based Long Short-Term Memory (QK-LSTM), as learned optimizers in a “learning to learn” paradigm. Our numerical experiments on the Max-Cut problem demonstrate that the QK-LSTM optimizer achieves superior performance, obtaining the highest approximation ratios and exhibiting the fastest convergence rate across all tested problem sizes (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n$</tex> = 10 to 13). Crucially, the QKLSTM model achieves highly robust parameter transferability by synthesizing a single, fixed set of near-optimal parameters, leading to a remarkable sustained acceleration of convergence even when generalizing to larger problems. This capability, enabled by the compact and expressive power of the quantum kernel architecture, underscores its effectiveness. The QK-LSTM, with only 43 trainable parameters, substantially outperforms the classical LSTM (56 parameters) and other quantum sequence models, establishing a robust pathway toward highly efficient parameter initialization for variational quantum algorithms in the NISQ era.

title: El Niño/La Niña-driven spatiotemporal heterogeneity of the Eastern Tropical South Pacific Oxygen Minimum Zone with satellite-based long-term records
authors: Zhang Yifan, Bai Yan, He Xianqiang, Li Teng, Gong Fang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Eastern Tropical South Pacific (ETSP) contains one of the largest oxygen minimum zones (OMZs) globally and is strongly influenced by El Niño-La Niña variability. Yet, long-term OMZ depth variability and its controlling processes remain poorly understood due to limited continuous observations. We develop a machine learning-based inversion model to estimate the depth of hypoxia (DOH, the shallowest OMZ depth) using satellite products and biogeochemical Argo oxygen profiles from 2008 to 2019. The model combines physical and biogeochemical predictors, including satellite-derived net community production, to represent the nonlinear regulation of subsurface hypoxia. Validation against independent observations (n = 809) demonstrates robust performance, with a coefficient of determination of 0.86 and a root mean square error of 22.7 m. Using this framework, we reconstruct a 17-year (2003-2019) monthly DOH dataset for the ETSP, enabling a high-frequency satellite-based assessment of OMZ vertical variability across recurrent El Niño-La Niña events. The reconstructed record reveals pronounced contrasts between offshore and nearshore systems. During 2003-2012, thermocline-driven offshore DOH shoaling contrasted with nonlinear nearshore fluctuations modulated by biological processes. The extreme 2012-2016 El Niño interrupted OMZ expansion, triggering pan-ETSP DOH deepening through divergent mechanisms—intensified stratification offshore versus reduced biological oxygen consumption nearshore. Following 2017, rapid offshore shoaling contrasted with delayed nearshore recovery. For the first time, we reveal the high-frequency temporal evolution of OMZ depth (DOH) in response to recurrent El Niño/La Niña events, and elucidate the El Niño/La Niña-driven heterogeneous hypoxia responses and associated environmental controls between nearshore and offshore upwelling systems.

title: THz Transistor Design and Applications
authors: M. J. W. Rodwell, Miguel Urteaga, Yihao Fang, Logan Whitaker, Johann Rode, Amirreza Alizadeh, Utku Soylu, Zach Griffith, Yuya Nemoto
publish: IEEE Nanotechnology Magazine
abstract: We consider the present performance, design principles, and prospects for further improvement, in THz transistor technologies, addressing how transistor characteristics limit IC performance, and showing several 100-300 GHz IC and systems examples.

title: IEEE DataPort
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Women in Engineering Magazine

title: Leveraging SATCOM Ka-Band Signals for PNTaaS
authors: Maor Dov, Alison Brown, Oleg Yakimenko, Giovanni Minelli, Noah Weitz
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) technology has existed for decades and has become so pervasive in modern life that reliance on it now extends far beyond what was originally envisioned, particularly in military applications. As GNSS usage expanded, the threats and vulnerabilities associated with it also grew, especially on the battlefield where GNSS signals are frequently targeted by adversary jamming. GNSS suffers from several inherent disadvantages, foremost among them its narrow and well-known frequency bands, which make it comparatively easy to disrupt. In response to these limitations, a new field of research has emerged in recent years, aiming to develop alternative Position, Navigation, and Timing (PNT) solutions that provide far greater resilience than GNSS. This field, known as Signals of Opportunity (SoOP), leverages opportunistic, non-GNSS signals to construct robust PNT solutions while avoiding many of the fundamental weaknesses inherent in GNSS technology. NAVSYS has recently advanced this concept by developing an innovative cloud-based technology that offers PNT as a Service (PNTaaS). This system aggregates signals from multiple satellite providers across diverse frequency bands to generate an alternative and more resilient PNT solution. To strengthen PNT robustness, incorporating signals from as many providers and orbital infrastructures as possible is essential. Building on NAVSYS’s efforts, this paper investigates the potential use of orbital systems that emit signals in the Ka band-specifically the O3b mPOWER constellation, a high-throughput medium Earth orbit (MEO) system. The discussion includes the envisioned system architecture, along with opportunities and challenges associated with tracking Ka-band signals from both MEO and geostationary (GEO) satellites. These signals may enhance positional dilution of precision, thus improving the performance of SoOP-based PNT solutions. Additionally, this paper presents preliminary results related to system modeling, signal integration approaches, and anticipated performance assessments for incorporating Ka-band satellite emissions into future resilient PNT architectures.

title: Enhancing TV APPs Authoring With LLM Agents
authors: Paulo Victor Borges, Daniel de S. Moraes, Simone D. J. Barbosa, Sergio Colcher
publish: IEEE Transactions on Broadcasting
abstract: Developing interactive hypermedia applications using the Nested Context Language (NCL) presents significant challenges due to its declarative nature and strict syntactic and semantic requirements. This paper introduces a multi-agent tool powered by large language models (LLMs) to assist in the authoring of NCL 3.0 documents. The proposed tool leverages agents specialized in generating, validating, and refining NCL elements, ensuring syntactic correctness and structural alignment. The tool’s usability, workflow efficiency, and perceived user satisfaction were evaluated through a structured observational study. This work contributes to the field of multimedia systems by providing a scalable, efficient, and user-friendly solution for NCL development, paving the way for the broader adoption of declarative languages in digital TV and multimedia applications.

title: Robust Variational Identification of LPV Systems With Asymmetric Measurement Noise
authors: Xiang Chen, Ke Li, Peng Su, Fei Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper considers the problem of parameter identification for linear parameter-varying (LPV) systems with asymmetric noise. Commonly employed noise distributions exhibit symmetric statistical properties that make it difficult to describe asymmetric measurement noise. This leads to a degradation in the performance of the identification algorithm. To cope with the asymmetric noise, the measurement noise is characterized by the generalized hyperbolic skew Student’s t (GHS) distribution. Then, the robust variational algorithm for the LPV system is proposed within the Bayesian framework. The variational Bayesian (VB) algorithm estimates the parameters of the model and the asymmetric noise from the measured data.
