We also present a Spatial-Temporal Adaptive Graph Convolutional Network to capture the intricate spatial-temporal correlations. Experiments on real datasets show KHGTL reduces MAE by 44.7% compared to baselines, outperforms full supervision with 80% faster training, addressing scalability and efficiency challenges in large-scale traffic prediction.

title: An Adaptive Frequency Charge Pump for High-Side Power Supply in Motor Pre-Driver Chips
authors: Yan Jiao, Mingyu Liu, Yunwu Zhang, Yangyang Lu, Zhikai Meng, Chunwei Zhang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: To address the high-side floating power supply requirements in motor pre-driver chips, charge pump technology has been widely researched and applied. This paper proposes an adaptive frequency charge pump scheme based on voltage difference feedback. Its core innovation lies in the real-time sampling of the voltage difference between the bus voltage and the high-side capacitor voltage, which is proportionally scaled into a continuous control voltage. This linearly modulates the internal oscillator frequency, achieving precise matching between the charging rate and the system's energy demand. Design and simulation based on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.18 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex> BCD process show that, compared to a charge pump with voltage detection functionality, this design reduces the output voltage ripple by 55 % while maintaining the same high-side drive capability and power consumption, and supports 100 % duty cycle operation. Additionally, small-signal analysis confirms the system's inherent stability, and layout considerations combined with the adaptive closed-loop architecture ensure robustness against parasitic effects and process variations. This work provides an innovative high-side power supply solution for high-reliability, high-efficiency motor pre-driver chips.

title: An Explainable Vision Mamba Framework for Road Crack Classification with Grad-CAM and LIME-Based Superpixel Interpretations
authors: Nymur Rahaman Antor, Nafisa Tasnim, Md Zubair Azam, Sabab Asfaq, Shahriar Siddique Arjon, Tamanna Yasmin
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Cracks that are not detected in a timely manner have a significant impact on traffic safety, infrastructure stability, and maintenance costs. Conventional human-based monitoring is expensive and slow, and existing deep learning solutions are often “black-box” in nature, limiting decision-making transparency. This study presents an interpretable deep learning framework, where the VisionMamba-based classification model is integrated with Grad-CAM and LIME. Specifically, the primary fracture image dataset used in the study was collected by us directly at the field level, which is consistent with the real environment. The proposed model demonstrated excellent performance in the Alligator crack and Edge crack classifications and achieved an overall accuracy of 98.98 %. The prediction process of the model is transparent as the Grad-CAM and LIME outputs clearly show the crack-related region. Overall, this approach provides an effective solution for smart road maintenance and infrastructure management by ensuring accuracy and transparency.

title: Investigation of electret properties of polylactic acid/curcumin composite films
authors: Temenuzhka Yovcheva, Asya Viraneva, Aleksandar Grigorov, Rumen Mladenov
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Extending the shelf life of food products is of great importance to the food industry. Polylactic acid (PLA) is known as a promising biopolymer that can be used for food packaging due to its high mechanical and permeability properties. However, its low elasticity and high rigidity at room temperature, as well as poor moisture and gas barrier properties limit its applicability. On the other hand, this biopolymer has a significant potential for optimizing physical properties through material modifications, including in combination with natural additives as different polyphenols. Polyphenols are a class of naturally occurring compounds found in plants, exhibit a wide range of beneficial properties, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. Curcumin, is prominent polyphenol with potent antioxidant properties [1]. Blending PLA with polyphenols offers the opportunity to improve the properties of PLA by utilizing the beneficial properties of the polyphenol [2]. The present paper aims to develop new PLA - based composite films with incorporated polyphenol curcumin with different concentration (0 %, 10 %, 15 %, 20 % against PLA) and to examine their electret properties. PLA composite films are prepared using a solution casting method. Curcumin with different concentration is added to the PLA solution. The film-forming solution is cast on levelled glass petri dishes and dried at a temperature of 35 °C until complete chloroform evaporation. All created composite films are cut into 30x30 mm samples and charged in a negative corona in normal atmospheric conditions. The charging of the samples in a corona discharge is carried out by means of a conventional corona triode system consisting of a corona electrode (needle), a grounded plate electrode and a grid placed between them [3]. The electrets surface potential of the charged samples is measured by the vibrating electrode method with compensation and the estimated error is less than 5%. Time and temperature dependences of the normalized surface potential for all investigated electrets are studied. The results obtained show a significant change in the electret behavior of the composite films after the inclusion of curcumin with different concentration into the PLA film. The possible surface potential decay mechanisms responsible for the electret's behavior are discussed. It is established that the surface potential decay depends on the corona polarity and the concentration of the curcumin. The conductivity of all of the investigated films is also determined and the collected results demonstrate that the increase of the curcumin concentration within the PLA films causes a respective increase in conductivity. The wettability of the investigated films is measured using the static water contact angle method. The surface free energy is also calculated and discussed.

title: AI-Driven Dielectric Characterization of E-Waste Composites for Radar Absorbers Using Electromagnetic Mixing Model
authors: Ravi Yadav, Meha Agrawal, Ravi Panwar, Slawomir Koziel
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Performing dielectric characterization via conventional optimization is exceptionally cumbersome due to extensive iterative recalibration. To facilitate the characterization task, this study focuses on designing a custom multilayer perceptron (MLP) model in a constrained environment (domain). The training data is generated within specified limits and follows the Kramers-Kronig causality relations to evaluate the dielectric properties of composites. A generalized MLP model is designed to optimize the EM mixing model parameters for all the desired composites simultaneously. Additionally, a distinctive fitness function integrated with the Arithmetic Optimization Algorithm (AOA) is employed to enhance the performance of a single-layer RAM that considers both transverse electric (TE) and transverse magnetic (TM) polarizations. A thin, cost-effective, angularly stable RAM is designed using e-waste with an optimal thickness of 1.3 mm. The RAM exhibits a reflection coefficient (RC) of –37.2 dB at 11.3 GHz and a bandwidth of 1.9 GHz below –10 dB at normal incidence. In TE and TM polarizations, the peak RC of –15.0 dB and –16.6 dB is achieved at a 45° incidence angle, respectively. This research emphasizes the potential of MLP-assisted characterization of e-waste composites in developing cost-effective, lightweight, and broadband RAMs, demonstrating their effectiveness at various incident angles.

title: Influence of μm-level profile slopes on vacuum DC flashover: a model guided design optimization
authors: Yuhang Huang, Sile Chen, Chao Wang, Yuying Wang, Zhaoquan Chen, Kehan Cao, Baipeng Song, Xiong Yang, Guanjun Zhang, Guangyu Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Surface microstructures (<1 mm) have important influences on the surface insulation strength of insulating materials, which are typically quantified by surface roughness. However, conventional roughness parameters, such as the arithmetic mean height of profile (Ra), cannot distinguish complex microstructures, potentially resulting in different insulation performances of materials with the same roughness. This study proposes a parameter, the sum of unit-length profile slopes (denoted as Rk), which is suitable for characterizing the surface roughness, especially the surface with a structure of regular grooves. Alumina ceramic samples with the same Ra and different Rk are obtained by laser surface grooving, and the effect of Rk on surface insulation performance is studied systematically. It is found that as Rk increased, the surface conductivity showed an upward trend, the surface potential decay of the samples accelerated, the secondary electron yield decreased, and the vacuum flashover voltage increased. Properly adjusting Rk can effectively enhance the strength of the surface insulation of alumina ceramic. The combination of Ra and Rk can serve as a useful tool for the design of surface structures on insulating materials.

title: Vibration Signal Generation for Straddle-Monorail Gearboxes Using a Physics-Conditioned VQ-VAE Model
authors: Ling Zhao, Hangjun Wu, Na Zhang, Gang Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Accurate fault diagnosis of straddle-monorail gearboxes is essential for operational safety. However, data-driven methods are often limited by the scarcity of labeled fault samples. Existing generative approaches, such as generative adversarial networks and diffusion models, tend to produce kinematically inconsistent signals and require substantial computational resources. To address these issues, this article proposes a physics-conditioned vector-quantized variational autoencoder. The model incorporates gearbox dynamics into the generation process via a lightweight, differentiable physical conditioner. This module generates time-aligned physical sequences, including meshing stiffness and fault-induced impulses, which are injected into the latent space via a dual-path conditioning mechanism. This design ensures spectral–temporal consistency between generated signals and underlying physical processes. Experimental results on both self-collected and public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves improved generation quality and computational efficiency. Specifically, the model achieves a root-mean-square error of 0.0497, a peak signal-to-noise ratio of 26.17 dB, and a frequency spectrum cosine similarity of 0.98 or higher. The method also shows strong generalization across multiple datasets, including Southeast University Gearbox and Case Western Reserve University. The proposed approach provides an effective and practical solution for data augmentation in industrial fault diagnosis.

title: When Future Communications Shift Toward Narrow Beams: A Forward-Looking Survey on Pointing Errors and Alignment Limits
authors: Meysam Ghanbari, Mohammad Taghi Dabiri, Osamah S. Badarneh, Mazen Hasna, Yazan H. Al-Badarneh, Mustafa K. Alshawaqfeh, Khalid A. Qaraqe
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Directional links in free-space optical (FSO), millimeter-wave (mmWave), and terahertz (THz) systems are a cornerstone of emerging 6G networks, yet their reliability is fundamentally limited by pointing errors and misalignment. Existing studies address this impairment using technology-specific definitions, models, and mitigation approaches, which hinders cross-domain comparison and transferable design insight. This survey provides a unified treatment of pointing errors across optical and high-frequency wireless communications. We establish consistent terminology and a cross-technology taxonomy of pointing errors, review angular misalignment and statistical distribution models, and analyze their impact on system performance. Mitigation techniques are systematically surveyed with emphasis on optical systems and their connection to underlying pointing-error models. The survey further provides a detailed examination of pointing-error effects in orbital angular momentum (OAM) links and quantum optical communications, and surveys the corresponding mitigation approaches tailored to mode-dependent impairments and quantum measurement constraints. The survey also outlines open challenges and future research directions. By consolidating fragmented literature into a coherent framework, this work supports consistent analysis and robust design of next-generation directional communication systems.

title: A Super Low Frequency Transmitter for Ground-transparent Communication Across Four-layer Medium
authors: Xie He, Jianya Zhang, Qiang Zhou, Changfeng Liang
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Rotating magnet based mechanical antenna (RMBMA) is a novel super low frequency electromagnetic transmitting technology. It uses the mechanical rotary motion of permanent magnet to directly excite electromagnetic waves and is expected to achieve the miniaturization of the super low frequency ground-transparent transmitting system. To reveal the ground-transparent propagation characteristics of the RMBMA, this paper establishes a four-layer medium model of air - reinforced concrete - air - underground rock layer. By using the Helmholtz equations and the Sommerfeld integral, the analytical expression of the RMBMA in the four-layer medium is given. Based on the electromagnetic field simulation software FEKO, the correctness of the proposed four-layer medium propagation model is verified, and the directional characteristics of electromagnetic wave propagation are analyzed. Moreover, the effects of the reinforced concrete layer conductivity and operating frequency on the propagation characteristics of the RMBMA are investigated, and a method for extending the horizontal transmission distance is provided. A prototype of the RMBMA is set up to conduct ground-transparent propagation and communication experiments. This verifies the feasibility of the scheme and lays a foundation for the realization of efficient and miniaturized ground-transparent communication.

title: ISAC-Assisted DRL for Dynamic MAC Scheduler Reconfiguration in O-RAN
authors: Neco Villegas, Juan Luis Herrera, Luis Diez, Domenico Scotece, Luca Foschini, Ramón Agüero
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: This paper introduces reconfiguration mechanisms for a 5th Generation (5G) Medium Access Control (MAC) scheduler, which uses Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) and is deployed as an xApp within the RAN Intelligent Controller (RIC) framework. The objective is to optimize the allocation of radio resources in 5G networks, seeking to meet Quality of Service (QoS) requirements while minimizing resource consumption. To this end, we propose the dynamic adjustment of configurable parameters in a Lyapunov-based scheduler, which addresses the challenges posed by the highly dynamic network environment, and the need to meet multiple QoS objectives. The DRL agent dynamically reconfigures the scheduling by leveraging Integrated Sensing and Communications (ISAC)-provided sensing data alongside conventional communication metrics. Exploiting the adaptability of DRL, our solution can effectively respond to fluctuating network conditions, thereby continuously enhancing scheduling decisions in real time. The proposal is evaluated through comprehensive simulations conducted over ns-3 5G-LENA, which demonstrate notable improvements in QoS performance and resource efficiency. In the analyzed scenarios, our proposed scheduling solution achieves a 30% reduction in radio resource utilization while maintaining full compliance with QoS requirements. These results highlight the great potential of exploiting DRL and sensing data to optimize MAC scheduling in modern wireless communication systems, offering a scalable and adaptive solution for 5G and future 6G networks.

title: Boosting Large Vision-Language Models with Hierarchical Semantic Fusion for Remote Sensing Visual Question Answering
authors: Fan Liu, Jian Huo, Dongmei Wang, Yihong Wang, Yi Xu, Wenwen Dai, Yuexuan An
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Remote Sensing Visual Question Answering aims to answer natural language queries by jointly modeling visual content and semantic meaning in remote sensing imagery. Large Vision Language Models offer strong visual understanding and reasoning capabilities and provide a promising foundation for VQA tasks. However, existing LVLMs are not well adapted to remote sensing scenes, as deep layer focused visual representations tend to overlook low and mid level structural cues required for RS specific reasoning. To address this limitation, we propose the Hierarchical Semantic Fusion Network, HSF-Net, a remote-sensing-oriented enhancement framework built upon LLaVA-v1.5 to balance detail perception and semantic reasoning in RS-VQA. HSF-Net integrates three complementary modules: 1) Frequency-Selective Unsharp Masking amplifies high-frequency structural details in RS imagery; 2) Hierarchical Cross-Level Semantic Fusion injects multi-scale shallow and mid-level cues into deep semantic features while preserving language alignment; 3) Self-Attention-based Semantic Reweighting refines internal semantic correlations to strengthen contextual understanding. Extensive experimental evaluations on three benchmark RS-VQA datasets, compared with lightweight specialized models and superior LVLMs, demonstrate HSF-Net’s state-of-the-art performance across all tasks. Our code is available at: https://github.com/HUOUO7/HSF-Net.

title: STIM-D: A 31× Faster Spatially-Informed Anomaly Detector with High-Confidence Saliency Preservation
authors: Krishnan Batri, Rajermani Thinakaran, S Lakshmi
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Real-time hyperspectral anomaly detection demands computational efficiency without compromising operational reliability. This letter presents STIM-D, a diagonal covariance approximation of the Spectral Thermodynamic Irregularity Model (STIM), designed to enable efficient anomaly detection under strict computational constraints. By replacing full covariance estimation with a diagonal approximation, STIM-D reduces perpixel computational complexity from O(B3) to O(B), where B denotes the number of spectral bands, resulting in an average 31× speedup. Experimental validation on five AVIRIS Cuprite scenes and one cross-scene agricultural dataset demonstrates stable performance across diverse imaging conditions, with 95th percentile (Q95) anomaly scores reaching 0.931, indicating reliable discrimination of high-confidence anomalies for thresholdbased operational systems. STIM-D requires no training data and maintains deterministic behavior, making it well suited for deployment on resource-constrained platforms such as satellite onboard processors and UAV-based environmental monitoring systems.

title: A Differential Resonant Ultrasound Pulser in 180 nm BCD: Fundamental Limits and Design Considerations
authors: Yaohua Zhang, Andreas Demosthenous
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This article presents the design of a differential resonant pulser implemented in 180 nm BCD process for PMUT-based ultrasound applications. By employing a precisely timed switching scheme together with differential resonant operation, a novel voltage step-up methodology is developed. A key contribution of this work is the first comprehensive, phase-by-phase analytical treatment of the pulser operation. This analysis reveals critical inter-phase coupling mechanisms that are not captured by conventional simulation-based approaches. It identifies two previously overlooked design limitations: 1) ac current leakage inherent to back-to-back DMOS configurations; and 2) an unavoidable timing offset caused by finite on-resistance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\mathrm {on}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) between the inductor current peak and the capacitor voltage zero-crossing during discharge. These effects establish fundamental performance boundaries for resonant pulsers. Measurement results validate the proposed design, achieving a 17.7 V output from a 5 V supply (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.5\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> voltage step-up) and a 71.7% reduction in power consumption. The measured performance represents state-of-the-art and shows excellent agreement with both theory and simulation.

title: Stochastic Modeling of the Physical-Digital Continuum: Assessing Feasibility in Decentralized Environmental Monitoring
authors: Carlos Melo, Jean Araujo
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: The disconnect between physical environmental sensing and digital settlement layers significantly limits the scalability of Voluntary Carbon Markets (VCMs). This gap, often characterized as the “Oracle Problem,” arises from the incompatibility between the probabilistic nature of AI-processed remote sensing data and the deterministic requirements of immutable blockchain ledgers. To mitigate this gap, this paper proposes an Autonomous System-of-Systems (SoS) architecture designed to secure the “Physical-Digital Continuum.” The proposed framework orchestrates the end-to-end flow of environmental assets by integrating high-resolution satellite telemetry, decentralized storage (IPFS), and the Hyperledger Fabric blockchain platform. Unlike traditional feasibility studies, we systematically assess the dynamic behavior of the system using formal modeling techniques. We create a Stochastic Petri Net (SPN) model that clearly captures the concurrency of parallel image processing streams, network synchronization delays, and the stochastic latency of distributed consensus protocols. Stationary analysis reveals that the system is arrival-driven, identifying the blockchain’s payment finality as the primary internal bottleneck limiting scalability. Sensitivity analysis shows that optimizing consensus quorum parameters and settlement latencies can reduce the system’s Mean Response Time (MRT) by $\mathbf{5 0 \%}$ and increase throughput by over $\mathbf{3 0 \%}$. These quantitative results provide a validated reference for implementing scalable, automated Digital Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (dMRV) systems.

title: Rate-Fidelity Tradeoffs in All-Photonic and Memory-Equipped Quantum Switches
authors: Panagiotis Promponas, Leonardo Bacciottini, Paul Polakos, Gayane Vardoyan, Don Towsley, Leandros Tassiulas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum entanglement switches are a key building block for early quantum networks, and a central design question is whether near-term devices should use only optical components and photonic qubits or also incorporate quantum memories. We compare two architectures: an all-photonic entanglement generation switch (EGS) that repeatedly attempts Bell-state measurements (BSMs) without storing qubits, and a quantum memory-equipped switch that buffers entanglement and triggers measurements only when heralded connectivity is available (herald-then-swap control). These two designs trade off simple, memoryless operation that avoids decoherence and memory-induced latency against heralding-based control that buffers entanglement to use BSMs more efficiently. We formalize both models under a common hardware abstraction and characterize their achievable rate-fidelity regions, yielding a benchmarking methodology that translates hardware and protocol parameters into network-level performance. Numerical evaluation quantifies the rate-fidelity tradeoffs of both models, identifies operating regions in which each architecture dominates, and shows how hardware and protocol knobs can be tuned to meet application-specific targets.

title: A Compact Physics-Aware Full $I-V$ Model for Nanowire nFET Aging
authors: Michiel Vandemaele, Adrian Chasin, Jacopo Franco, Stanislav Tyaginov, Dieter Claes, Hans Mertens, An De Keersgieter, Geert Hellings, Ben Kaczer
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: We model experimental NanoWire (NW) nFET I-V aging under Bias Temperature Instability (BTI) and Hot-Carrier (HC) stress using our compact physics-aware framework ComphU. We introduce a new simplified and computationally efficient approach to Distribution Function (DF) modeling applicable for short- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$L_{\mathrm{g}}$</tex> FETs and successfully lump the time- 0 variability into a limited parameter set. Our model describes experimental <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta V_{\text {th }}(t)$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta I_{\text {d,on }}(t)$</tex> traces well. Using the calibrated model, we find that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$i$</tex>) the intrinsic time- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0 g_{m, \text { max }}$</tex> is 39 % higher than the extrinsic one, ii) aging outside the calibration region is predicted reasonably, and that the BTI lifetime iii) roughly doubles for every 0.1 V reduction in the supply voltage and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$i v$</tex>) increases up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6.3 \times$</tex> for every 0.1 V increase in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text {th, } 0}$</tex>.

title: Accuracy-Adaptive Speedup of Indirect Optimization Heuristics
authors: Mehreen Khan, Armin Zimmermann
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: This paper addresses a key limitation in simulation-based optimization, where metaheuristics such as simulated annealing (SA) spend excessive time on computationally expensive simulations. We introduce an accuracy-adaptive approach AA-SA that links the simulation part stronger to the SA metaheuristic. Our methodology controls the simulation’s accuracy directly within the SA loop. The search begins with fast simulations to efficiently explore the design space. Simulation accuracy is systematically increased with the convergence process of the SA heuristic. This improves the trade-off between speed and accuracy for simulation-based SA, as simulation cost grows quadratically for a smaller relative error. We further contribute a theoretical model that predicts the speedup achievable by this approach and demonstrate its use with an M/M/1 queue optimization problem. The results confirm that our method achieves significant computational savings while largely preserving the quality of the final solution.

title: Quantum Random Forest for the Regression Problem
authors: Kamil Khadiev, Liliya Safina
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: The Random Forest model is one of the popular models of Machine learning. We present a quantum algorithm for testing (forecasting) process of the Random Forest machine learning model for the Regression problem. The presented algorithm is more efficient (in terms of query complexity or running time) than the classical counterpart.

title: PQC-Enhanced QKD Networks: A Layered Approach
authors: Paul Spooren, Andreas Neuhold, Sebastian Ramacher, Thomas Hühn
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: We present a layered and modular network architecture that combines Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) and Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) to provide scalable end-to-end security across long distance multi-hop, trusted-node quantum networks. To ensure interoperability and efficient practical deployment, hop-wise tunnels between physically secured nodes are protected by WireGuard with periodically rotated pre-shared keys sourced via the ETSI GS QKD 014 interface. On top, Rosenpass performs a PQC key exchange to establish an end-to-end data channel without modifying deployed QKD devices or network protocols. This dual-layer composition yields post-quantum forward secrecy and authenticity under practical assumptions. We implement the design using open-source components and validate and evaluate it in simulated and lab test-beds. Experiments show uninterrupted operation over multihop paths, low resource footprint and fail-safe mechanisms. We further discuss the design's compositional security, wherein the security of each individual component is preserved under their combination and outline migration paths for operators integrating QKD-aware overlays in existing infrastructures.

title: Enabling Digital Twins for Heterogeneous Battery Systems – An Experience Report
authors: Emil Lundin, Johan Cederbladh, Martin Viker
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Lithium-ion Batteries (LiBs) are one of the more prominent examples of efficient energy storage that can be applied to a wide number of domains. One existing challenge in LiB design is the trade-offs between existing battery cell technology during design towards electrical properties, as no single cell chemistry or cell design is optimal for all operational parameters. A Heterogeneous Battery System (HEBAT) is a means of combining different battery cell technology to enable more flexible architecture in battery systems. A significant tradeoff with a HEBAT towards traditional battery design is added complexity in control and management of the battery. In this context, we propose the use of Digital Twins (DTs) to control and manage HEBATs. This paper describes the design of a HEBAT, and experiences in building custom embedded system software and hardware for management and control to enable DT operation. We demonstrate that HEBATs are a suitable application and use case for DTs, and discuss the current status of our implementation to provide lessons learned.

title: Efficient Time-Aware Partitioning of Quantum Circuits for Distributed Quantum Computing
authors: Raymond P. H. Wu, Chathu Ranaweera, Sutharshan Rajasegarar, Ria Rushin Joseph, Jinho Choi, Seng W. Loke
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: To overcome the physical limitations of scaling monolithic quantum computers, distributed quantum computing (DQC) interconnects multiple smaller-scale quantum processing units (QPUs) to form a quantum network. However, this approach introduces a critical challenge, namely the high cost of quantum communication between remote QPUs incurred by quantum state teleportation and quantum gate teleportation. To minimize this communication overhead, DQC compilers must strategically partition quantum circuits by mapping logical qubits to distributed physical QPUs. Static graph partitioning methods are fundamentally ill-equipped for this task as they ignore execution dynamics and underlying network topology, while metaheuristics require substantial computational runtime. In this work, we propose a heuristic based on beam search to solve the circuit partitioning problem. Our time-aware algorithm incrementally constructs a low-cost sequence of qubit assignments across successive time steps to minimize overall communication overhead. The time and space complexities of the proposed algorithm scale quadratically with the number of qubits and linearly with circuit depth, offering a significant computational speedup over common metaheuristics. We demonstrate that our proposed algorithm consistently achieves significantly lower communication costs than static baselines across varying circuit sizes, depths, and network topologies, providing an efficient compilation tool for near-term distributed quantum hardware.

title: From Cornfields to Canyons: Route-Specific, Physics-Based, Predictive Reliability Modeling of DC–DC Power Converters
authors: Mir Md Fahimul Islam, Mikhail Lopes, Jabir Bin Jahangir, Woongkul Lee, Muhammad A. Alam
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: The reliability of power electronic converters remains a key bottleneck in electric vehicle (EV) adoption, directly affecting drivetrain longevity and total cost of ownership. Conventional lifetime models evaluate components independently under static thermal conditions, overlooking the coupled and time-varying stresses that dominate real-world operation. In this work, a physics-based degradation framework is developed and experimentally calibrated to capture three layers of a realistic application: (i) coupled component aging induced by self-heating (SH) under shared electrical stress, (ii) thermal cross-talk (TCT) between neighboring components, and (iii) system lifetimes determined by the temperature profiles of EV trips across the U.S. Incorporating SH and TCT results in a 92% reduction in the predicted active lifetime of a DC-DC converter compared to fixed thermal stress model. Integration of the trip profile further extends model applicability, enabling climate-specific lifetime predictions across different U.S. regions. The proposed framework provides a predictive, stress and temperature-aware reliability tool for robust EV power converter design

title: Instruction-Set Architecture for Programmable NV-Center Quantum Repeater Nodes
authors: Vinay Kumar, Claudio Cicconetti, Riccardo Bassoli, Marco Conti, Andrea Passarella
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Programmability is increasingly central in emerging quantum network software stacks, yet the node-internal controller-to-hardware interface for quantum repeater devices remains under-specified. We introduce the idea of an instruction-set architecture (ISA) for controller-driven programmability of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center quantum repeater nodes. Each node consists of an optically interfaced electron spin acting as a data qubit and a long-lived nuclear-spin register acting as a control program. We formalize two modes of programmability: (i) deterministic register control, where the nuclear register is initialized in a basis state to select a specific operation on the data qubit; and (ii) coherent register control, where the register is prepared in superposition, enabling coherent combinations of operations beyond classical programmability. Network protocols are expressed as controller-issued instruction vectors, which we illustrate through a compact realization of the BBPSSW purification protocol. We further show that coherent register control enables interferometric diagnostics such as fidelity witnessing and calibration, providing tools unavailable in classical programmability. Finally, we discuss scalability to multi-electron and multi-nuclear spin architectures and connection to Linear combination of unitaries (LCU) and Kraus formulation.

title: IEEE INTELLIGENT TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS SOCIETY

title: ilLog: Incremental Learning Based Anomaly Detection from Evolving System Logs
authors: Jiyu Tian, Mingchu Li, Zumin Wang, Liming Chen, Jing Qin, Jianyuan Gan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Log anomaly detection (LAD) is of paramount importance to enhance the reliability and stability of software systems. Current state-of-the-art LAD suffers a significant performance degradation when dealing with consistently evolving log events caused by system updates. To build a reliable LAD model under the context of log data evolution, we propose an incremental learning-based method for LAD, namely ilLog, to avoid catastrophic forgetting of previously learned knowledge while continuously updating the model for better detection when processing the evolving log events. In particular, we design a novel entropy-driven sorting algorithm for real log sample replay, which enables the preservation of old knowledge via storing representative samples with discrete sequence features from previous tasks. Additionally, we introduce a Halton-based low discrepancy sequence to better approximate the sliced Cram´ er distance between the probability distributions of two models, thus enhancing the model learning capability. Based on a standard incremental learning protocol setting, we evaluate the newly proposed ilLog method on three publicly available datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves the best performance compared to SOTA LAD methods and models by applying existing IL-based methods in evolving software systems.

title: Fault Diagnosis and Fault Tolerance Method for Position Sensor in DSEG Based on Line Voltage and Position Signal
authors: Yixi Chen, Bo Zhou, Yi Lu, Yijun Zhang, Jiadan Wei
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The stable operation of controlled rectifier system in doubly salient electromagnetic generator (DSEG) is highly dependent on the reliability of position sensor. The fault of position sensor can cause commutation failure, leading to degraded power generation performance and even system instability. To address this issue, this article proposes a fault diagnosis and fault tolerance method for position sensor based on line voltage and position signal. The method accurately identifies rotor sector and estimates rotor position in real time by monitoring line voltage. By analyzing identified sector state, estimated rotor position, and position signal, fault diagnosis is performed for single, two, or three position sensors. For single or two position sensor faults, a fault tolerance method is studied based on position signal reconstruction, while for three position sensor faults, a position sensorless tolerance method is presented by utilizing line voltage. The proposed method is applicable under conditions of varying rotor speed and load, and the maximum time of error commutation is one thirty-sixth of an electrical angle period. Finally, the effectiveness of proposed method is validated on a 18/12 pole DSEG experimental platform equipped with three hall position sensors.

title: Physics-Aware Deep Reinforcement Learning for Energy and Aging Management in Electrified Powertrains
authors: Md Ragib Rownak, Wafeeq Jaleel, Athar Hanif, Muhammad Qaisar Fahim, Dat D Le, Hamza Anwar, Matthew Nelson, Qadeer Ahmed
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: This study develops an aging-aware deep reinforcement learning framework for heavy-duty electrified powertrains, optimizing fuel consumption jointly with three physics-based degradation dynamics: battery calendar and cycling aging and generator thermal aging. The discrete–continuous actions are addressed using Physics-Aware Soft Actor–Critic (PA-SAC) agents, and training stability is enhanced by a Dynamic Programming (DP)-based warm start combining behavior cloning, expert replay-buffer seeding, and critic warmup. The framework is applied to a range-extender series-hybrid architecture, with agents trained on the Heavy Heavy-Duty Diesel Truck (HHDDT) cycle and evaluated on the training cycle and two unseen test cycles under a terminal state-of-charge (SOC) constraint of [0.5480, 0.5520]. Benchmarked across five seeds per case against DP optima, Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3), and Proximal Policy Optimization with Lagrangian constraints (PPO-L), PA-SAC attains the tightest DP gap on the training cycle and lowest seed-to-seed variability. Aging objectives reshape the control strategy, reducing battery capacity degradation due to cycling aging by up to 91% relative to the fuel-only baseline and redistributing load between the battery and engine-generator subsystems under the aging-aware objective. The trained policy is deployed on an embedded controller target in a controller hardware-in-the-loop testbench, reproducing the training-time control behavior across twelve runs.

title: A Comprehensive Review of Controller Techniques for Wireless Power Transfer in Electric Vehicles Charging Applications
authors: M. Saranraj, Saravanan Kaliyaperumal, Ali Elrashidi, Kareem M. AboRas, R Narayanamoorthi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Wireless power transfer technology has emerged as a key enabler for efficient and convenient charging of electric vehicles, addressing the limitations of conventional plug-in charging systems. However, WPT systems face significant challenges, including power control, resonance frequency tuning, load regulation, and efficiency optimization. To overcome these challenges, various control strategies have been developed to enhance the stability, safety, and performance of these systems. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of different controller techniques, including proportional-integral controllers, sliding mode controllers, model predictive controllers, and adaptive control methods. It also explores optimization algorithms and hybrid approaches that aim to improve the dynamic response, robustness, and energy efficiency. By comparing the effectiveness of these control strategies, this study offers guidance to researchers and engineers in selecting the most suitable controller for specific applications in wireless charging of electric vehicles. The review concludes by highlighting future research directions and potential advancements in intelligent control strategies for next-generation wireless charging systems.

title: A Time-updated Fusion of Fast-varying and Slow-varying Signals for Health Assessment of Liquid Aerospace Engines
authors: Zhenyi Chen, Wenhao Zhang, Jianshu Yin, Jin Chen, Yanyang Zi, Hongxing Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Many time-varying state monitoring methods, such as auto-regressive moving average (ARMA) and adaptive threshold algorithm (ATA), often analyze these data streams independently, lacking a joint analysis of the multi-physics signals. Furthermore, liquid aerospace engines (LAEs) operate under multi-source loads and complex conditions, leading to difficulties in system-level assessment throughout the entire working stage. To solve these issues, this paper proposes a dynamic fusion method for fast-varying signals (FVS) (e.g., vibration and pulsating pressure) and slow-varying ones (SVS) (e.g., flow, pressure) to comprehensively assess the system-level health state. First, the concerned instantaneous frequencies of FVS are obtained by second-order synchrosqueezing transform, providing fusion indexes of FVS. Second, a time-varying upper confidence limit algorithm and a confidence interval algorithm are proposed to map structural and functional health indicators onto a unified scale for system-level, whole-stage evaluation. Finally, a dynamic fusion algorithm, where the confidence boundaries of health indicators evolve with time rather than remaining fixed, is proposed for customized nonlinear time-tracking health indexes to translate the extracted features of FVS and key features of SVS into a standardized scale, quantifying dynamic deviation for each feature from its healthy baseline, and subsequently fusing these multi-source indicators into a comprehensive health index to achieve a holistic system-level assessment. To validate its performance, two ground-running tests of LAEs are conducted. The results show that the proposed method can not only cover the whole working stage for health assessment, but is also capable of assessing structural health, functional health, and system-level health, providing an effective tool for health monitoring and assessment for LAEs.

title: In-Situ Hardware Error Detection Using Specification-Derived Petri Net Models and Behavior-Derived State Sequences
authors: Tomonari Tanaka, Takumi Uezono, Kohei Suenaga, Masanori Hashimoto
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: In hardware accelerators used in data centers and safety-critical applications, soft errors and resultant silent data corruption significantly compromise reliability, particularly when upsets occur in control-flow operations, leading to severe failures. To address this issue, we introduce two methods for monitoring control flows: a method using specification-derived Petri nets and a method using behavior-derived state transitions, targeting applications where the control flow is independent of the datapath, with the assumption of manageable operation patterns. We validated our method across four designs: convolutional layer operation, Gaussian blur, AES encryption, and a router in Network-on-Chip. Our fault injection campaign targeting the control registers and primary control inputs demonstrated high error detection rates in both datapath and control logic. Synthesis results show that a maximum detection rate is achieved with a few to around 10% area overhead in most cases. The proposed detectors quickly detect 48% to 100% of failures resulting from upsets in internal control registers and perturbations in primary control inputs. The two proposed methods were compared in terms of area overhead and error detection rate. By selectively applying these two methods, a wide range of area constraints can be accommodated, enabling practical implementation and effectively enhancing error detection capabilities.

title: Wafer-Level 1/f Noise Characterization of Scaled P-Type Nanosheets and Comparison with Planar HKMG Transistors
authors: Ruben Asanovski, Anabela Veloso, Ben Kaczer, Naoto Horiguchi, Jacopo Franco
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: We present a comprehensive wafer-level 1/f noise study of scaled Gate-All-Around (GAA) p-type nanosheet transistors and benchmark them against large-area planar High- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\kappa$</tex> Metal Gate (HKMG) devices to assess the impact of device architecture on reliability. By statistically analyzing noise data from 188 nanosheets, we extract the effective trap density in the dielectric and compare it to that of planar transistors with identical gate stack, comparable processing thermal budgets, and no specific reliability anneals. The effective trap density extracted from 1/f noise is comparable in planar and nanosheet architectures. This indicates that transitioning to GAA nanosheets does not increase noise, which remains governed by gate stack quality.

title: Contract Theory-Driven Incentive Mechanism and Client Selection for Asynchronous Federated Learning
authors: Danni Yang, Yun Ji, Zhoubin Kou, Sheng Zhang, Xiaoxiong Zhong, Fangming Liu, Jiangang Sh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Despite advantages of federated learning in distributed data processing, it still faces significant challenges related to data and system heterogeneity, client engagement willingness, and security against malicious attack. To address these issues concurrently, various incentive and client selection mechanisms have been implemented. However, existing incentive mechanisms, which are typically designed for traditional synchronous aggregation, still suffer from significant straggler issues. This study introduces a novel asynchronous federated learning framework that integrates contract theory-driven incentive mechanism and client selection mechanism to address these challenges. In incentive mechanism, we classify clients based on data quality and customize contracts accordingly. By considering factors such as time delay and test accuracy, we adaptively adjust the local model training epochs of clients to maximize utility of task publisher, thereby encouraging high-quality clients to accelerate their training processes. For client selection mechanism, we take into account model staleness, model quality, and client level within asynchronous aggregation context, which allows us to select reliable clients and enhance security of FL. Extensive experiments demonstrate effectiveness of our framework in stimulating and selecting clients within asynchronous FL settings, significantly improving both global accuracy and training time.

title: Dr. Kendra Siler: Bringing Together Technology, Processes, and People [Women to Watch]
authors: Katianne Williams
publish: IEEE Women in Engineering Magazine

title: Hot-Electron Degradation under Switching Accelerated Lifetime Testing (SALT) of 650 V E-Mode GaN HEMTs: A Novel Experimental Approach
authors: M. Dell'Andrea, M. Boito, M. Fregolent, R. Fraccaroli, I. Rossetto, C. De Santi, A. Abbisogni, M. Giuffrida, M. Ruvolo, M. E. Castagna, F. Iucolano, G. Meneghesso, E. Zanoni, M. Meneghini
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: We present a novel experimental setup for Switching Accelerated Lifetime Testing (SALT) based on a boost topology combined with a fast on-state voltage measurement clamp circuit (OVMC). This configuration was validated by investigating the dynamic on-resistance degradation kinetics of on-wafer E-mode GaN HEMTs under hard-switching (HS) operation; an exponential-like increase in R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf> was observed and ascribed to hot-electron trapping at the AlGaN/GaN interface or at the surface. The acceleration factor with voltage under hardswitching operation has been estimated for the first time in GaNbased devices. Finally, Pulsed-Drain Current Transient (P-DCT) measurements allowed to confirm hot-electron trapping as the dominant degradation mechanism: the amplitude of Ron increase extracted from P-DCT Ron transients in the semi-on state exhibited a bell-shaped dependence on gate bias, which is a hallmark signature of hot-electron effects.

title: ArtNet: Hierarchical Clustering-Based Artificial Netlist Generator for ML and DTCO Applications
authors: Andrew B. Kahng, Seokhyeong Kang, Seonghyeon Park, Dooseok Yoon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: In advanced nodes, optimization of power, performance and area (PPA) has become highly complex and challenging. Machine learning (ML) and design-technology co-optimization (DTCO) provide promising mitigations, but face limitations due to a lack of diverse training data as well as long design flow turnaround times (TAT). We propose ArtNet, a novel artificial netlist generator designed to tackle these issues. Unlike previous methods, ArtNet replicates key topological characteristics, enhancing ML model generalization and supporting broader design space exploration for DTCO. By producing realistic artificial datasets that more closely match given target parameters, ArtNet enables more efficient PPA optimization and exploration of flows and design enablements. In the context of CNN-based DRV prediction, ArtNet’s data augmentation improves F1 score by 0.16 compared to using only the original (real) dataset. In the DTCO context, ArtNet-generated mini-brains achieve a PPA match up to 97.94%, demonstrating close alignment with design metrics of targeted full-scale block designs.

title: Expanded Deep Embedding Clustering With Adversarial Learning and Adaptive Graph Constraint_supp1-3688565.pdf
authors: Jin Zhou
publish: N/A
abstract: The autoencoder (AE) is an efficient feature extraction tool that learns latent representations from raw data by minimizing the reconstruction loss. Building upon the AE architecture, deep clustering models are designed to jointly optimize the deep neural network and perform unsupervised clustering. However, existing methods directly impose the clustering objective on the latent features produced by the AE network, thereby neglecting the potential conflict between data clustering and data representation. Specifically, data clustering aims to enhance data aggregation, whereas data representation focuses on ensuring that latent features faithfully reflect the manifold structure of the raw data. To address this issue, this article proposes an innovative expanded deep embedding clustering (E-DEC) model, in which the AE network is employed to seek better latent representations, and a novel residual expansion module (REM) is integrated to construct an expanded feature space that better serves clustering tasks. Furthermore, adversarial learning between the soft cluster assignments and a prior one-hot distribution is adopted in lieu of the conventional Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence, so as to enhance the discrimination of different clusters and avoid the degeneracy problem. Finally, an entropy regularization technique is incorporated to adaptively refine the affinity graph throughout the clustering process, thereby reducing the sensitivity of clustering performance to the initial affinity graph. Extensive experiments on real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed model over state-of-the-art deep clustering methods.

title: Impact of Conductive Filament Rupture Mechanisms in RRAM on Random Telegraph Noise and Its Applications
authors: Dejiang Mu, Han Lu, Pan Liu, Yujian Zhong, Xiangshui Miao, Xingsheng Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: During the RESET process of resistive random-access memory (RRAM), the high stochasticity of conductive filament (CF) rupture exacerbates multi—bit read errors. Through 3D multi-physics simulations, this study shows that the rupture—gap location shifts with rising RESET voltage. Experimental and simulation results both confirm that this positional variation modulates the amplitude of random telegraph noise (RTN) current fluctuations. Building on these findings, we propose a method to reduce inter—bit read errors by suppressing RTN fluctuations.

title: Victim Circuits for Predicting the ESD Reliability of IO Circuits
authors: P. Sear, S. Huang, Y. Zhou, E. Rosenbaum
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: This work presents electrostatic discharge (ESD) victim circuits designed to fail similarly to real IO circuits. Circuit topology is found to affect the ESD failure level. Electrical measurements are used to identify the device that failed during the ESD testing. Simulation may be used to predict failure levels if carried out with custom ESD compact models and models of the interconnects extracted from electromagnetic simulation.

title: Modeling and Executing Defensive Deceptive Network Policies using the DEVS Formalism
authors: David Haighton, Sylvain P. Leblanc
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: Various defensive deceptive network policies have been proposed and mathematically studied in the literature. These policies show promising results as a proactive defense against network intrusions, but none have been sufficiently analyzed for potential deployment on actual networks. While some few have been evaluated through some form of simulation, many have not and virtually none have been analyzed using more realistic mechanisms. As a result, it is difficult to predict if there will be differences between the expected theoretical performance of a policy and its performance in an operational setting. This article introduces work to help network operators gain insight into the potential use of defensive deceptive network policy on their own network. The article presents a generic two-step system called the Defensive Deceptive Network Policy Empirical Evaluation System that helps a network operator gain confidence in a policy’s suitability for use in defending a given network by implementing a defensive deceptive network policy first using discrete event system specifications simulation and second using a testbed. Each of the simulation and testbed implementations produce insights into progressively more realistically representations of an operational network. The system allows a network operator to progressively invest more effort into the investigation of a defensive deceptive network policy which is much less costly than attempting deployment on the operational network under study. The system helps researchers conduct high quality empirical research on defensive deceptive network policies. The article describes how the system is validated through the implementation of a candidate deceptive defensive network policy and its evaluation against a candidate operational network.

title: Masthead
authors: N/A

title: Decoupled Per-Phase Control of a Four-Leg Grid-Feeding Inverter for Voltage Unbalance Mitigation in Low-Voltage Networks
authors: Iñaki Aizpuru, Jose Antonio Cortajarena, Estefania Planas, Iñigo Kortabarria, Iker Aretxabaleta
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The increasing integration of distributed energy resources and single-phase loads in low-voltage distribution networks introduces significant voltage unbalance, degrading power quality and compromising supply reliability. Existing four-leg inverter solutions typically rely on grid-forming operation or sequence-component decomposition, limiting their applicability in grid-connected scenarios with parallel utility supply. This paper proposes a decoupled per-phase current control strategy for a four-leg inverter operating exclusively in grid-feeding mode, which constitutes the main scientific contribution of this work. The method assigns an independent proportional-resonant controller to each phase, enabling per-phase active power regulation and bidirectional current flowwithout sequence-component transformation, ensuring stable parallel operation with the utility network. A DC bus provides the necessary voltage stiffness for current regulation. Experimental validation under diverse unbalanced operating conditions demonstrates a reduction of the voltage unbalance ratio to zero, with total harmonic distortion of injected currents remaining below 15% in all tested cases. These results confirm the suitability of the proposed framework for low-voltage networks with high penetration of distributed generation.

title: Wind Turbine Enhanced Power Recovery Through Overrating of In-Service Permanent Magnet Generators
authors: Aras Ghafoor, Judith Apsley, Siniša Djurović
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: With increasing global energy demand, extracting higher output power from in-service wind turbines is a cost-effective option for wind farm operators. This paper presents a novel strategy for Enhanced Power Recovery through Overrating (EPRO) of a permanent magnet synchronous generator (PMSG), which increases generator currents above nominal rating in the turbine’s constant power operating region. Innovative temperature sensing in the stator windings protects against overheating and field weakening control is integrated with the turbine pitch control to obtain higher current, whilst remaining within the original inverter voltage limit. Operation in the maximum power point tracking region is unchanged. A custom integrated electro-thermal model of the wind generator is used at the design stage to identify hotspots and ensure magnet and winding temperatures are not exceeded. A grid-connected, kW-scale test rig with built-in Fibre Bragg temperature sensors provides experimental validation. The EPRO strategy is compared for different dynamic wind speed profiles, with good agreement between simulation and experiment. Laboratory results demonstrate a sustained increase in generated power of up to 26% above conventional operation, whilst remaining 30°C below the insulation temperature constraint. At MW-scale, the power increase would depend on the PMSG thermal design, the required upgrade in grid interface hardware, and availability of the wind resource.

title: Efficient Approximation of Goldbach Partition Function Using Machine Learning Models
authors: Cheng-Zhe Wu, Shyong Jian Shyu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Goldbach’s conjecture, still unproven, posits that every even integer greater than 2 can be expressed as the sum of two primes. The Goldbach partition function G(n) counts the number of such prime pairs for even integer n > 2. While several analytic approximations exist, recent studies have explored machine learning approaches to estimate G(n). Building on prior architectures, we propose refined models that integrate Euler’s totient function φ(n) as a primary feature, combined with nonlinear logarithmic-exponential transformations, data exchange, residual learning, and deeper layers with increasing unit sizes. These innovations enable more accurate and stable approximations. Experimental results confirm that our models consistently outperform existing ones by achieving lower mean squared errors and Error rates. Our best-performing model not only delivers reliable accuracy but also demonstrates strong robustness across various test ranges.

title: Learning Context-Adaptive Motion Priors for Masked Motion Diffusion Models with Efficient Kinematic Attention Aggregation_supp1-3684730.mp4
authors: Jie Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: Vision-based motion capture solutions often struggle with occlusions, which result in the loss of critical joint information and hinder accurate 3D motion reconstruction. Other wearable alternatives also suffer from noisy or unstable data, often requiring extensive manual cleaning and correction to achieve reliable results. To address these challenges, we introduce the Masked Motion Diffusion Model (MMDM), a diffusion-based generative reconstruction framework that enhances incomplete or low-confidence motion data using partially available high-quality reconstructions within a Masked Autoencoder architecture. Central to our design is the Kinematic Attention Aggregation (KAA) mechanism, which enables efficient, deep, and iterative encoding of both joint-level and pose-level features, capturing structural and temporal motion patterns essential for task-specific reconstruction. We focus on learning context-adaptive motion priors-specialized structural and temporal features extracted by the same reusable architecture, where each learned prior emphasizes different aspects of motion dynamics and is specifically efficient for its corresponding task. This enables the architecture to adaptively specialize without altering its structure. Such versatility allows MMDM to efficiently learn motion priors tailored to scenarios such as motion refinement, completion, and in-betweening. Extensive evaluations on public benchmarks demonstrate that MMDM achieves strong performance across diverse masking strategies and task settings. The source code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/xxx/xxx</uri>.

title: Quantifying the Impacts of Social Media Engagement in E-Commerce Product Performance Using Decision Modelling
authors: R. Joshiga, Sharanya Jegadeesan, Kawmadi Jayasinghe, D.P. Jayathunga, Murugaiya Ramashini
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: The growth of e-commerce has made social media engagement a critical factor in shaping consumer trust and product visibility. Metrics such as likes, shares, comments, and views provide signals of consumer interest, but their measurable effect on product performance is not well established. This study examines how social media engagement influences product outcomes on Amazon’s clothing, shoes and jewellery category by integrating product ratings and review data with Facebook engagement metrics. Using Lasso regression, the analysis identifies the most influential engagement variables and evaluates their predictive power through error-based performance metrics. Results show that total reactions and shares are the strongest predictors of ratings and review counts, while comments and views contribute minimally. Among the strong predictors total reaction gave negative impact on rating while shares matric has given positive impact. The regression models achieved low prediction errors (MSE = 0.1936, RMSE = 0.44 for ratings; MSE = 0.1722, RMSE = 0.41 for review counts), demonstrating strong predictive capacity. In contrast, the binary classification model achieved balanced accuracy (82.9%) but weak discriminative power (ROC AUC = 0.51). The study contributes by integrating cross-platform datasets, applying an interpretable modelling approach, and demonstrating that social engagement can be transformed into reliable decision signals. These insights enrich both the theoretical understanding of social influence and the practical approaches used in data-driven marketing.

title: Spoof Surface Plasmon Polariton-Based Shadow Region Illumination for Wireless Networks
authors: Praneet Jain, Saptarshi Ghosh, Simon Cotton, Okan Yurduseven
publish: 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Antenna Technology (iWAT)
abstract: This work presents a Spoof Surface Plasmon Polariton (SSPP)-based waveguide that functions as a low-pass filter below 6.3 GHz. A co-planar waveguide (CPW) with flared ground planes is employed to excite the unit cells. Rectangular inserts are incorporated to ensure momentum matching between the CPW and the SSPP unit cells. After validating the performance of the planar configuration, the waveguide is bent by 90° to create a conformal architecture, exhibiting similar transmission characteristics to the original planar design. The conformal SSPP design is then employed in an example sub-6 GHz wireless blind spot illumination scenario, showing its potential as an enabling technology for improving coverage in wireless networks. The proposed structure provides a simple and cost-effective approach for feeding antenna array systems aimed at illuminating shadow regions caused by dense urban buildings.

title:
Electrically Tunable Strain Orientation in MoS
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    for Reconfigurable Logic Circuits
authors: Xinchuan Du, Yang Wang, Yi Wan
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: In this paper, we present an electrically reconfigurable logic design based on monolayer MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, in which an oriented in-plane electric field directly modulates the strain distribution and directional transport. By integrating orthogonal lateral gates and a suspended channel architecture, we enable deterministic control of charge transport through anisotropic electromechanical coupling. When the in-plane electric field is aligned with the zigzag axis of monolayer MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, the induced strain is maximized, leading to a tunable transition from linear to rectifying conduction. Building on this effect, a compact dual-device logic unit is realized, enabling flexible switching between AND and OR functionalities via electrically tunable bias, which achieves sub-100 μs switching time and operates above 100 kHz. This approach highlights field-direction programming as a scalable route toward high-density and adaptive 2D logic circuits.

title: Arab Public Opinion Perceptions on Artificial Intelligence Ethics: Digital Sovereignty, National Security, and Individual Rights
authors: Rashed Alrasheed, Ayad Mohamed Samir, Nour Naim, Dhari Alghanam, Dalal Alshammari, Dana Showaiter
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study examines Arab public opinion on the ethical implications of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in national security, focusing on digital sovereignty, civil liberties, and governance. As AI technologies rapidly integrate into security sectors, concerns arise over transparency, foreign dependence, and cultural relevance. The research aims to assess perceptions of AI’s impact on individual rights, trust in government cybersecurity, and the adequacy of current legal frameworks. Using a descriptive quantitative method, data from 572 respondents across Arab countries were analyzed. Findings reveal strong support for culturally grounded AI ethics and skepticism about foreign AI reliance. The study advocates for inclusive, culturally sensitive AI policies that prioritize transparency, and regional representation.

title: Facilitating Continuous Professional Development: A Machine Learning Approach to Personalized Learning Paths
authors: Cristian-Valentin Valea, Ovidiu Aritoni
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: In the era of rapid digital transformation, continuous professional development and skill enhancement have become imperative. This paper presents a novel software prototype designed to support certification exam preparation through personalized learning pathways powered by advanced Machine Learning techniques. The system comprises a comprehensive multi-platform ecosystem (web, mobile, and desktop applications) enabling users to complete competency assessments via adaptive multiple-choice questionnaires. The primary contribution of this work lies in the development of an automated question classification system coupled with an intelligent adaptive test generation module, implemented within a scalable and resilient software architecture. The AI-powered classifier, developed in Python, employs a multi-provider aggregation strategy that synthesizes outputs from multiple Large Language Model services-OpenAI GPT, Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini, and Cohere-augmented by dedicated APIs and conventional Natural Language Processing techniques (TF-IDF, spaCy). This hybrid approach ensures robust item analysis and precise categorization aligned with educational learning objectives. The system incorporates sophisticated fallback mechanisms, Redis-based caching, and comprehensive cost monitoring capabilities. The software architecture features a Java-based backend implementing RESTful services interfaced with a MySQL relational database. To optimize the learning experience, an adaptive test generation algorithm dynamically constructs assessments comprising 70-80 questions while respecting multiple constraints including difficulty progression, topic distribution, and examination duration. The algorithm continuously monitors individual learner performance, iteratively adjusting content delivery by detecting learning plateaus, identifying knowledge gaps, and dynamically calibrating question difficulty to maximize pedagogical effectiveness and accelerate competency acquisition.

title: Deep Learning Architectures for Real-Time Monkeypox Detection: A Comparative Study of CNNs, Transformers, and Siamese Networks
authors: Sakib Azmain Sahil, Badhan Rani Das, Hrithik Majumdar Shibu, Aisha Hayder Chowdhury
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Monkeypox (MPOX) is a zoonotic viral disease transmitted from animal hosts to humans through direct contact with the bodily fluids of infected individuals. Since its resurgence in 2022, the global outbreak has intensified, necessitating an early and accurate diagnosis along with taking tangible measures for timely intervention and containment. Clinical diagnosis is challenging due to the overlap of symptoms with other viral infections. Although the Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) test is currently construed as the gold standard for the detection of MPOX, it comes with a lot of downsides; it is expensive, time-consuming, and depends on specialized equipment and trained personnel. This paper presents a comparative study combining deep learning and vision transformers for MPOX diagnosis based on skin lesion images, facilitating recent advances in computer vision. We investigated and compared the performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Vision Transformers (ViTs), and a novel and latest Siamese Neural Network-based model on both binary classification (monkeypox vs. non-monkeypox) and multi-class classification (monkeypox, chickenpox, measles, cowpox, hand-foot-and-mouth disease, and healthy). All models were fine-tuned and evaluated using standard performance metrics. Experimental results demonstrate that CNNs provide fast and reliable baseline performance, with the ConvNeXt model scoring 99% accuracy on both types of datasets. ViTs consistently achieve the highest classification accuracy (95% and 98%), and Siamese Networks offer robust similarity-based detection capabilities with the accuracy of 99% and 98% for binary and multi-class datasets, respectively. The proposed framework demonstrates promising potential for clinical deployment, facilitating rapid, accurate, and accessible MPOX diagnosis, particularly in resource-limited settings.

title: Performance Modeling of Data Rebalancing in Sharded MongoDB Clusters
authors: Georgios Paterakis, Efstratios Ntallaris, Kostas Magoutis, Ioannis Moscholios, Georgios Bouloukakis
publish: 2026 Panhellenic Conference on Electronics &amp; Telecommunications (PACET)
abstract: Scalable storage systems typically provide mechanisms for re-distributing data over time to ensure a balanced storage allocation. Such mechanisms have traditionally been considered as background activities meant to run at low priority, to avoid penalizing applications accessing storage. In this work, we focus on an alternative design point where data re-distribution is run as a high-priority activity, meant to re-balance data at the full speed allowed by the newly added resources. Such rapid redistribution has become viable in recent years due to the rise of low-overhead technologies in the networking and disk storage space. A challenge in such a scenario is to schedule data transfer flows to newly added nodes in a way that fully and efficiently utilizes network, CPU, and disk resources in the new nodes, while avoiding overload. To understand the impact of different parameters of the rebalance process, such as the number of simultaneous senders, we develop a queueing network model of the process used in MongoDB, and describe a preliminary evaluation of performance prediction via simulations.

title: Typing Under Pressure: Estimating Cognitive Load from Keystroke Dynamics and Textual Patterns
authors: Mahmudur Rahman, Pranto Goswamee, Maria Sultana, Kamruddin Nur
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Keystroke dynamics can indicate the cognitive load, enabling real-time support without relying on intrusive sensors. Most existing methods in this area depend heavily on labeled data and tend to miss session-level patterns, such as pauses, error corrections, and overall typing speed. To address this limitation, this study focuses on engineering session-level features, such as pause-ratios, rhythm irregularity, backspace and efficiency. Data were recorded from 100 participants of 20 nationalities, who performed two distinct tasks: password-retype and transcription tasks. Using unsupervised clustering, hidden cognitive-load states were discovered, and the generated labels were later applied to train a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier. The proposed pipeline achieved an ROC-AUC of 99% and F1 of 0.900 for distinguishing the cognitive-load states. Combining unsupervised label discovery with a SVM classifier on interpretable keystroke features yields an accurate, scalable and sensor-free framework for cognitive-load detection.

title: A Comparison Between 82.16 Mhz Pseudo and Pass Transistor Logic Based 8: 1 Multiplexer in 45 Nm Process Node
authors: Mustaq Mahmud Tafhim
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: As technology advances into the deep submicron regime, efficient multiplexer (MUX) design becomes increasingly important within modern VLSI systems. This paper compares two logic design approaches for implementing an 8:1 MUX in a 45 nm CMOS process: Pass Transistor Logic (PTL) and Pseudo CMOS Logic. In order to achieve important performance metrics like propagation delay, rise/fall time, power consumption, slew rate, transistor count, layout area, and robustness across process-voltage-temperature (PVT) variations, the designs are created, simulated, and verified using Cadence Virtuoso. The 73 transistors pseudo-CMOS logicbased MUX performs well in terms of speed and signal integrity. Strong voltage swing across outputs, an exceptional slew rate of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$556.7 \text{GV} / \mathrm{s}$</tex>, and a propagation delay of 15.39ps are all attained. While the post-layout area is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$194.5 \mu \mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex>, the power consumption is still moderate at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$242 \mu W$</tex>. Strong statistical reliability and appropriateness for high-frequency, timing-critical applications like digital signal processors and embedded systems are further demonstrated by this design's stability under PVT variation and Monte Carlo simulations. On the other hand, the PTL-based MUX provides outstanding power efficiency and area optimization with just 20 transistors. It achieves an extremely low dynamic power consumption of only 389.1nW, a faster delay of 13.48ps, and an exceptionally small layout area of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30.38 \mu m^{2}$</tex>. However, it is more appropriate in ultra-low-power settings where area and energy efficiency are more important than speed because of the decreased voltage swing and weaker noise margins. The layout-versus-schematic (LVS), electrical rule check (ERC), and design rule check (DRC) requirements are all met by both designs. The implementations, which run at 82.16 MHz, show that they are feasible for use in sophisticated SoC design. The study offers insightful information for upcoming low-power and high-performance digital systems by highlighting significant trade-offs between speed, power, area, and reliability when choosing MUX architectures.

title: PruneFedAD: Federated Learning with Structured Pruning for Low-Resource Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
authors: Mizbaul Haque Maruf, Farhana Tazrin, Pronob Saha, Aritra Das, M. F. Mridha, Jungpil Shin
