title: A Feature-Driven Approach to Diabetic Retinopathy Grading with SMOTE-Based Class Balancing
authors: Shafi Mahamud, Md. Jahidul Hasan Jahid
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Diabetes is becoming more common which shows why it is important to notice and treat its complications, mainly Diabetic Retinopathy (DR), as it can cause vision loss and blindness if left unchecked. Usually, DR detection requires an ophthalmologist to study retinal images manually, creating a process that can be both tiresome for them and prone to error. The rapid growth of AI and DL makes it possible for automation of DR detection to help improve both the reliability and efficiency in making a diagnosis. In this work, we have applied different pre-trained models to get deep features from retinal images and then classify them using a Sequential neural network. A technique called Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique or SMOTE, was used to deal with class imbalance, balancing the training data. By using transfer learning along with data balancing methods, we managed to create an approach that achieved classification accuracy of 89 % and a weighted-average F1-score of 85.2 % for automated DR diagnosis where Inception V3 was used to get deep features and Sequential neural network as the classifier.

title: Room-temperature, Broadband, and Integrable Graphene Terahertz Detectors enabling Wireless Communications for 6G
authors: Zhuo Dong, Dong Wang, Kang Zhou, Boyu Dong, Keqin Tang, Liu Yang, Junwen Zhang, Hua Li, Kai Zhang
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: The contradiction between limited spectrum resources and growing data demands in wireless communication is intensifying. Terahertz (THz) band is a promising candidate for next-generation wireless communication technology, and its development requires miniaturized receivers that are high-speed, broadband, and highly sensitive. This study developed a direct and compact THz detector (area＜0.1 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) based on graphene, which achieves a broadband response (W/J band, 2.55 THz). The detector demonstrated a high sensitivity of 6.6 pW/Hz<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1/2</sup> and achieved a data transmission rate exceeding 220 Mbps. This work demonstrates the potential of graphene detectors for wireless communication.

title:
Angle-Dependent Conductivity in MoS
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Field-Effect Transistors with Bi Contacts
authors: Han-Chieh Lo, Tilo H. Yang, Edmayelle Alforja, Ming-Hao Liu, Min-Hung Lee, Yann-Wen Lan
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: The directional dependence of single crystalline molybdenum disulfide (MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) critically influences its electronic transport properties. Recent advances in contact engineering have enabled ultralow contact resistance in MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> field-effect transistors (FETs) using semi-metal bismuth (Bi) contacts. In this work, we systematically investigate the angle-dependent conductivity of Bi-contacted MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> FETs by varying the relative orientation between the electrodes and the MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> lattice. The conductivity exhibits a continuous angular dependence, reaching a maximum at 0° and minimum at 30°, with nearly a twofold enhancement at the optimal alignment. These results demonstrate that precise control of contact orientation is essential for realizing high-performance two-dimensional devices and provide valuable guidelines for designing next-generation heterogeneous integrated circuits with enhanced energy efficiency and computational speed.

title: The Effect of Financial Development on Iraq's Economic Growth
authors: Ala Fathi Assi, Zhiyar Saadi Ali Ali
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: The study examines the impact of financial development on Iraq's economic growth from 1991 to 2022, focusing on domestic credit to the private sector, money supply (M2), and inflation. Despite oil's role in Iraq's economy, the importance of financial sector development for diversification and sustainable growth has been emphasized. The Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) model was used to account for short-run dynamics and long-run cointegration effects. The findings show that domestic credit to the private sector has a significant positive effect on GDP per capita, while M2 has both positive and negative impacts due to inflationary pressures. Inflation is negatively related to growth, confirming the Mundell-Tobin effect, which suggests that high inflation makes it difficult to balance savings investment. These findings highlight the need for specific financial sector reforms in Iraq, such as improved credit allocation mechanisms and inflation-stabilizing policies. The study contributes to the existing literature on the relationship between finance and growth in the Iraqi economy and offers policy implications for other resource-based economies.

title: Under-FET Buried PN Junction T-Sensor for Thermal Management in 22nm FDSOI
authors: Weiquan Hao, Zijin Pan, Xunyu Li, Runyu Miao, Zijian Yue, Zexiao Li, Albert Wang
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: Modern integrated circuits (IC) suffer from severe self-heating, limiting chip performance and reliability, while complicating systems due to cooling. On-chip thermal management is highly desirable, which however remains impractical due to poor chip-level temperature mapping (T-mapping) limited by existing coplanar temperature sensors (T-sensor). This paper reports the first under-FET T-sensor using buried PN junction structure in commercial CMOS technologies. Demonstrated in a foundry 22nm FDSOI technology, the T-sensor prototype utilizes triple-well structure, native to the FDSOI, to build diode temperature sensors. Simulation and measurement show advantages of new under-FET diode T-sensor over its coplanar counterparts. The prototype under-FET T-sensors achieve high sensitivity that is more than two times higher than coplanar sensors (0.165 K/mW to 0.496 K/mW), in measurement, while saving ~90% of layout area. The new under-FET T-sensor will enable runtime full-chip T-mapping with spatial resolution down to transistor level and potentially revolutionize dynamic thermal management for future chips and systems.

title: Hybrid Relay-Reflecting Intelligent Surface-Assisted Covert Communication for Satellite-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Jiayan Li, Yan Liu, Ranran Sun, Weidong Yang, Yulong Shen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Satellite Internet (SAT-NET)
abstract: This paper investigates a hybrid relay-reflecting intelligent surface (HR-RIS) assisted covert communication scheme for a satellite-terrestrial network comprising a geostationary Earth orbit (GEO) satellite Alice, a terrestrial legitimate receiver Bob, a terrestrial warden Willie, and an HR-RIS deployed on a building facade. By combining reflective and amplifying elements, the HR-RIS strengthens the legitimate link while suppressing detectability at Willie, which enhances covertness and alleviates severe path loss. Building on channel modeling for satellite-terrestrial and HR-RIS links, we formulate a joint optimization of satellite transmit power and HR-RIS configuration under practical power and covertness constraints, and propose an alternating optimization algorithm to solve it effectively. Simulation results demonstrate that HR-RIS achieves higher covert rates than passive RIS and approaches fully active RIS with reduced power consumption and hardware complexity.

title: First Design Proposal of Capacitor-less DRAM Array with Feedback-enhanced Self-refresh and Crosstalk Suppression
authors: Anzhi Yan, Enyi Zhang, Jingkai Song, Houfang Liu, Yi Yang, Tian-Ling Ren
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: The amorphous oxide semiconductor (AOS)-based two-transistor-zero-capacitor (2T0C) dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) has emerged as a promising candidate for next-generation memory technology due to its elimination of trench capacitors, non-destructive readout capability, and potential non-volatility. However, inherent challenges including capacitive coupling and crosstalk have hindered its implementation in array configurations with demonstrated memory functionality. Capacitive coupling induces data disturbances during write/read operations, while crosstalk generates low-resistance pathways between read word lines, triggering sharp current surges, driver overstress, and subsequent readout errors. To address these limitations, this study presents a novel 1 kbit 2T1d DRAM array architecture, integrating complementary cells, rectifier diodes, and feedback-enhanced self-refresh modules. Comprehensive circuit simulation and functional verification demonstrate that both the proposed cell structure and array design successfully execute essential DRAM operations, while also exhibiting scalability for manufacturing implementation. The effective mitigation of capacitive coupling and crosstalk in this array-level realization highlights the viability of 2T1d DRAM technology for embedded memory applications and marks a significant advancement toward practical implementation of capacitor-less DRAM.

title: Real-Time Plant Disease Detection in Smart Agriculture Using a YOLO11-Based Deep Learning Model
authors: Md. Mostafuzur Rahaman Nafiz, Md. Asraful Islam, Sharfuddin Mahmood
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Plant diseases are one of the biggest challenges to agriculture. They reduce the quality of the produce, reduce yield, and can lead to economic losses that are substantial. Early and accurate detection of diseases is very important as this helps farmers to respond quickly and not suffer huge losses. Traditional methods are dependent on visual observation, which is prone to being fatiguing, time-consuming, and inconsistent at the scale necessary for agriculture. Deep learning methods are faster and more consistent. Here, we adopted the YOLO11x model for detection of disease in four crucial crops, i.e., eggplant, chili, potato, and tomato. The model is trained and tested on a sizeable data set that includes diverse choices of plant disease images. We implemented data augmentation to make the system more resilient under diverse field conditions such as lighting and overlapping leaves. The model achieved a significant improvement in disease classification, reaching an accuracy of 99%. The results show that YOLO11x can potentially be an efficient plant disease detection system. It can promote precision agriculture and smart agriculture by helping farmers detect potential problems before they arise. The system can reduce damage to crops, optimize food production, and promote sustainable farming.

title: A Blockchain-Based Framework for Secure and Efficient KYC Using Hyperledger Fabric and IPFS
authors: Mohd. Sultan Ahammad, Mahmudul Hasan, Amit Karmaker
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Enterprise applications such as insurance services require data sharing in an anonymous, selective, and tamper-proof manner. Validating the Know Your Customer (KYC) information of clients is one example where customers share personal data. The insurance company verifies customer identity and assesses the risks associated with each client. However, the current KYC process is often redundant, inefficient, and costly for both insurance companies and customers. Blockchain technology offers a suitable solution to ensure secure and transparent transactions between multiple parties, possessing the necessary capabilities to meet enterprise application requirements. Furthermore, clients should retain control over their shared KYC data, and the relationships between customers and their insurance companies should remain hidden from other insurers. In this work, we propose a blockchain-based KYC system that leverages the InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) to store KYC documents and employs smart contracts on the Hyperledger Fabric network to enable anonymous sharing of this information among insurance providers. The proposed method also empowers customers to manage access to their data. Additionally, two smart contract protocols are introduced to ensure that documents and consent records cannot be forged. The proposed solution promises significant cost savings, enhanced customer experience, and improved process efficiency.

title: A Multi-Dataset and Manual Annotation-Driven Framework for Accurate Blood Cell Detection with YOLO and GELAN-E Architectures
authors: Md. Sakib Bin Islam, Shamima Sultana Ridoy, Shahneela Shailee, Rafia Jannat, Akid Ornob
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Manual blood cell counting is labor-intensive, error-prone, and highly dependent on technician expertise, while automated hematology analyzers, although accurate, remain costly and inaccessible in resource-limited settings. To address this gap, we propose a deep learning-based framework for blood cell detection and classification using YOLO and GELAN-E architecture. Our method integrates seven publicly available blood smear datasets with manual annotations, enabling simultaneous classification of eight blood cell types, including red blood cells, platelets, five white blood cell subtypes, and abnormal leukocytes associated with leukemia. Initial training and optimization were performed with YOLOv5 to refine dataset size, preprocessing strategies, and hyperparameters, followed by deployment on YOLOv9c and GELAN-E. The YOLOv9c model achieved a precision of 94.6%, recall of 92.8%, mAP50 of 97.1%, and mAP50-95 of 75.5%, while GELAN-E achieved a precision of 95%, recall of 94%, mAP50 of 97%, and mAP50-95 of 75%, demonstrating superior generalizability across diverse imaging conditions. EigenCAM visualizations confirmed that the models captured critical morphological features for accurate classification. Overall, this framework provides a cost-effective, scalable, and accurate solution for AI-driven hematological analysis with strong potential for integration into mobile and point-of-care diagnostic systems, especially in low-resource healthcare environments.

title:
Enhancing Ferroelectric Performance of Al
                    <sub>0.7</sub>
                    Sc
                    <sub>0.3</sub>
                    N Capacitors through Substrate Engineering
authors: Yuan Fang, Haoji Qian, Xiaoxi Li, Jiawei Li, Cizhe Fang, Xiangyu Zeng, Tao Wu, Chengji Jin, Jiajia Chen, Yi’an Ding, Yan Liu, Genquan Han
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: In this work, we investigated the impact of different substrates on the ferroelectric properties of Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.7</inf>Sc<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.3</inf>N capacitors. The Si-based device exhibited superior performance compared with those on Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> and Ga<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> substrates. Specifically, it achieved a remanent polarization (2P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf>) of 366 μC/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> compared with 257 μC/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> for the Ga<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> device, and its coercive field was reduced by 0.12 MV/cm. Endurance measurements further revealed stable switching beyond 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sup> cycles with low leakage, while the Ga<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>-based device broke down after ~2×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup> cycles. These improvements are attributed to the stronger c-axis orientation and smaller lattice constant c of 4.95 Å for the Si-based devices. These results demonstrate that substrate engineering is an effective strategy to lower switching barriers and leakage currents, enabling reliable low-voltage operation of wurtzite ferroelectric devices for next-generation memory and logic applications.

title: Sinhala Speech Recognition System for Speech-Based Autism Intervention in Children Using the NAO Robot
authors: Heshani Bopage, Koliya Pulasinghe, Samantha Rajapaksha
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: This research focuses on the development of a Sinhala speech recognition engine tailored to identify the language content of conversations with children. The engine leverages machine learning algorithms and natural language processing (NLP) techniques to transcribe and classify speech in Sinhala. Key features include an acoustic model optimized for the nuances of Sinhala phonetics and a language model trained on datasets encompassing texts of child-directed speech. The system evaluates linguistic aspects to assess the appropriateness of content and engagement levels in child-centric dialogues. By addressing challenges such as phoneme variation and informal conversational patterns, the system aims to enhance the understanding and facilitation of Sinhala-based child interactions, promoting effective communication and developmental support.

title: Explainable Predictions of Preterm Birth: A LIME-Supported Ensemble Framework
authors: Mohammad Tarek Aziz, Renzon Daniel Cosme Pecho, Nahida Zakir, Md Saharab Hossain, Akba Ull Hasna Era, Nayeem Uddin Ahmed Khan, Nasrin Akter Srabony, Sevara Sadullayeva, Odamova Ugiljon, Valisher Sapayev Odilbek Uglu, Tanjim Mahmud
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Predicting preterm birth remains a critical medical challenge, as early identification can significantly reduce health risks for both mothers and newborns. In this study, we investigate the effectiveness of machine learning techniques for accurately predicting preterm birth. A comprehensive exploratory data analysis (EDA) was first conducted using correlation analysis, feature distribution visualizations, and pie charts to better understand and preprocess the dataset. After data normalization, several classifiers—including Random Forest, AdaBoost, Decision Tree, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes, Neural Network, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and XGBoost—were applied for prediction. Among the individual models, KNN and Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy of 99%. An ensemble learning approach further improved the performance, reaching a maximum accuracy of 99.14%. To enhance model transparency and clinical interpretability, the Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) technique was employed to explain individual predictions. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach can reliably predict preterm birth and has strong potential to assist healthcare professionals in early risk assessment and decision-making.

title: Multi-Threaded Recast-Based A* Pathfinding for Scalable Navigation in Dynamic Game Environments
authors: Tiroshan Madushanka, Sakuna Madushanka
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: While the A* algorithm remains the industry standard for game pathfinding, its integration into dynamic 3D environments faces trade-offs between computational performance and visual realism. This paper proposes a multi-threaded framework that enhances standard A* through Recast-based mesh generation, Bezier-curve trajectory smoothing, and density analysis for crowd coordination. We evaluate our system across ten incremental phases, from 2D mazes to complex multi-level dynamic worlds. Experimental results demonstrate that the framework maintains 350+ FPS with 1000 simultaneous agents and achieves collision-free crowd navigation through density-aware path coordination.

title: Heterogeneous Bit- and Channel-Aware Mapping Framework for Area-Efficient and Robust RRAM-based CIM
authors: Zecheng Pan, Jiyang Cao, Zhoujie Pan, Yuwan Hong, He Tian, Yi Yang, Tianling Ren
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: Resistive random-access memory (RRAM)-based computing-in-memory (CIM) offers massive parallelism for neural network acceleration but suffers from severe accuracy degradation and resource inefficiency under device nonidealities. This work presents a heterogeneous bit- and channel-aware hybrid inference framework that simultaneously optimizes precision allocation and array utilization for robust and area-efficient RRAM-CIM. The proposed method introduces a two-level heterogeneous mapping mechanism: (1) bit significance–aware mapping that assigns the most significant bits (MSBs) to robust binary memristor crossbars (BXBs) and the least significant bits (LSBs) to compact multi-level crossbars (MXBs); and (2) a channel-importance–aware allocation that dynamically prioritizes high-impact convolutional channels with more reliable hardware resources. Moreover, a tunable resource–accuracy balancing algorithm enables flexible operation modes—accuracy-prioritized, area-prioritized, and balanced—for diverse deployment scenarios. Comprehensive simulations based on an extended MNSIM platform demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves up to 50% area reduction with less than 0.5% accuracy loss and maintains robust inference under nonlinearity and variation, significantly outperforming conventional homogeneous CIM schemes. This heterogeneous mapping strategy establishes a scalable pathway toward hardware-adaptive and reliability-aware CIM architectures for next-generation edge intelligence.

title: An Efficient Distributed Framework for Top-k Dominating Query Over Uncertain Databases
authors: Md. Safwan Zaher Asif, K. M. Azharul Hasan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Probabilistic top-k dominating (PTD) queries are essential for retrieving the most influential objects from uncertain data, but executing them over large datasets is computationally expensive. This work presents a distributed framework that evaluates PTD queries in a Hadoop environment by combining a filtering stage with a lightweight refinement step. The framework introduces a dynamic candidate pruning strategy that exploits lower and upper bounds to eliminate weak objects early, and an aggregated emission strategy that reduces redundant intermediate data during MapReduce execution. These methods improve query efficiency while preserving exact results. The proposed framework was tested on two datasets, a synthetic smartphone collection and a Bangladesh road dataset derived from OSM geometries, and the results show that the wall clock time was reduced by 34.2% on the synthetic dataset and 24.7% on the real dataset, while the communication cost remained unchanged. Communication cost remained unchanged as output cardinality was kept identical to the baseline for fair comparison. These improvements confirm that the framework works effectively across different uncertain domains, demonstrating that pruning and emission aggregation can cut unnecessary computation and data transfer and making the approach practical for large-scale probabilistic query processing in distributed environments.

title: BnSFD: YOLOv12-Based Detection of Bangladeshi Street Foods for Cultural Heritage
authors: Ahammad Nafiz, Mohammad Saidul Islam, Abu Anas, Moshiur Rahman Sabbir, Abid Hasan, Khushnur Binte Jahangir, Shoib Ahmed Shourav, Md. Tarek Hasan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Current computer vision systems tend to focus on Western foods, leaving many traditional South Asian dishes out of the picture. That means popular Bangladeshi street foods like Singara, Peyaju, and Beguni often go unrecognized by automated tools. To address this gap, we developed a new real-time detection method using an improved version of YOLO (YOLOv12m). Our first step was creating a robust dataset: we gathered and labeled 3,988 high-quality images covering 13 traditional Bangladeshi food categories. In this study, we focused on three especially challenging ones: Singara, Peyaju, and Beguni. After optimizing the model, we achieved an average detection accuracy of 87.7% on these classes, while running smoothly at 151 frames per second. We also added image enhancement and refined the model to handle real-world variations in lighting, background, and food presentation. This work isn't just about better accuracy, it's about making technology more inclusive. By recognizing diverse cuisines, our system can help with nutrition tracking, support cultural preservation, and move toward computer vision that truly sees the richness of global food traditions.

title: AI-Powered Protection of Energy and Flexibility Scheduling in Interconnected Local Energy Networks Across Automation Cyber-Physical System
authors: Abolfazl Nazari, Ali Riki, Amir Abdollahi, Mahdi Ramezani Karim
publish: 2026 International Conference on Protection and Automation of Power Systems (IPAPS)
abstract: The growing reliance on renewable energy resources and digital communication technologies in modern smart grids requires operational strategies that are not only fast and reliable but also robust against cyber threats targeting automation infrastructures. As digitalization increases, both software-based and hardware-based components of cyber-physical automation systems become potential entry points for attacks that can compromise the accuracy of energy and flexibility scheduling. Local energy networks (LENs) are established to integrate distributed energy resources and improve system reliability, yet their dependence on automated sensing, communication, and control devices makes them vulnerable to data manipulation. This study introduces a cyber-secure scheduling platform for interconnected local energy networks (ILENs) that explicitly addresses these vulnerabilities. A bilevel scheduling framework is developed in which the central operator coordinates energy and flexibility exchanges, while local operators optimize their internal operation. To assess and enhance the cybersecurity of the automation system, false data injection (FDI) attacks—representing manipulation of software-based metering processes and potential exploitation of hardware interfaces—are modeled using a Gaussian probability distribution. A machine-learning-based defense mechanism, employing an XGBoost-driven deviation-bound detection and correction scheme, is proposed to protect the automation system from such attacks. The method demonstrates a maximum detection accuracy of 91.67 percent, and its corrective actions restore scheduling performance to conditions closely aligned with pre-attack results. This confirms the approach's ability to reinforce both software and hardware cybersecurity within automated energy management systems.

title: Deep Learning-Based Classification of Sugarcane Leaf Diseases Using ResNet101 for Agricultural Sustainability
authors: Karamala Naveen, M.D.Raj Kamal, S. Kaliappan, Manishka Chitra
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This research presents a deep learning approach using the ResNet101 model to classify the sugarcane leaf diseases into five classes: Some of the diseases include Healthy, Mosaic, Red Rot, Rust, and Yellow Disease. A dataset of 2,569 images was used for training, validation, and testing, coming from Kaggle and having a stratified split ratio of 70:15:15. To enhance the generalization ability of the model, resizing and normalization of data were performed to preprocess the data, while flipping, rotating, and adjusting the brightness were also done to augment the data. The model achieved an overall accuracy of 86.92% and high precision, recall, and F1 scores for most classes. Nonetheless, some difficulty was encountered in differentiating the Mosaic disease because of the similarity in symptomatology. The study is focused on the use of transfer learning to solve agricultural problems. Possible future work includes the analysis of larger datasets and the inclusion of geographic diversity, multi-label classification, and incorporation into mobile applications for real-time use in the field. From the observations made in this research, there is evidence that deep learning has the capability to revolutionize the management of diseases in sugarcane farming.

title: From Impulse to Introspection: Two-Round Peer-Aware Architectures for Large Language Model Trading Agents
authors: Jung-Hua Liu, Yiqing Ma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: This study introduces a two-round prediction-execution architecture for large language model (LLM) trading agents, separating prediction and execution to improve decision quality. Tested across four LLMs on cryptocurrency markets, the framework reduced overtrading and exposed systematic overconfidence and herding. Findings highlight implications for consensus design and confidence calibration in AI decision systems.

title: MobileNetV4-Conv-M for Mushroom Safety: Leakage-Free Augmentation and High-Accuracy Edible-vs-Poisonous Classification
authors: Avani Mittal, Sneha, N. Lakshmi, A. Anto Spiritus Kingsly, J. Mahil, Ashish Kumar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Accurate identification of poisonous mushrooms is a safety-critical problem often limited by the lack of labeled data. This study constructs a relatively small, reproducible pipeline centered around MobileNetV4-Conv-M, including leakage-free preprocessing, stratified 85:15 splitting, and train-only augmentation for better generalization on a small Kaggle image set (edible vs. poisonous). Transfer learning is used with AdamW & cosine scheduling, and five-view test-time augmentation is used for inference. The system achieves 95.63% peak validation accuracy (minimum val loss 0.0576) and test accuracy of 92.31%, macro-F1 = 0.923, and balanced accuracy = 92.86%. The confusion matrix shows that the poisoning predictions have no false positives and one false negative, reflecting conservative decisions regarding toxicity with high specificity. The protocol, including duplicate control, class weighting, and thresholdable probabilistic outputs, provides a practical pattern for tiny-sample, risk-sensitive image classification. Findings support the deployment of these systems on resource-constrained devices and advocate expanding them to large, specimen-diverse corpora and to calibrated, risk-aware decision rules.

title: Adaptive Modality Gating: Explainable, Confidence-Guided Fusion for Source-Only Zero-Shot Deepfake Detection
authors: Kalyan Kanti Halder, Joy Debnath, Md Abdul Masud
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Deepfakes often keep real speech while altering facial motion (and vice versa), which can fool detectors trained on a single dataset. We propose Adaptive Modality Gating (AMG), a per-clip, confidence-guided fusion module that weights visual, geometric, and optional audio cues. Here, adaptive means clipwise gating, not domain adaptation. We add two general temporal signals: (i) how smoothly landmarks move over time, and (ii) how much the visual embedding changes frame to frame. A lightweight gate decides, per clip, which cues to trust: it boosts audio when manipulation is likely (e.g., voice-conversion artifacts or A/V mismatch) and down-weights audio when it is not informative (e.g., real/flat audio as in DeepfakeTIMIT). We train and select on source datasets only and evaluate zero-shot on targets, using per-clip normalization so no target statistics leak in. AMG also reports per-clip weights, offering a clear explanation of each decision. This simple, confidence-guided, per-clip choice of when to trust audio versus visual cues helps transfer across datasets, while a tiny gate suppresses non-diagnostic streams to improve robustness without target statistics. In short, we propose a simple, per-clip, confidence-guided gating scheme that uses generic temporal cues to decide when to trust audio versus visual streams. In our survey, this lightweight pairing of reliability cues with exported fusion weights as the explanation is rarely emphasized, and it improves zero-shot cross-dataset transfer without target statistics.

title: A Feedback-Driven Machine Learning Framework for Teaching Mode Decision Support in Higher Education Using Module Characteristics
authors: P.A. Janendra Chamodi, Dinesh Asanka
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: The post-pandemic expansion of online and blended learning has intensified the need for systematic decision-support mechanisms in higher education. Universities are increasingly required to determine appropriate teaching modes for academic modules while balancing student engagement, pedagogical effectiveness, and resource constraints. However, delivery-mode decisions are often guided by intuition or retrospective evaluations rather than structured, data-driven analysis. This study presents a feedback-driven machine learning framework designed to support teachingmode decision making in higher education. The framework integrates comparative student feedback with structured module characteristics to provide advisory recommendations on whether a module is more aligned with online or physical delivery. A dataset comprising 245 undergraduate survey responses across 32 academic modules was analyzed, incorporating pedagogical variables such as module type, credit count, interaction level, assessment type, semester, and academic year. Supervised learning models, including Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost, were evaluated. Experimental results indicate that module-level pedagogical characteristics contribute meaningfully to differentiating teaching-mode suitability, with the XGBoost model demonstrating the strongest overall performance (Accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=63.3 \%$</tex>, Macro-F1 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.58$</tex>, AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.63$</tex>). Rather than emphasizing predictive accuracy as a standalone objective, the study positions machine learning as a decision-support tool that complements academic planning processes. The proposed framework enables transparent, feedback-informed, and context-sensitive teaching-mode planning, particularly relevant for resource-constrained higher education systems.

title: Sentiment Analysis in Bangla E-Commerce Reviews: An Aspect Based Approach
authors: Jahidul Islam Rayhan, Ayesha Siddika, Md Nazir Ahmed, Fahim Shakil Tamim, Akibul Islam, Shahinur Alam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: When it comes to learning what consumers actually think about goods and services when they shop online, customer reviews are crucial. Despite being the seventh most spoken language with more than 284 million speakers, Bangla has lagged behind major languages like English and Chinese, which have seen tremendous advancements in sentiment analysis due to abundant data and excellent tools. We developed BEABSA, a brand-new dataset designed especially for Bangla E-commerce Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis, to address this problem. 4,300 carefully chosen Bangla reviews from well-known Bangladeshi websites like Pickaboo and Daraz are included in this collection. Importantly, each review has been skillfully classified by native speakers using sentiment tags that correspond to particular aspects of the product. We tested a variety of analytical techniques on BEABSA to demonstrate the value of this data, ranging from deep learning (CNN) to sophisticated Transformer-based models, and from conventional machine learning (such as LR, KNN, and SVM). Results show that while CNN (68%) and SVM (62 %) produce acceptable results, transformer models show notable gains. Bangla-BERT greatly outperforms all baseline models, achieving an accuracy of 82 % and an F1-score of 81 %. These results highlight transformer architectures' capacity to comprehend contextual significance in Bangla and set a strong benchmark for future BEABSA research.

title: Advances in Developing Metal Structures Suppressing Global Flying Crosstalk in CMOS
authors: Albert Wang, Zijin Pan, Weiquan Hao, Xunyu Li, Runyu Miao, Zijian Yue
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: Global flying noises emerge as a dominant crosstalk problem for complex IC chips, particularly when various in-substrate noise-isolation techniques have been routinely used to efficiently block the crosstalk throughout a chip substrate. This paper provides an overview of recent advances in developing novel metal structures in CMOS back end of line (BEOL) stack to efficiently suppress global flying crosstalk on a chip.

title: Real-Time Earthquake Magnitude Classification from Initial P-Waves: Models, Dataset, and Comparative Analysis for South Asia
authors: Md Nasiat Hasan Fahim, Md. Abid Ullah Muhib, Rayhanul Amin Tanvir, Abdullah Al Noman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Rapid earthquake magnitude estimation is crucial for effective early warning systems that can save lives and reduce economic damage. In this paper, we present a comprehensive study of magnitude classification using only the vertical component of the initial 7 -second <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{P}$</tex>-wave window from a single station. We compare six machine learning approaches that range from traditional models to state-of-the-art deep learning architectures. We also curated a novel dataset of 7,318 earthquake events in South Asia. The dataset was categorized into five Richterscale classes: slight (3.0-3.9), light (4.0-4.9), moderate (5.0-5.9), strong (6.0-6.9) and severe (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq 7.0$</tex>). Our experiments show that deep learning models substantially outperform traditional approaches. Our Transformer based architecture achieved 76.23% standard accuracy and 81.56 % adaptive accuracy with 4.8 ms inference latency. The adaptive-accuracy metric is introduced for the inherent uncertainty in magnitude estimation of near class boundaries. These results indicate that the attention mechanisms in Transformers combined with adaptive classification effectively capture the temporal dynamics of seismic signals. The architectural advantage facilitates promising generalization to rare high-magnitude events, despite the inherent data scarcity characteristic of seismic catalogs. The adaptive accuracy provides a more realistic assessment of model performance, and the result suggests viability for real-time deployment.

title: An AI-Driven Approach for User-Personalized Interior Color Matching Using Room Complexity and Visual Context
authors: A.H.T.N. Premachandra, A.N. Warnasooriya, A.M.C.B. Adikari, D.M.I.P.B. Gunasinghe, S.P.D.M.V. Dharmasinghe, K.A.S.H. Kulathilake
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Interior color selection plays a critical role in shaping the aesthetic quality and psychological comfort of indoor spaces. However, most existing interior design tools rely on predefined color palettes or rule-based systems that fail to consider individual user preferences, room characteristics, and existing visual context simultaneously. As a result, color recommendations often lack personalization and practical relevance. This paper proposes an AI-driven framework for user-personalized interior color matching that integrates multiple complementary models into a unified system. The framework integrates a fuzzy logic-based room complexity analysis, user preference clustering, color and texture detection, and a color recommendation strategy to generate context-aware and personalized color palettes. Room complexity is quantified using area-based, entropy-based, and visual clutter metrics, which are fused through a fuzzy logic decision system. User preferences are collected through a structured questionnaire and grouped using K-means clustering to identify dominant preference patterns. In parallel, computer vision techniques are applied to detect wall regions, identify textures, and extract dominant wall colors from interior images. The outputs of these models are integrated within a color recommendation module that applies color harmony principles while adapting color vibrancy based on room complexity and user cluster characteristics. A lightweight real-time visualization approach is employed to allow users to preview recommended colors directly on room images without relying on hardware-intensive augmented reality technologies. Experimental evaluations and user studies confirm improved user satisfaction (84 % realism, 88 % preference) and efficient mobile performance (1.7 s per image) for the proposed personalized color recommendation framework. This research highlights the potential of combining user modeling, environmental analysis, and visual context to advance AI-assisted interior design systems.

title: Predictive Modeling of LED Luminaire Degradation Using Artificial Neural Networks and Environmental Input
authors: Pavol Belany, Zuzana Florkova, Peter Hrabovsky, Roman Budjac
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: One of the key requirements in the design of lighting systems is the long-term reliability of LED luminaires. This aspect is particularly emphasized in smart buildings and infrastructure, where maintenance must be optimized both economically and functionally. Despite the excellent properties of LEDs in terms of energy efficiency and lifetime, their components inevitably degrade over time due to thermal, electrical and environmental stresses. This leads to a gradual decrease in luminous and photometric performance. This paper proposes a hybrid predictive framework to estimate the degradation of LEDs by calculating a wear index based solely on indirect inputs such as daylight intensity, daily energy consumption and operating time. In this work, two multilayer artificial neural network models were developed: one to predict daily energy consumption, the other to estimate the average operating time of luminaires. The outputs of these models are then used to calculate the wear index using a derived formula based on the principles of physical degradation. Experimental validation using real measurement data shows that the proposed models achieve high predictive accuracy with relative errors of around 5% for partial parameters and below 10% for the final estimate of the wear index. The results presented in the paper confirm the suitability of models based on artificial neural networks for predicting the degradation of LED diodes without the need for direct operational monitoring or measurement of operating time. The proposed approach enables the implementation of cost-effective predictive maintenance strategies, which allows for the early identification of luminaires approaching the end of their functional life. This method supports the concept of proactive lighting management in smart environments, ensuring visual comfort, operational continuity and extended system sustainability. This approach eliminates the need for direct luminaire runtime monitoring, significantly reducing system complexity and operational costs.

title: Model Persistence in Distributed Environments: Change-Based Encoding on Blockchain
authors: Marco Scappa, Juri Di Rocco, Alfonso Pierantonio
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Change-Based Persistence (CBP) is a mechanism used in Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) tools to record the evolution of models as sequences of fine-grained change events. CBP models are typically stored as local XML files, making them vulnerable to accidental corruption, loss, or intentional tampering. This paper introduces COBRA, a blockchain-backed persistence approach that ensures integrity, immutability, and long-term auditability of CBP histories. COBRA serializes model change events into blockchain-compatible transactions and stores each modeling session as an immutable block on a permissioned Hyperledger Fabric network. The approach is agnostic to any specific modeling language or notation: any EMF-based model represented as change events can be persisted and reconstructed. COBRA also implements duplicate detection and chunked storage to handle large histories efficiently. We provide a complete encoding/decoding pipeline together with an empirical evaluation measuring execution time and energy consumption under different workloads. Results show that COBRA preserves full recoverability of model histories while providing trustworthy, tamper-resistant storage with acceptable computational overhead.

title: Dual-Branch Attentional Multi-Fusion Network with Uncertainty Quantification and Explainable AI for Skin Cancer Classification
authors: Ankita Saha, Fazlay Rabby, Ayetullah Al Khomane, Nusrat Hasan, Mou Das
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Skin cancer remains one of the most prevalent cancers worldwide, and early and accurate diagnosis is essential for reducing mortality. This study introduces AMF-Net, a novel dual-branch attentional multi-fusion network introducing a three-tier, uncertainty-based decision framework, directly addressing a critical gap in clinical readiness. Our unique framework integrates EfficientNet-B3 and ResNet-50 with enhanced CBAM attention and a new multi-component focal loss. The model was trained on a diverse 12,577-image dataset, including a newly collected hospital dataset, which significantly enhances clinical applicability beyond public benchmarks. Experimental results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance with 96.47% accuracy and ROC-AUC scores exceeding 0.90 across all seven lesion classes. Calibrated uncertainty quantification via Monte Carlo Dropout enables our three-tier system (automated, expert-assisted, expert-driven), mirroring real-world clinical workflows and ensuring patient safety. Grad-CAM++ visualizations further improve interpretability by highlighting clinically relevant features validated by dermatologists. An ablation study confirms the superiority of our dual-branch fusion design. AMF-Net's unique combination of high accuracy, reliability, and explainability makes it a highly promising and clinically applicable candidate for deployment in real-world dermatology settings.

title: A Generative Bangla Question Answering System Using Large Language Model for University Admission Related Frequently Asked Questions
authors: Srijita Dhar, Hasan Murad, Aninda Roy Dhruba
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Accurate dissemination of information to Bangladeshi candidates for university admission depends on frequently asked questions (FAQs). This endeavour aims to solve frequent questions about admissions to help students by introducing a generative question-answering system. Low-resource languages like Bangla specifically lack sufficient datasets, especially in fields like university admissions. As such, Bangla question-answer pairs related to university admissions lack a comprehensive dataset. The data set consists of 5000 Bangla question-answer pairs derived from university prospectuses, social media groups, and first-year candidates. Three categories define the dataset of general, public, and private universities. We have utilized the exceptional natural language understanding of the unsloth LLama, Qwen, Gemma, Mistral & DeepSeek, and TinyLlama model. Our evaluation using Bert-Score, BERT-based Semantic Metrics, Partial, and Fuzzy Match Performance, MRR, and Hit Rate, and METEOR score shows that the system routinely generates relevant and accurate findings. We prioritized DeepSeek and LLaMA over other models because they consistently delivered better evaluation scores across key metrics. DeepSeek had the edge over LLaMA in most metrics, Mean Reciprocal Rank (0.9870 vs. 0.9850), Hit Rate@1 (0.9741 vs. 0.9701), partial and fuzzy match accuracy, and average METEOR score, and LLaMA, meanwhile, slightly outperformed on BERT-based precision. Basically, we choose DeepSeek for better retrieval, ranking, and semantic understanding, or LLaMA for more comprehensive text generation and precision-oriented tasks. As a result, these two models received higher priority in our experiments and analysis.

title: Persistent Human Feedback, LLMs, and Static Analyzers for Secure Code Generation and Vulnerability Detection
authors: Ehsan Firouzi, Mohammad Ghafari
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Existing literature heavily relies on static analysis tools to evaluate LLMs for secure code generation and vulnerability detection. We reviewed 1,080 LLM-generated code samples, built a human-validated ground-truth, and compared the outputs of two widely used static security tools, CodeQL and Semgrep, against this corpus. While 61% of the samples were genuinely secure, Semgrep and CodeQL classified 60% and 80% as secure, respectively. Despite the apparent agreement in aggregate statistics, per-sample analysis reveals substantial discrepancies: only 65% of Semgrep's and 61% of CodeQL's reports correctly matched the ground truth. These results question the reliability of static analysis tools as sole evaluators of code security and underscore the need for expert feedback. Building on this insight, we propose a conceptual framework that persistently stores human feedback in a dynamic retrieval-augmented generation pipeline, enabling LLMs to reuse past feedback for secure code generation and vulnerability detection.

title: Development of a Cost-Effective Multi-Parameter Patient Simulator for Enhanced Medical Education
authors: Tooba Khan, Ahsan Mirza, Naqi Chatta, Waheed Sultan, Amna Sheikh, M. Zeeshan Ul Haque
publish: 2026 Global Conference on Wireless and Optical Technologies (GCWOT)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases remain the leading global cause of mortality, necessitating enhanced medical training methodologies. This study presents the development and evaluation of a cost-effective, multi-parameter patient simulator designed to address the limited accessibility of simulation-based medical education (SBME) in resourceconstrained environments. The simulator incorporates three modular components: a CPR training module with forcefeedback mechanisms, an ECG simulation module generating physiologically accurate waveforms, and a heart sound reproduction system with mobile application integration. Using an Arduino-based microcontroller architecture, the system achieved 95% cost reduction compared to commercial alternatives while maintaining clinical fidelity. Evaluation with 45 healthcare professionals demonstrated significant positive outcomes across all performance metrics (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}<0.001$</tex>), with mean satisfaction scores ranging from 4.1 to 4.6 on a 5-point Likert scale. The real-time compression depth feedback was provided by the CPR module; the cardiac rhythms, both normal and pathological, are also successfully generated by the ECG module, and the heart sound module effectively reproduced the different normal and diseased heart sounds across five cardiac regions. This study demonstrates that high-quality medical training can be provided to medical students by developing lowcost human simulators.

title: Multiscale Characterization for Physical Analysis of Time-Dependent Dielectric Breakdown in Ultrathin Dielectrics of FinFETs
authors: Zuoyuan Dong, Xiaomei Li, Zixuan Sun, Zirui Wang, Lan Li, Peng Xu, Yu Yao, Runsheng Wang, Xing Wu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: The time-dependent dielectric breakdown (TDDB) is a critical reliability concern in modern FinFET technology. This work presents a comprehensive multiscale characterization of the dielectric breakdown process in FinFETs from microscale to nanoscale. By combining focused ion beam (FIB), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), and electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS), we directly visualize the structural and compositional evolution from soft to hard breakdown. Our results reveal the progressive degradation of the gate dielectric on a single fin, culminating in a breakdown path. EELS analysis further identifies significant chemical changes, such as oxygen vacancies, localized at the breakdown site. The multiscale characterization physical analysis methods provide complementary structural information. This offers insights into the microscopic mechanisms of dielectric failure in three-dimensional (3D) semiconductor devices.

title: Smart Process Optimization in Apparel Manufacturing Through Value Stream Mapping and Integrated Root Cause Analysis
authors: Md. Saiful Islam Seam, Shezan Ahmed, Md. Foysal Uddin, Nasif Morshed, Altaf Hussain Chowdhury
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: In the apparel manufacturing industry, reducing lead time and minimizing waste are crucial for maintaining competitiveness and ensuring an efficient workflow. However, operational inefficiencies, such as excessive work-in-process (WIP) and inadequate process flow, affect productivity and market responsiveness in Bangladesh's garment sector facilities. This study focuses on identifying key inefficiencies in production systems and guiding improvement initiatives to achieve reduced lead times and streamlined operations compared to traditional methods. To identify the root causes of these inefficiencies, Fishbone Diagram, Fault Tree Analysis and 5-Why techniques were implemented. A case study was conducted in a major garment facility in Bangladesh using detailed time-study and observational data. Then, a detailed current state VSM was developed and revealed a high level of lead time, high WIP levels, manual handling, lack of standardization and poor process flow. Based on these insights, a future state map was developed incorporating lean interventions, including workstation realignment, WIP reduction, standardized task sequences, operator cross-training and implementation of 5S methodology. These changes led to a 33.8% reduction in total lead time, a 39.8% improvement in processing time and significant decreases in unnecessary motion and waiting times. Ultimately, this study emphasizes the importance of identifying systemic inefficiencies and applying targeted interventions to gradually but significantly enhance productivity and sustain operational performance in the apparel manufacturing industry.

title: Economic Optimal Dispatch of Integrated Energy System Considering Renewable Energy Output Level and Energy Storage Configuration
authors: Lei Chen, Yongqiang Zhu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: The study develops an optimal dispatch model for an electricity-heat-gas integrated energy system considering renewable energy output level and energy storage configuration. With the objective of minimizing the total operating cost of the system, the model comprehensively accounts for energy procurement cost, operation and maintenance cost, and penalties for wind and photovoltaic curtailment, thereby establishing an economic optimal dispatch framework for the integrated energy system. Four scenarios are designed for comparative analysis, namely, no energy storage, only electric storage, only heat storage, and combined electric and heat storage. The results show that, as renewable energy output level increases, the role of energy storage in reducing system operating cost and curtailment rate becomes more pronounced. Electric storage performs better than heat storage in both cost reduction and the improvement of renewable energy accommodation. Further analysis indicates that the effectiveness of heat storage is strongly constrained by electricity-to-heat conversion capability, and its performance is closely related to the capacity configuration of the electric boiler. The findings of this study can provide a useful reference for energy storage configuration and optimal dispatch of integrated energy systems under high renewable energy output level.

title: Aging Characterization on Band Gap Reference Circuit
authors: Gumer Rodriguez-Martinez, Srijita Patra, Ricardo Ascazubi, Rahul Sharma, Shiv Shankar Gupta, Sachin L, Balkaran Gill
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: Band gap reference (BGR) circuits are crucial for maintaining a stable reference voltage under power supply and/or temperature fluctuation. However, limited studies are available on how aging will affect the performance of BGRs. In this study, we present an accelerated aging evaluation on a sub 1 V BGR circuit designed and fabricated in Intel 4 process technology, and validated on the Quality and Reliability On-Chip (QROC) platform. The BGR reference output voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{ref }}$</tex>) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 3 6 ~ m V}$</tex> was measured while sweeping the power supply voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{cc}}$</tex>) from 1 V to 1.4 V across temperatures from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex> to 120° C. The variation of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text{ref }}$</tex> was <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 6 \text{mV}$</tex>, which ensured the robustness of the BGR circuit. The accelerated aging stress conditions were <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\text{cc}}=1.45 \mathrm{V}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{T}=120^{\circ} \mathbf{C}$</tex> for 8 days, corresponding to an estimated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex>-year equivalent aging. After stress, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{V}_{\text{ref }}$</tex> degraded by approximately 1%, confirming BGR's robustness and precision, and highlighting the importance of aging evaluation for integrated-circuit (IC) performance and reliability.

title: Design and Experimental Validation of a Low-Temperature-Resistant Lithium-Ion Battery Pack Using Fiber Carbon-Nanotube Film Laminated Composite Heaters
authors: Jinyang Li, Jirong Huang, Zonghong Xie
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Electrical Technology and Automation Engineering (ETAE)
abstract: This study addresses the significant degradation in power performance of lithium-ion battery packs under low-temperature environments, which reduces the operational efficiency of electric devices such as electric aircraft and electric vehicles in cold conditions. A lithium-ion battery pack capable of stable operation in low-temperature environments was developed based on fiber carbon-nanotube film laminated composite heaters and a temperature control module. An experimental platform was established to conduct low-temperature heating experiments and constant-power discharge tests after heating for the power lithiumion battery pack. The results show that the designed battery pack achieved an average heating rate of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.98^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / {\min}$</tex>. After heating, the temperature difference within the battery pack was controlled within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>, while the energy consumption accounted for 5.16% of the total battery energy. Compared with the unheated discharge condition, the initial current during 1000 W constant-power discharge after heating decreased by 2.9 A, while the discharge capacity increased by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex>.

title: Origin of Pinched Hysteresis Loop: Unified Model for Wake-Up and Imprint in Sub-5 nm Ferroelectric HZO
authors: Zhenhong Liu, Zuocheng Cai, Mitsuru Takenaka, Shinichi Takagi, Kasidit Toprasertpong
publish: 2026 IEEE International Reliability Physics Symposium (IRPS)
abstract: This study systematically investigates the origin of the wake-up effect in ferroelectric <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Hf}_{0.5} \text{Zr}_{0.5} \mathrm{O}_{2}$</tex> (HZO) films, particularly found prominent in sub- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5\ n m}$</tex> thickness. We examine and verify a model explaining the pinched loops, wake-up, and imprint behavior in ultra-thin HZO: (1) a pinched loop in pristine samples results from imprint with opposite directions of the net-zero polarization state; (2) this imprint-induced pinched loop is stronger in thinner films due to more trap sites for charge trapping; (3) wake-up can be considered as the recovery of imprint. We experimentally confirm this model by reproducing the pristine condition through properly initializing films to a net-zero-polarization state. We can observe the re-splitting of switching peaks by imprint at the net-zero polarization state with the more prominent re-splitting behavior in thinner HZO films, reproducing the pinched loop in sub-5nm films.

title: Threat Modeling of Infineon AURIX for Automotive ECUs: A Systems Engineering Approach
authors: Narges Rahimi, Mitra Mirhassani
publish: 2026 IEEE International Systems Conference (SysCon)
abstract: The increasing connectivity of modern road vehicles has elevated the critical importance of cybersecurity within embedded automotive systems. Although much research has focused on vehicle-level cyber risks, limited attention has been given to micro-level threat exposure within embedded architectures such as Electronic Control Units (ECUs). ECUs constitute the computational backbone of vehicle control and communication networks, and their microcontroller platforms present highly attractive attack surfaces for adversaries. This study focuses on the Infineon AURIX TC3xx microcontroller family, a platform extensively deployed in safety-critical automotive domains that demand high reliability, security, and functional safety compliance. A structured threat modeling methodology is applied by integrating systems engineering principles with the STRIDE framework. The AURIX architecture is decomposed into process components, external entities, and data stores, followed by the construction of Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs) to represent internal connectivity and communication paths. Based on these relationships, threat scenarios are systematically generated, analyzed, and refined using the Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool (TMT), one of the commonly used tools for threat modeling. The resulting threat model establishes a foundational reference for identifying cybersecurity threats associated with AURIX-based ECUs. The outcomes support early-stage design decisions, facilitate securityaware system engineering, and contribute to the development of robust, standards-aligned threat analysis practices for embedded automotive systems.

title: Topology-Dependent Enhancement of Entanglement Extraction in Repeater Graph States
authors: Poramat Chianvichai, Poramet Pathumsoot, Naphan Benchasattabuse, Michal Hajdušek, Rodney Van Meter, Sujin Suwanna
publish: 2026 International Conference on Quantum Communications, Networking, and Computing (QCNC)
abstract: Quantum repeaters are essential for establishing long-distance quantum communication to overcome the exponential decay of entanglement due to photon loss. Traditional repeater architectures rely on physical quantum memory, which introduces decoherence and poses significant practical implementation challenges. The repeater graph state (RGS) architecture offers a promising memory-less alternative that is inherently resilient to photon losses. A key challenge in implementing RGS lies in the requirement for highly efficient graph state generators and complex qubit measurement. In this work, we aim to investigate the strategies for extracting the maximum number of Bell pairs from the RGS structure via its qubit connection to resolve the well-known bottleneck problem of RGS in which only a single Bell pair can be extracted from a complete bipartite graph state. From simulations, we observe that the maximum number of extracted Bell pairs depends on its connection topology, where the Bell-pair yield tends to be maximal at low to moderate edge densities. As the number of network hops increases, the RGS must be equipped with higher inner-qubit connectivity to maintain a sufficient yield of extractable Bell pairs. Thus, the expected resource requirement shifts toward the use of RGSs with higher innerqubit connectivity.

title: Frequency Compounding Cross Spectral Matrix Fitting for Passive Cavitation Imaging
authors: Célestine Lachambre, Bruno Gilles, Jean-Christophe Béra, François Varray, Barbara Nicolas, Adrian Basarab
publish: IEEE Transactions on Ultrasonics
abstract: High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) can induce cavitation, which requires monitoring for applications such as sonoporation, targeted drug delivery, and histotripsy. Passive acoustic mapping has been proposed as a cavitation monitoring method, but it suffers from limited axial resolution and coherent artifacts, particularly in non-inertial cavitation for the latter, leading to potential mislocalization of the cavitation cloud. Previous work introduced the Cross-Spectral Matrix Fitting (CMF), a regularized inverse problem approach, to improve axial resolution. Since coherence artifact positions vary with frequency, a common strategy is to mitigate them by summing maps from different frequencies. In this study, rather than summing frequency-specific maps, we propose Weighted Frequency Compounding-Cross-Spectral Matrix Fitting (WFC-CMF), which integrates multiple frequencies directly into the inverse problem and applies regularization to the reconstructed solution. To enhance robustness against outliers, we incorporate a Lorentzian M-estimator. Compared to Delay-and-Sum, WFC-CMF improves the Dice score by up to 35 points and outperforms summed-frequency CMF (sCMF) up to 14 points.

title: PLUM: A Prompt-Guided Lightweight Unified Model for Enhancement of Multidegraded Underwater Image
authors: Alik Pramanick, Akshay Daydar, Sonal Kumar, Arijit Sur, V. Vijaya Saradhi
publish: IEEE Journal of Oceanic Engineering
abstract: Degradation in underwater images is commonly caused by haze, low contrast, blur, and color distortion, which can hinder the effectiveness of many computer vision tasks. Most of the existing image enhancement works treat these degradations separately, thus limiting their applicability. To handle this problem, we propose a unified model to learn multiple degradations concurrently, improving resilience to practical conditions. First, we generate various degraded versions (haze, blur, low-contrast, and color distortion) of the input degraded image through domain translation. Next, original degraded and translated images are fed into a novel multidegraded enhancement network, which employs multiple encoders with an intermediate feature-swapping mechanism and a single decoder. Each encoder block enhances the network’s ability to characterize internal relationships in spatial and frequency domains by incorporating the fast Fourier transform. Intermediate feature swapping encourages the model to learn more robust representations. The decoder block contains the proposed prompt-guided spatial-frequency cross-attention block to encode degraded visual information effectively. Extensive experiments on several underwater image enhancement data sets demonstrate that our model outperforms the state-of-the-art methods with less computational cost (e.g., 1.20% structural similarity index boost on UIEB data set with 84.1% less parameter).

title: Large-scale and High-density Hydrogen Sensor Based on OFDR and Weak Fiber Bragg Grating Array
authors: Xing Liu, Qiujuan Li, Wanru Tang, Xinyue Yu, Weiming He, Yaxi Yan, Dexin Ba, Yongkang Dong
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: A large-scale and high-density hydrogen sensor is proposed by integrating a Pt/WO₃ coated identical weak fiber Bragg grating (WFBG) array with optical frequency domain reflectometry (OFDR). Pt/WO₃ generates local temperature change upon hydrogen exposure, inducing a spectral shift of WFBG with hydrogen concentration changes. Leveraging OFDR's high spatial resolution, it achieves simultaneous spatial localization and spectral demodulation of identical WFBGs without crosstalk, overcoming scalability limits of conventional hydrogen sensors. Experimental results show that the hydrogen sensing scale, density, sensitivity and response time are 5,000 points, 20 mm, 0.365 nm/% and 10 s, respectively. And the sensor has good repeatability, selectivity and in-situ temperature compensation capability. The proposed large-scale and high-density hydrogen sensor provides a scalable solution for leakage monitoring of hydrogen storage facilities and pipeline transportation.

title: CoV-ME: Memory Editing for Self-Consistent Reasoning in Large Language Models
authors: Mir Tahmid Hossain, Mahsa Sanaei Nourani, Nasrin Sultana
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Robotics, Automation, Artificial-Intelligence and Internet-of-Things (RAAICON)
abstract: Large Language Models often generate fluent but flawed reasoning chains, with undetected errors in logic, arithmetic, or assumptions. While methods like chain-of-thought prompting and self-refinement improve outputs, they do not correct internal representations. We propose CoV-ME (Chain of Verification with Memory Editing), a framework that enables LLMs to detect and correct flawed reasoning within their hidden states. CoV-ME combines a verifier that identifies inconsistencies in a reasoning chain with a differentiable memory editor that modifies intermediate transformer activations to reflect corrected beliefs. The editor is trained via self-supervised learning using synthetic reasoning errors, allowing it to reconstruct coherent hidden states during inference. We evaluate on GSM8K, StrategyQA, HotpotQA, and AQuA, showing that CoV-ME achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, outperforming strong baselines by +3.9 to +6.5 points. Gains are largest on StrategyQA (+6.3 over PAL), where reasoning steps are implicit and must be inferred, a key strength of our verification-guided editing mechanism. Ablation studies confirm the critical role of the memory editor, while visualizations show targeted corrections to erroneous steps. By changing hidden states based on consistency checks, we have presented solid evidence for self-correcting and more warranted reasoning.
